title: An in-depth exploration into the climate grief experiences of eleven women, using a life story approach
abstract:
Climate change poses several risks to our current way of life. In this context, this thesis argues that there is an urgent need to consider climate change and the impacts thereof within the personal and societal sphere. This thesis explores climate grief, which is inspired by ecological grief, a concept pioneered by Cunsolo & Ellis (2018). Due to the localised impacts of climate change, this thesis suggests that grief responses can extend beyond the ecological or environmental dimension.

Based on the need to innovate social research methods within the environmental sciences (Lertzman 2015), a novel adaptation of the Biographic Narrative Interpretative Method (BNIM) was designed to study climate grief experiences amongst eleven women in the United Kingdom. The data analysis was supported by Worden’s model (2001) on regular grief experiences as well as psychoanalytical approaches inspired by Hollway and Jefferson’s(2000) work. The findings illustrate that climate grief was dominated by three main emotions, including sadness & sense of loss, anger and fear. The emotional experiences, most clearly demonstrated through the expression of anger, were often enmeshed in social dynamics.

An evaluation of the method is also provided, demonstrating the advantages and limitations of using life story methods to study emotions and social phenomena. While BNIM can elicit rich data and is a valuable method in examining hidden emotions, it can be labour intensive, as it requires additional interpretative work. Although SQUINs may pose ethical risks for the participant and researcher, these can be managed by incorporating several practices which enhance psychological safety within and beyond the interview setting.

ANGLIA RUSKIN UNIVERSITY 
 
 
 
FACULTY OF SCIENCE AND ENGINEERING 
 
 
 
 
 
 
AN IN-DEPTH EXPLORATION INTO THE CLIMATE GRIEF EXPERIENCES OF 
ELEVEN WOMEN, USING A LIFE STORY APPROACH 
 
Maxine van Bommel 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
A thesis in partial fulfilment of the requirements of Anglia Ruskin University for the 
degree of a Doctor of Philosophy 
 
 
 
 
Submitted: December 2025 
 
Key words: Climate Grief, Ecological Grief, Biographic Narrative Interpretative Method, 
Identity, Climate Emotions 
 
i 
Acknowledgements 
 
 
I’d like to express my gratitude for having the opportunity to deliver a piece of research of this 
kind. While the PhD rested on my shoulders, the completion of it was based on the 
convergence of several things. It is therefore impossible to acknowledge everyone, but there 
are a few who stand out.  
 
First, I’d like to thank my parents for supporting me throughout this process. Even though we 
have lived apart and in different countries for so many years now, your support has been 
invaluable. I’d also like to thank my husband. You have been generous and patient in giving 
me the freedom to dedicate this time of my life to work on this project. 
 
In the end, there are only a handful of individuals who truly understand what this work has 
really been about, so I have an enormous amount of appreciation for my two supervisors, 
Professor Rosie Robison and Dr Melanie Rohse. This would not have been possible without 
your guidance and support. Finally, I’d like to thank the women who trustingly shared their life 
stories and emotions with me. I hope I have done them justice. 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
ii 
Abstract 
 
 
Climate change poses several risks to our current way of life. In this context, this thesis argues 
that there is an urgent need to consider climate change and the impacts thereof within the 
personal and societal sphere. This thesis explores climate grief, which is inspired by ecological 
grief, a concept pioneered by Cunsolo & Ellis (2018). Due to the localised impacts of climate 
change, this thesis suggests that grief responses can extend beyond the ecological or 
environmental dimension.  
 
Based on the need to innovate social research methods within the environmental sciences 
(Lertzman 2015), a novel adaptation of the Biographic Narrative Interpretative Method (BNIM) 
was designed to study climate grief experiences amongst eleven women in the United 
Kingdom. The data analysis was supported by Worden’s model (2001) on regular grief 
experiences as well as psychoanalytical approaches inspired by Hollway and Jefferson’s 
(2000) work. The findings illustrate that climate grief was dominated by three main emotions, 
including sadness & sense of loss, anger and fear. The emotional experiences, most clearly 
demonstrated through the expression of anger, were often enmeshed in social dynamics.  
 
An evaluation of the method is also provided, demonstrating the advantages and limitations 
of using life story methods to study emotions and social phenomena. While BNIM can elicit 
rich data and is a valuable method in examining hidden emotions, it can be labour intensive, 
as it requires additional interpretative work. Although SQUINs may pose ethical risks for the 
participant and researcher, these can be managed by incorporating several practices which 
enhance psychological safety within and beyond the interview setting.

1 
1. 
Introduction 
 
A recent representative survey of UK adults found that 80% were very or fairly concerned 
about climate change (DESNZ, 2024). This suggests that climate change is causing some 
difficult emotional responses within the UK population. However, despite this national concern, 
there remains a lag in propelling the societal change that is needed to mitigate climate change. 
This is also reflected in the slow pace of change within the UK government, with a recent 
report suggesting that adaptation is not being treated as a top priority (Climate Change 
Committee, 2025). This discrepancy between concern and a lack of action point to a gap in 
our understanding of the socioemotional barriers that may be inhibiting us from engaging with 
climate change.  
 
However, these issues are not just confined to the United Kingdom. Studies from around the 
world illustrate the myriads of ways in which communities are being psychologically affected 
by climate change (Ojala, et al., 2021). While some communities may be grappling with anger 
(Gregersen et al., 2023), others may be suffering from profound feelings of grief (Cunsolo, et 
al., 2020) or crippling anxiety (Pihkala, et al., 2021). In other cases, there may be a 
combination of all three (Zurba, et al., 2024). Yet there exists a complexity to our emotional 
experiences, which means that they can also be easily misinterpreted.   
 
As an example, emotional overwhelm can be disguised by denial and behind indifference may 
lie powerlessness and a deep sense of complicity. By clearly recognising and acknowledging 
our emotional responses to climate change, we can also learn to manage them more 
effectively. In doing so, we will also become much better equipped to deal with many of today’s 
complex social challenges such as climate change. Hence understanding how climate change 
impacts us on an emotional level is both pertinent on an individual, as well as national and 
global level.  
 
In the next section of this chapter (section 1.1), I will outline three key areas which are 
foundational to this thesis, leading up to a description of the research contributions (section 
1.2) and a brief outline of the thesis (section 1.3). 
  
 
 
 
2 
1.1. 
Areas of focus 
 
Loss within the context of climate grief  
How climate change impacts on the nontangible, more subjective parts of our lives, such as 
our emotional wellbeing can be framed through the domain of Loss and Damage. This domain 
recognises that as climate change transforms natural environments, this can lead to losses in 
biodiversity and ecosystems, which may also trigger losses within socio-ecological systems, 
impacting on more nontangible aspects of life, such as livelihoods, health and mental 
wellbeing, as well as dignity and identity. However, because these types of losses are more 
subjective and not easily quantifiable, they remain less well understood within policy as well 
as within academia (McNamara, Karen E. and Jackson, 2018). Yet better understanding 
nontangible losses is of particular importance as this can help to shape and influence how 
financial compensation for climate losses can be fairly allocated. While there are several 
different kinds of nontangible losses to consider, there is one specific kind of loss which stands 
out in the context of emotions, which is identity.   
 
According to the UNFCC (2024), climate change can lead to a loss of identity, where identity 
is often understood from a cultural or livelihoods perspective. Research suggests that failing 
to acknowledge or adequately replace identity losses can lead to diminishing self-worth, 
esteem and emotional distress (Borish, et al., 2021). In that sense, identity dynamics appear 
to play a part in helping to explain the complexity of emotional responses to climate change. 
The multiple ways in which identity intersects with climate change is presented in section 2.2 
of the literature review.  
 
Examining identity and emotions within the context of climate change can be approached from 
several different angles. One pathway is through the concept of climate grief - which is the 
focal point of this thesis. This concept is based on the term ecological grief, which was 
popularised through the work of Cunsolo & Ellis (2018). The need for a more expansive term 
for this research was due to my desire to look at complex emotional responses to climate 
change which extend to losses beyond the ecological domain. In the absence of a clear 
definition of climate grief, this thesis offers a working definition which is presented in Chapter 
2.  
 
It is worth mentioning here that while I set out to study both identity and climate grief, the 
notion of identity remained slippery. Hence, in the analysis I focused the study more closely 
on the climate grief experience itself. Through this work, however, a strong foundation has 
 
3 
been laid for further exploration, with ample work left to do in better understanding identity 
processes within the context of climate grief. I discuss several avenues for future research in 
section 6.1.3. 
 
Although Cunsolo & Ellis’s work (2018) has inspired several studies since it was published, 
that body of research appears to have predominantly focused on indigenous populations or 
rural farming communities whose livelihoods and identities are closely intertwined with the 
land. However, more than half of the world’s population currently live in urban or semi-urban 
areas, and this number is expected to rise sharply over the course of the next decade. Hence, 
there is a need to consider to the ways in which climate grief may be experienced in 
communities who may have a different kind of relationship with the natural environment. This 
will allow us to obtain a more nuanced understanding of the climate grief experience. 
 
Understanding women’s climate grief in the United Kingdom  
Obtaining a localised understanding of climate grief is important for several reasons. First, 
climate change will affect communities in different ways. While one geographic region may 
experience extreme flooding, another may experience prolonged periods of drought. Hence, 
each community will be faced with different kinds of environmental challenges, requiring 
different mitigation approaches. Yet these environmental impacts will also intermesh with the 
local economic, political and sociocultural context of a specific place. This also influences and 
shapes how individuals experience their emotions and how they emotionally respond to 
climate change.   
 
One place where climate grief has not yet been studied extensively is within the United 
Kingdom. While climate change impacts may not yet appear as severe as other countries, 
extreme weather is affecting the United Kingdom in several ways. According to the Climate 
Change Committee (2025), climate change in the United Kingdom has caused unprecedented 
wildfires and flooding, impacting on harvests, creating risks for infrastructure, food production, 
livelihoods and health. Despite these changes, there remains a lack of depth in understanding 
how climate change is affecting the emotional states of individuals within the United Kingdom. 
Obtaining a clearer, more localised understanding of climate grief would allow for more tailored 
climate change interventions and can pave the way for making them more meaningful and 
inclusive.  
 
Making interventions more inclusive also entails listening to the voices of women. To date 
however, women’s perspectives within climate change remain grossly underrepresented. Yet 
 
4 
for localised climate change mitigation interventions to be successful they must be relevant 
across genders. We know for example that women are disproportionately more vulnerable to 
the impacts of climate change (United Nations, 2022) and this can largely be explained 
through the uneven distribution of wealth within societies, as well as different gender norms 
and roles. For example, as primary care givers, women will often carry the additional burden 
of childcare and the household, and with less access to resources and decision-making power 
in society they may find themselves in more vulnerable positions. I discuss this in more detail 
in section 2.4. of my literature review.  
 
There is a risk that if climate engagement or mitigation programmes fail to reflect the reality of 
women’s experiences, they will not feel fully relevant for half of the population. Including 
women’s voices in climate research thus plays a critical role in societal transformation that is 
needed to adapt to climate change.  
 
The need for methodological innovations  
As this is the first study which explores women’s experiences of climate grief within the United 
Kingdom several research methods were considered. To study complex climate emotions, 
such as climate grief, considerable care was taken to craft the approaches and establish a 
safe space for emotional expression. Yet studying a novel concept also required 
methodological innovations.  
 
Findings ways to improve and innovate social science research methods within the 
environmental sciences has been advocated by Lertzman (2019). She argues that much of 
the work within the field of climate engagement has focused on attitudes, perceptions and 
motivations, with the assumption that removing individual barriers will encourage climate 
action. However, this type of thinking is outdated and fails to address the subconscious mental 
processes which drive human behaviour.  
 
Methodological approaches that acknowledge this problem can be found in the psychosocial 
tradition. From this perspective, human beings are understood as products of their own 
psychic realities but also a shared social world (Hollway and Jefferson, 2000). Within the 
context of climate change, this means that social and cultural factors influence how we engage 
with and respond to climate change, but there may also be several unconscious defences at 
play which may affect our interactions. This perspective inspired the methods designed to 
study climate grief experiences which I present in detail in chapter 3.  
 
 
5 
Within this research, Wengraf’s (2001) work has been particularly influential through his 
biographical life story method. The Biographic Narrative Interpretative Method or BNIM in 
short, falls within the wider category of ‘life story methods’, which is a qualitative research 
approach that explores individual life experiences through their life stories. To accommodate 
the needs of the research, BNIM was modified, and as such the adaptation of the method 
called for a critical examination of its effectiveness. The advantages and disadvantages of my 
method is presented in chapter 5. Critically evaluating our research methods remain important 
because they help to catalyse innovation.  
 
1.2. 
Research contributions 
 
Overall, this thesis contributes to academic research across four different areas which are 
summarized below.  
 
1. This study contributes to a more nuanced understanding of the non-economic losses 
of climate change within the domain of Loss and Damage. Studies on economic losses 
are overrepresented, compared to studies on the more subjective nontangible losses, 
such as identity. 
2. This study contributes to a localised understanding of the climate grief experience. To 
date, studies on ecological grief have primarily focused on indigenous of farming 
communities. It is therefore unknown how climate grief is experienced within an entirely 
different local context, such as in the United Kingdom.  
3. This study contributes to our understanding of women’s experiences in climate change. 
To date, women’s voices within the climate change are underrepresented. As identity 
processes and emotional experiences are governed by gendered social norms, it is 
important to include women’s perspectives.   
4. This study provides a methodological contribution to the application of life story 
methods. This is done through an evaluation of an adaption of the Biographic Narrative 
Interpretative Method designed specifically for this study.  
 
Although the findings from this research are centred exclusively on the climate grief 
experience (contributions 1, 2 and 3) and the evaluation of my BNIM adaptation (contribution 
4), this study has also laid the foundation for understanding several ways in which identity 
intersects with climate change. I discuss several avenues for future research in Chapter 6.  
 
 
 
6 
1.3. 
Structure of the thesis 
Following this introduction, this thesis consists of five chapters. Chapter 2 will provide an 
overview of the academic literature to help ground the research. Chapter 3 delves into the 
methods which I designed to study my research questions. My findings are then divided across 
two chapters. Chapter 4 will address my first research question on the climate grief 
experience, while chapter 5 addresses my second research question concerning my adaption 
of BNIM. The final chapter of this thesis, chapter 6, provides my overall conclusions and 
discussion of the results.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
7 
2. 
Literature Review: Climate emotions and identity 
 
The aim of this chapter is to present and discuss the current academic literature on climate 
emotions and identity, and to lay a foundation of how these two areas intersect. Considering 
the novelty of combining these two topics, I initially adopted a very broad and organic 
approach, drawing on contributions from several overlapping disciplines.  
 
Using the online library database of Anglia Ruskin University in conjunction with the database 
‘Web of Science’, I carried out several searches using search terms and then reviewing the 
reference lists of key articles. Various combinations of search terms were used, starting 
broadly with key words such as ‘emotions’, ‘climate change’, ‘identity’, ‘and then drilling down 
into specific emotions in relation to climate change such as ‘anxiety’, ‘depression’, ‘grief’ and 
‘ecological grief’. I also included several specific types of identities as I came across them, 
such as ‘self-identity’, ‘place-identity’ and ‘place attachment’.  
 
While this approach was useful, most of the literature was identified by reviewing the 
references of key articles, as well as the articles that referred to them, using Scopus. This 
technique proved especially useful as several studies were not always linked to the topic of 
identity. The foundational work of this review was carried out between March and September 
2020, and it was revised in March 2025 following the same principles.  
 
To help ground this literature review, the first section will start with an introduction to the domain 
of Loss and Damage. This area of work which influences global policy focuses on how losses 
due to climate change should be measured and reimbursed. However, there remains a gap in 
understanding the harder to quantify, social and psychological impacts of climate change, such 
as identity loss.   
 
This will be followed by a wider discussion on the intersection between climate change and 
identity. Although I will draw on contributions from multiple disciplines, the place-based 
literature appeared most promising in understanding identity processes within the context of 
climate change. In addition to presenting the work on place identity, I will also show how 
emotions could be a missing link between identity processes and pro environmental behaviour.  
 
Next, I will discuss several studies which show how sociocultural processes can shape identity 
and emotional responses to climate change. This will be followed by a presentation of studies 
from the domain of Psychology, which provide insights into different types of identities, beyond 
 
8 
place identity, which could be relevant within the context of climate change. Taking this a step 
further, I explore contributions from the psychoanalytical tradition, which provide a deeper 
understanding of emotional responses to climate change.  
 
This will be followed by a deep dive into ecological grief (Cunsolo and Ellis, 2018), which is 
the focus of this thesis. In their study, Cunsolo & Ellis (2018) suggest that ecological grief could 
be in part be caused by identity loss, which may occur due to changes in the natural 
environment. In my own work, I use the term “climate grief” to account for grief responses 
related to climate change that may include, but do not necessarily relate to changes in the 
natural environment.  
 
Since Cunsolo & Ellis published their study in 2018, two years before I started working on this 
thesis, the concept of ecological grief has inspired a flourishing body of work across multiple 
domains, making it a truly transdisciplinary concept. However, this literature review will 
demonstrate that several knowledge still gaps remain. This chapter will close with a 
presentation of studies highlighting women’s voices within the context of climate change and 
climate emotions, followed by my two proposed research questions and a summary of key 
contributions of this thesis.  
 
2.1. Non-economic losses and values-based approaches in the 
    domain of Loss and Damage 
 
In recent years, there has been a shift in thinking to recognise that losses associated with 
climate change extend beyond economic costs. This emerging climate policy domain, referred 
to as ‘Non-Economic Loss and Damage’ (NELD) is led by the Executive Committee of Warsaw 
International Mechanism for Loss and Damage (EXCOM) and falls under the United Nations 
Framework Convention on Climate Change (UNFCCC). The overarching concept of ‘Loss and 
Damage’ was officially developed in 2010 and was implemented into a framework following 
COP19 in 2013. The purpose of the Loss and Damage framework is to uncover how to address 
climate associated losses and impacts, with a specific focus on developing and vulnerable 
communities in the Global South.  
 
In 2022 a Loss and Damage Fund was established, and although some progress has been 
made around funding commitments, there remains some ambiguity around how Loss and 
Damage should be understood, measured and valued. According to Balzter et al., (2023) more 
evidence is needed to understand the impacts of climate change, and these should go beyond 
the geophysical and biological. In doing so they argue that the social sciences have a critical 
 
9 
role to play in providing insights into the psychosocial impacts of Loss and Damage (Balzter, 
et al., 2023). 
 
While there is some overlap between economic and non-economic losses, due to spillover 
effects (e.g. loss of land leading to a decline in agriculture and social wellbeing), the latter 
refers to items which are generally not traded in markets. Non-economic losses are in that 
sense understood as the more subjective, indirect, intangible, not easily quantifiable losses 
experienced by individuals and communities due to climate change. According to a technical 
paper from the UNFCCC (2013), non-economic losses may include: 
 
“Losses of or related to, among other things, life, health, displacement and 
human mobility, territory, biodiversity, ecosystem services, cultural heritage, 
indigenous/local knowledge and other social capital.” (UNFCCC, 2013 p. 12) 
 
Within the academic literature in the domain of Loss and Damage, only a small proportion 
focusses on non-economic losses. This was highlighted in a literature review from 2018, which 
showed that studies regarding non-economic losses, particularly around values and identity 
are missing. Hence, a closer investigation of what people and communities value and how 
they should engage with difficult emotions such as loss and grief was recommended as an 
area of future research (McNamara, Karen E. and Jackson, 2018). Since their work was 
published however, my reviews have found that the literature on non-economic losses in this 
domain continues to be lacking.  
 
As the Loss and Damage framework was originally developed to support the Global South to 
cope with irreparable climate losses, studies which do focus on non-economic losses within 
this framework tend to be focused on these regions (Nand, Bardsley and Suh, 2023; Van 
Schie, et al., 2024). Although countries in the Global South are arguably more in urgent need 
of attention due to a lack of resource, there is still a need to understand what climate losses 
mean for individuals and communities in the Global North as well. A deeper understanding of 
subjective losses, such as identities and cultural notions are still missing from public policy 
and adaption narratives, suggesting that more work is still needed. Better understanding these 
subjective losses is also important from the perspective that the Global South is reliant on the 
Global North to help mitigate the negative effects of climate change. Having frameworks on 
how to understand losses which can be universally applied will thus be useful. 
 
 
10 
Within the academic literature, climate losses within Loss and Damage are often understood 
through a values-based lens, aligned with the emergence of what has been called “a new 
science of loss” (Barnett, et al., 2016). From this perspective, a climate loss, such as an 
identity, whether associated with a particular place, culture or social group can be understood 
as an item of value, which can be at risk of loss due to climate change. According to Barnett 
et al. (2016) losses are experienced when individuals are “dispossessed” of things they value, 
which cannot be replaced. The science of loss scholarship asks what people value in their 
place-specific context, with the understanding that values are shaped by their culture and the 
context of their everyday life (Barnett, et al., 2016). 
 
According to O’Brien & Wolf (2010) a values-based approach to climate change is useful 
because it makes much more explicit what is important to individuals and cultures. How 
individuals and communities respond to climate change is not only dependent on how climate 
change will impact on them - but it will also raise questions around what is valued and worth 
saving amid catastrophic losses. In a recent paper, Van Schie et al. (2024) illustrated how 
adopting a values-based approach to study climate change losses allowed them to prioritize 
locally appropriate responses. In addition, they reported that such an approach could help to 
close the divide between economic and non-economic losses (Van Schie, et al., 2024), which 
are not as distinct in practice.  
 
For example, Nand et al. (2023) studied the impact of climate change on economic and non-
economic losses within the sugar cane industry on the Fiji Islands. The study highlighted how 
the current NELD categories (which included: individual, society and environment) could not 
fully capture the extent of Loss and Damage at the local level, particularly when it came to 
psychological and cultural losses. In addition, losses were also seen as highly subjective, 
making distinctions between economic and non-economic losses in practice quite challenging. 
For example, some individuals categorized their homes and animals as a non-economic loss 
due to its sentimental value, while from another perspective such items could be viewed simply 
as infrastructure or livestock (Nand, Bardsley and Suh, 2023). The boundaries between 
economic and non-economic are thus not always clear and can be highly subjective, putting 
into question the categories defined by the NELD.  
 
Nonetheless, several losses have already been reported around the world. Using a values-
based framework, Tschakert et al. (2019) analysed over 100 cases from the Global North and 
South to demonstrate the contextual nature of losses. The values at risk of loss included sense 
of place, social fabric, identity, dignity and self-determination and influence (Tschakert, P., et 
al., 2019). Case studies were also assessed based on country level income (e.g. low to high) 
 
11 
and found that climate harms and losses from high-income countries tended to focus on 
values, such as self-actualization, while in low-income countries, they tended to focus on 
losses related to basic needs and survival. Losses related to identity and dignity were not a 
focal point in cases from low-income countries, but they were overrepresented in cases from 
high-income countries. However, it is important to note that these findings do not imply that 
identity and dignity are irrelevant in populations from low-income countries. Rather this could 
be a reflection of the uneven nature of climate change impacts, regional contexts, as well as 
the specific groups within a society which were selected for study. Furthermore, it may also be 
an indication of an academic discipline which may influence a particular thematic direction.  
 
Although values-based approaches may help to understand what may be at risk of loss, it is 
important to note that values are unlikely to be static and could be traded off. In an earlier 
paper, Tschakert et al. (2017), suggests that what is valued is highly context specific and 
personal - and may not always be considered ‘rational’. What individuals will choose to hold 
onto and to let go of will likely entail trade-offs. That values within the context of climate change 
can be traded off was illustrated in a recent qualitative study. Taking place in Western Australia, 
Henrique et al. (2024) examined how individuals considered and prioritised their options to 
protect what they valued most when face with climate change impacts. Although emotional 
wellbeing and the land, plants and animals were highly valued by most, the results illustrated 
that not everyone’s priorities converged (Henrique, et al., 2024).  
 
Overall, the Loss and Damage domain is useful in grounding this research from a policy 
perspective. The values-based approaches which have arisen in this domain indicate that the 
lines between economic and non-economic losses remain blurred and subjective. This 
suggests that more intricate work is needed to better understand the nature of non-economic 
losses, such as identity.  
 
2.2. 
The intersection between identity and climate change  
 
This section explores the role of identity within the context of climate change. Here, the place-
based literature appears to be leading and is thus the focus of the first subsection. However, 
to obtain a broader understanding of the relationship between identity and climate change, 
several contributions from other fields, such as Psychology, Sociology, psychoanalytical 
traditions and the adaptation literature have also been included.  
 
 
12 
2.2.1. Place identity, pro-environmental behaviour and the role of emotions 
Within the literature on climate change and identity, it appears that the study of place identity 
dominates. However, several terminologies and operationalizations of place-identity have 
been developed, including place attachment, sense of place, rootedness, place dependence 
and place satisfaction. Peng et al.’s (2020) review of the literature differentiated between two 
main categories, which can be helpful for distinction purposes, namely: ‘people’s place identity’ 
and ‘place identity of a place’. While place identity of a place, essentially describes the identity 
of a specific geographic location and the meanings associated with it, assigned and 
constructed through discourse (Groote and Haartsen, 2008), people’s place identity describes 
an individual’s identification with a specific place.  
 
Within the context of this thesis, the notion of people’s pace identity is of relevance. This 
construct, originating in the field of Psychology is largely based on Proshansky’s (1978) work, 
which understands place identity as a component of an individual’s self-identity. Place identity 
is defined as “those dimensions of self that define the individual’s personal identity in relation 
to the physical environment by means of a complex pattern of conscious and unconscious 
ideas, feelings, values, goals, preferences, skills and behavioural tendencies relevant to a 
specific environment.” (Proshansky, 1978, p. 155).  
 
Within the literature, people’s place identity has been used to study individual behaviours 
which have a positive impact on the environment. Several overlapping terms have been used 
to categorize these types of actions such as: ‘climate adaptative’, ‘pro-environmental’ and 
‘environmentally responsible’. Climate adaptative responses are ascribed to specific kinds of 
behaviours which can have a positive effect on climate change, such as carbon offsetting 
(Whitmarsh and O'Neill, 2010). Pro-environmental behaviours include any behaviour which 
can have a positive impact on the environment, such as buying organic milk (Carfora, et al., 
2019). However, this may also sometimes be referred to more broadly as ‘environmentally 
responsible behaviour’ (Cheng, C. Wu and Huang, 2013).  
 
A few studies suggest that place identity could, in part, have an impact on pro-environmental 
behaviour. Many of these types of studies have been conducted amongst tourists using 
quantitative methods (Tonge, et al., 2015; Groshong, et al., 2019). In these cases, place 
identity is studied as a component of ‘place attachment’. For example, Cheng et al. (2013) 
illustrated how a tourist’s attachment to a specific destination resulted in a higher likelihood of 
pro environmental behaviour, while Groshong et al (2019) illustrated how national park visitors 
with stronger place attachment predicted the likelihood to volunteer at the park. Nonetheless, 
 
13 
such studies are lacking in depth to explain any further nuances or whether these results could 
extend to other settings or were influenced by other factors.  
 
More recently, Daryanto & Song (2021) carried out a meta-analysis to obtain a broader 
understanding of the association between place attachment and pro-environmental behaviour. 
The study incorporated additional factors including culture (individualist vs collective), as well 
as ‘place users’, i.e. whether the individuals were local or were simply visiting for the day. The 
study confirmed a positive relationship between place attachment and pro-environmental 
behaviour. However, this effect was stronger in collectivist cultures than individualist ones. 
This suggests that although place attachment is important, identification with culture and 
community also places a role. In that sense, members who are part of a collective culture, 
may prioritize those behaviours that are in service of, or benefit the community, rather than 
their own self interests. Overall, it highlights how different cultural contexts may influence the 
effect of place attachment on pro-environmental behaviours (Daryanto and Song, 2021). 
 
Although the literature previously discussed suggests that people’s place identity could play a 
role in pro- environmental behaviours, other identity constructs which are not directly related 
to place have also been studied. Several of these appear to share a similar theoretical 
framework regarding, the Theory of Planned Behaviour (Nigbur, Lyons and Uzzell, 2010; 
Whitmarsh and O'Neill, 2010) and a few different self-identity types have been used. Examples 
included self-identity constructs related to behaviour (e.g. recycler) (Nigbur, Lyons and Uzzell, 
2010), ‘higher order’ (e.g. being moral) (Gatersleben, et al., 2019), or more generic (e.g. pro-
environmental) (Whitmarsh and O'Neill, 2010). An overview of examples has been provided 
in figure 1.  
 
In terms of higher order self-identity constructs, Carfora et al. (2019) observed that individuals 
who self-identified as a higher-order identity of being ‘green consumers’, were more intent on 
purchasing ‘green’ products, such as organic milk. Similarly, Gatersleben et al. (2019) found 
that two higher order self-identity constructs, that of being frugal and moral, predicted pro-
environmental behaviour. Although these types of results appear promising, they are not 
always consistent. For example, Thorgersen (2018), examined how ‘being frugal’ or ‘being 
environmentally friendly’ could be associated with pro-environmental behaviour. While these 
two higher order self-identities were strongly linked to attitudes towards energy saving (e.g. 
making a point to use as little energy as possible), they did not translate to energy saving 
behaviour (e.g. switching off lights).  
 
 
 
 
14 
Figure 1: Identities associated with pro-environmental behaviours 
 
 
 
 
 
A lack of consistency amongst study results was also demonstrated by Whitmarsh and O’Neill 
(2010), which showed that a generic pro-environmental identity was not associated with all 
the different types of pro-environmental behaviours included in the study. For example, while 
a pro-environmental self-identity could predict the intent to reduce waste, regularly conserve 
domestic energy consumption and shop and eat in a more ecofriendly way - it did not predict 
intent for one off domestic energy conservation, travel or political behaviours.  
 
This suggests that there are limits to the influence of self-identity on encouraging pro 
environmental behaviour and other factors should be considered. For example, while Nigbur 
et al. (2010) showed that a self-identity of ‘being a recycler’ was predictive of recycling 
 
15 
behaviour, self-identity was comprised of several different social roles. This illustrated that 
although ‘being a recycler’ was a good predictor recycling behaviour, other factors, such as 
personal and social norms also played a role (Nigbur, Lyons and Uzzell, 2010). 
 
While the studies presented in this subsection are certainly not exhaustive, overall, what they 
collectively appear to illustrate is that self-identity constructs can be useful to help categorize 
different individuals, according to their disposition (e.g. I am frugal) or their behaviour (e.g. I 
am a recycler). However, self-identity constructs are insufficient in bridging the gap between 
intent and pro-environmental behaviour. Similarly, while the body of work around place 
attachment discussed in the previous section, does appear to be associated with pro- 
environmental behaviours to some extent, contextual factors remain important such as the 
influence of culture and social norms.  
 
A factor which appears to be missing from this discussion is the role that emotions play in 
influencing our behaviours. This perspective has also been demonstrated through a review of 
the climate emotions literature (Ojala, et al., 2021). One international study across 23 countries 
illustrated that individuals who worried more about climate change were more likely to support 
climate change policies (Bouman, et al., 2020).  This suggests that several interrelated factors, 
including identity and our emotional states could play a role in how we engage with climate 
change.   
 
Many of these studies examine the experiences of individuals who have been affected by 
climate change, and whose lives and livelihoods are closely intertwined with the natural 
environment, such as indigenous groups (Cunsolo Willox, et al., 2012) or farmers (Ellis and 
Albrecht, 2017). Several concepts have been used to study these types of place-based climate 
emotions, many of which appear to overlap, such as solastalgia (Albrecht, 2019), sense of 
place (Ellis and Albrecht, 2017), place attachment (O'Neill and Graham, 2016) as well as 
ecological grief (Cunsolo and Ellis, 2018).  
 
Within the context of climate change and place identity, one of the most prominent climate 
emotions is that of ‘solastalgia’. In his work, Albrecht (2019) illustrates how a person’s identity 
is tied to place, through feelings of belonging, and how environmental changes to that 
environment can disrupt or erode an individual’s identity, causing painful emotions. In his own 
words Albrecht (2019) describes solastalgia as: 
 
 
 
16 
“The pain or distress caused by the ongoing loss of solace and the sense of desolation 
connected to the present state of one’s home and territory. It is the existential and lived 
experience of negative environmental change, manifest as an attack on one’s sense of 
place. It is characteristically a chronic condition, tied to the gradual erosion of identity 
created by the sense of belonging to a particular loved place and a feeling of distress, 
or psychological desolation, about its unwanted transformation.” (Albrecht, 2019, p. 39) 
 
Since the concept was introduced solastalgia has been studied empirically in several different 
places and ways, although qualitative techniques dominate. McNamara & Westoby (2011) 
studied solastalgia amongst a small group of women living in a small island state, illustrating 
how a changing climate led to feelings of estrangement and uncertainty, diminishing their 
sense of identity which was closely tied to the natural environment (McNamara, K. E. and 
Westoby, 2011). Identity erosion was also observed amongst a rural farming community in 
Ghana who were forced to migrate to the slums due to drought. The study by Tschakert et al. 
(2013) reported that these feelings, however, were not only felt by those displaced, but also 
by those who were left behind. Communities experienced solastalgia as social networks 
crumbled alongside the natural environment, with many community members feeling 
homesick in ‘hollow homes’ (Tschakert, Petra, Tutu and Alcaro, 2013).  
 
While Tschakert et al. (2013) illustrated how solastalgia could be felt by those displaced and 
those who stayed behind, a more recent study (Leviston, et al., 2023) showed that direct 
experience is not needed to feel solastalgia. The concept was used to understand the 
psychological impact of bushfires on a community in Australia and illustrated how solastalgia 
could help explain the poor mental health outcomes in the aftermath of a bushfire. Comparing 
the results with another population who were not directly impacted by the bushfires, it was 
found that they also experienced solastalgia. These findings have implications for 
understanding place identity processes, suggesting that identity can extend beyond local and 
national place contexts.   
 
Besides solastalgia, other concepts have also been used. For example, O’Neill & Graham 
(2016) illustrated how climate change and adaptation affected ’place attachment’ in an 
Australian coastal community threatened by flooding. Here ‘place attachment’ was defined as 
a bond between people and places that is constantly evolving as it responds to both the social 
and physical landscape. In an Australian farming community, Ellis and Albrecht (2017) used a 
similar concept, ‘sense of place’, to describe the profound sense of emotional connection that 
farmers felt towards their farms, which formed an essential part of their own sense of self-
identity. In this case, the famers’ livelihoods and farms symbolized an extension of self which 
 
17 
meant that changes in weather conditions which impacted on their farms and livelihoods 
impacted on their sense of self and emotional wellbeing.  
 
In another study, several of these concepts were studied in unison. Cunsolo Willox et al. (2012) 
examined ‘sense of place’, ‘place attachment’ and ‘place identity’ amongst an Inuit community. 
In this community, climate change affected several interrelated aspects of ‘place’, broadly 
including changes to the physical environment, individual and collective sense of place 
attachment (as meaningful bonds to the environment), disruption to sociocultural practices tied 
to the land, changes in mental conceptions of place as well as place-based identities on an 
individual and collective level.  
 
Alongside changes to the physical environment, the study illustrated how this affected several 
interrelated aspects of place and how this impacted the individuals and the community on an 
emotional level. Community members experienced many different emotions, including 
sadness, frustration and fear. Remarkable were the accounts of participants who experienced 
a decreased sense of self-worth due to uncertainty about who they were and how to spend 
their time, when their regular activities, such as hunting, fishing and travelling became less 
available (Cunsolo Willox, et al., 2012).  
 
In a similar type of indigenous community in Northern Canada, ecological grief was the focus 
of study (Borish, et al., 2021). This concept popularized by Cunsolo & Ellis (2018) has been 
studied amongst other indigenous populations as well, such as the Sami in Finland (Markkula, 
et al., 2024). The term ecological grief has broadly been defined as “the grief felt in relation to 
experienced or anticipated ecological losses, including the loss of species, ecosystems and 
meaningful landscapes due to acute or chronic environmental change” (Cunsolo & Ellis, 2018). 
Although I discuss ecological grief in more detail in section 2.4, it would be difficult not to 
mention it in this context, as the concept overlaps with place identity by connecting people’s 
identity to the land and natural environment. Yet it also closely overlaps with social and cultural 
identities which are tied to people’s land centered livelihoods.  
 
Recent studies illustrate how the natural environment, including animals and associated 
activities such as the caribou in Northern Canada (Borish, et al., 2021) and salmon in reindeer 
herding groups in Finland (Markkula, et al., 2024) play an integral role in the emotional 
wellbeing, identity, as well as cultural continuity of the community. For example, in Finland, 
salmon and salmon fishing are not just a source of livelihood - they form an intimate part of 
childhood and are woven throughout people’s life stories. As such salmon is an integral part 
of the herding community’s collective and cultural identity (Markkula, et al., 2024). 
 
18 
All together, these types of studies illustrate how people’s identities can be closely intertwined 
with the natural environment either through their livelihoods or the emotional bonds they form 
with the land and animals. As a result of that connection, a change in the natural environment 
impacts on their sense of identity which can lead to emotional distress. However, the 
relationship between identity loss and environmental change is complex and can also be 
influenced by the cultural and sociohistorical processes specific to a particular place. 
 
2.2.2. Identities rooted in cultural and socio-historical processes 
While the previous section illustrated how climate change intersects with identities related to 
place, it is important to also consider how cultural and historical contexts influence emotional 
responses to climate change. From this perspective identities are socially constructed and 
influenced by individuals’ surroundings, circumstances and history. Altogether, this small body 
of work, with examples from Norway (Norgaard, 2011), Russia (Graybill, 2012) and Italy 
(Brugger, et al., 2012), show how cultural and historical contexts can also play a role in shaping 
identities and affects how communities respond to climate change.  
 
In her work in a small village in Norway, Norgaard (2011) illustrated how the cultural and 
historical context of Norway influences how individuals respond to climate change. As a 
country which greatly benefits from oil and gas extraction, in Norway, climate change is an 
uncomfortable topic resulting in the use of unconscious strategies to deal with climate change. 
The study found that, for Norwegians, information about global warming contradicted their 
identity and perception of themselves as being environmentally responsible, egalitarian and 
socially just, causing difficult emotions. As a way to protect themselves, a socially organized 
form of denial emerged (Norgaard, 2011).  
 
Protection through denial tactics is not an uncommon strategy and this can occur on an 
individual but also societal level. On a grander scale, denial can be particularly problematic in 
helping societies to face reality and adapt to climate change. Yet it is not uncommon. Socially 
organized denial was also found in a study amongst a small community in Northern Russia 
(Graybill, 2012). Graybill’s (2012) work illustrated that within a small community feeling the 
impacts of the climate change, villagers not only felt unable to adapt to their changing 
environment, but they were also highly influenced by the national discourse of climate 
skepticism dominating in Russian culture. Although sociocultural and historical processes are 
localised, these studies illustrate how they can influence emotional responses to climate 
change in different ways. 
 
 
19 
However, emotional responses may also be connected to a specific natural feature or 
landscape. For example, a study on glacial retreat in the Peruvian Andes, Italian Alps and US 
North Cascades showed how the loss of glaciers which held historical value threatened local 
identities (Brugger, et al., 2012). In the Italian case, the glaciers embodied and held memories 
of important battles from the First World War. Although the end of the Great War is still 
commemorated every year, it was feared that the loss of such culturally significant landmarks 
could diminish their collective memories over time.  
 
Overall, these studies illustrate that emotional responses to climate change are highly 
localised and are influenced by socio cultural and historical processes. From that perspective, 
it is worth discussing how climate change may impact on role identities in a different context.  
 
2.2.3. Negotiating role identities in the context of climate change 
While hunters in Arctic communities may be directly impacted by the receding sea ice (Cunsolo 
Willox, et al., 2012) and experience emotional distress, individuals with other types of identities 
can also be affected to climate change, but in very different ways. For example, individuals 
who practice social roles with high exposure to climate change, such as climate scientists 
(Head and Harada, 2017), sustainability managers (Wright, Nyberg and Grant, 2012) and 
climate activists (Coppola and Pihkala, 2023) also experience emotional distress. What these 
studies tell us though, is that within the context of climate change, attaching to certain identities 
can become a source of internal conflict, but they can also be navigated to alleviate emotional 
distress.  
 
Climate scientists and activists are frequently exposed to climate change and therefore are 
considered more vulnerable to climate emotions. While exposure to worrying information 
about climate change plays a part in how they respond to climate change, the complexity of 
the emotional experience is influenced by the other identities or roles they might practice 
alongside their role of scientist or activist. For example, a recent study amongst young climate 
activists showed how the social expectation associated with some of their identities impacted 
on their emotional experience. Several youths struggled with seemingly contradictory 
identities – e.g. being a climate activist as well as the child of a beef farmer (Coppola and 
Pihkala, 2023).    
 
However, in certain cases role identities can also be navigated to bridge contradictions and to 
alleviate emotional distress. For example, in their study, Wright et al. (2012) illustrated how 
sustainability specialists in large corporations enacted multiple unfixed roles or characters, 
 
20 
and how they shifted saliency between identities, depending on the context they found 
themselves in. This could either be at work, within wider society, but also in the home 
environment. For example, some said that they switched off ‘green identities’ at home to 
alleviate the stresses of the job (Wright, Nyberg and Grant, 2012). This was also observed in 
Head & Harada (2017) who found that scientists separated themselves from their emotions to 
uphold their identity and reputation of a scientist but also a means to protect their emotional 
wellbeing. However, Head & Harada (2017) suggest that the ways in which scientists 
distanced themselves from their emotions could be understood as a form of denial. Switching 
in between identities could thus be a way to alleviate internal conflict and emotions.  
 
Overall, these studies illustrate how specific role identities may increase exposure to climate 
change and cause internal identity conflicts. Although these can be managed to some extent, 
they nonetheless add complexity to how climate emotions are experienced. However, as 
identity conflicts were not always the main topic of study, more research is needed in this area. 
In addition, it is important to consider the emotional impact of climate change on individuals, 
who do not practice an occupational role with high exposure to climate change. This will in 
part be explored in the next section, which approaches emotional responses to climate change 
from a psychoanalytical perspective.  
 
2.2.4. Taking an identity lens, psychoanalytical studies suggest a relationship 
between identity threats and complex climate emotions.  
A body of work well-suited to explore in-depth identity issues within the context of climate 
change is research based in psychoanalytical traditions. Yet to date, most of these studies 
have focused on addressing the underlying roots of behaviour or inaction, by looking at anxiety 
(Weintrobe 2012), narcissism (Shaw and Bonnet, 2016), apathy (Lertzman 2016) and loss 
and grief (Randall 2009). Broadly speaking, these studies posit that climate change confronts 
individuals with difficult emotions from which they try to defend themselves, in part through 
unconscious processes. While the focus of these studies has been to unravel the emotional 
responses to climate change, upon closer examination, distress related to identity threats 
appears to be an underlying thread. From that perspective, psychoanalytical studies are useful 
in helping us to better understand how individuals respond to climate change.   
 
The work of psychoanalyst Sally Weintrobe (2013) is prominent in this field and in her work, 
she attributes climate related anxiety to identity loss. She suggests that in Western society, 
identities are bound to our lifestyles, and that our current way of living encourages self-
expression through material possessions. Losing them would therefore put at risk our sense 
of identity (Weintrobe, 2013). Such propositions have also been echoed by Randall (2009) 
 
21 
about adopting pro environmental lifestyles. Yet Weintrobe (2013) takes this a step further and 
suggests that humans are anxious about their destructive arrogant nature, and giving up their 
sense of entitlement to have whatever they want. “What we dread giving up is not so much 
particular material possessions or particular ways of life, but our way of seeing ourselves as 
special, and as entitled…”. (Weintrobe, 2013, p. 43). This self-obsessed focus, expressed 
through excessive materialism resonates with Shaw and Bonnet (2016), who suggest that 
narcissistic responses to climate change can be reframed as a response to grief, whereby 
inaction is caused by a refusal to take responsibility and an unwillingness to change damaging 
self-absorbed behaviours. From this perspective, self-absorption is perceived as a defense 
mechanism (Shaw and Bonnet, 2016). 
 
Guided by a similar paradigm to Weintrobe (2013), Lertzman’s (2015) study is based on the 
idea that fear and anxiety can result in defense mechanisms such as denial or disavowal which 
can be harmful and can lead to a state of unresolved mourning. Denial in the context of climate 
change consists of acknowledging that climate change is occurring, while also suppressing 
the difficult emotions to protect oneself. Disavowal is a form of denial whereby climate change 
is also acknowledged, but its relevance is minimized or avoided as a form of self-protection.  
 
Lertzman’s (2015) US-based study examines anxiety more closely in the context of 
environmental engagement. Yet, expecting to find narratives around anxiety, Lertzman (2015) 
instead found that loss dominated, and that this loss was a times associated with a loss of self 
and identity.  For one community member, she found that “To explore the loss of a wetland or 
a lake was also to explore the loss of potential associations and strong memories and how 
this constitutes an enduring sense of self and identity” (Lertzman, 2015, p. 77). Developing 
the concept of ‘environmental melancholia’, Lertzman (2015) argues that contrary to popular 
belief, melancholia is not solely associated with a loss of human relationships - but may also 
relate to losses in relation to identity.  
 
The process of splitting and the consequence of unresolved mourning is also a key part of 
Randall’s (2009), work on loss and grief. Splitting refers to process whereby individuals see 
themselves or others through polarized terms, as either all good or all bad. This is sometimes 
subconsciously used as a coping mechanism to deal with difficult emotions. In her work, 
Randall (2009) suggests that underneath public indifference and cynicism is a perception of 
threatened personal losses. Individuals may fear having to cope with losses associated with 
lower carbon lifestyles (e.g. less flying). Through her Carbon Conversation workshops, she 
demonstrates how personal adaptations to lower carbon lifestyles can be interpreted as 
threats to not only relationships and livelihoods, but also aspirations, status and identity. She 
 
22 
questions “Who am I if I no longer arrive at the school gates in a BMW and holiday with friends 
in Florida?” (Randall, 2009, p. 123). Like Weintrobe (2012), there is focus on identity loss in 
relation to materialism and consumption. 
 
In a more recent study, Randall and Hogget (2018) explore and compare the trajectories, 
emotional experiences and resilience of climate activists and scientists. Their work illustrates 
how specific social contexts, including organisations, may impact on an individuals’ ability to 
express and cope with climate change. For example, while most climate activists had found 
outlets to express and deal with the difficult truths they faced, some scientists appeared 
hampered by a culture of scientific rationality, similar to the experiences of scientists in Head 
& Harada’s (2017) research. Describing their coping mechanisms, scientists likened 
themselves to prey, while a sense of agency prevailed amongst activists. For some scientists, 
it appeared that the duality of two identities or states of being was difficult to comprehend and 
manage. For example, one scientist described the excitement of making a scientific discovery, 
albeit with detrimental environmental consequences. From an identity perspective, one could 
argue that with a scientist hat on, such a discovery would be hugely rewarding, but as a father 
or mother, such an outcome could be unsettling.  
 
Overall, when it comes to identities, psychoanalytical studies are particularly useful in helping 
to unpack complex climate emotions, and these studies presented above have illustrated this 
association. Perhaps it safe to say that examining defense mechanisms in their psychosocial 
form can serve as a critical shift, to help us better understand human behaviour when it comes 
to climate change.  
 
2.2.5. Adaptation, resilience and the significance of identity dynamics 
While the previous sections have clearly illustrated how several different layers of identity are 
interrelated with emotional responses to climate change, to close this segment of the chapter 
I want to briefly expand on why identity dynamics ultimately matter. Although the literature 
appears limited, a few studies suggest that not acknowledging identity processes can impede 
adaptation to climate change. Yet it is promising that the contributions touch upon several 
different layers of identity, including collective and social identities (Frank, Eakin and López-
Carr, 2011), self-identity (Randall, 2009; (Herrick, 2018), as well as occupational identities 
(Marshall, N. A., et al., 2012). Adopting a wider lens, Adger et al. (2013) suggest that how 
societies respond and adapt to climate change will depend in large part on how climate change 
threatens cultural dimensions, such as identity, sense of place and community cohesion. This 
interpretation further emphasizes the importance of this area of work and suggests that 
adaptation needs to be localised (Adger, et al., 2013). 
 
23 
A few studies which primarily focus on rural and indigenous communities have illustrated how 
a changing landscape can threaten cultural practices and social roles causing emotional 
responses (Borish, et al., 2021; Boafo and Yeboah, 2024). But how does this impact 
communities in the longer term and their ability to adapt? Other studies provide clues. From 
an adaptation perspective, Eakin et al. (2019) examined the impacts of climate change on 
villagers living on the periphery of Mexico City. This study illustrated how long-term erosion of 
place-identity diluted a sense of collective identity, damaging the social cohesion required for 
collective adaptation and action (Eakin, et al., 2019). These outcomes are aligned with an 
earlier study (Frank, Eakin and López-Carr, 2011) amongst Mexican coffee farmers, which 
suggests that social coffee farmers’ identities impacted on how they perceived and interpreted 
threats about climate change and in turn, their ability to adapt. As a coffee farmer, dependent 
on stable weather patterns, this greatly influenced how they interpreted climate change.  Both 
studies demonstrate that failing to address the importance of identity and culture can 
undermine a community’s resilience and ability to adapt to climate change.  
 
In another study Marshall, N.A. et al (2012) examined how place and occupational identities 
impacted on the capacity of peanut farmers to adapt by relocating to a more peanut farming 
climate friendly site. The study demonstrated how different identities can impact on willingness 
to adapt. Peanut farmers were highly attached to their place and occupation, but unlikely to 
move in order to continue growing peanuts but were willing to consider growing other crops if 
needed. A willingness to grow another crop - but not move suggests that the ‘peanut’ identity 
of the farmer is malleable, while place and overall occupational identity as a farmer were less 
so. Furthermore, those with higher levels of attachment to place or occupation were much 
more willing to undertake change. This may well demonstrate the importance of identity, as 
failing to adapt would threaten their occupation and place identity. Overall, the study highlights 
the complexity of identities, with some being more salient than others (Marshall, N. A., et al., 
2012). 
 
Crompton & Kasser (2009) called for a revised strategy of environmental organizations which 
focuses on ‘identity campaigning’. Alongside values, life goals and social identity, they 
addressed how individuals find ways to manage threats to their existence and identity with 
distressing emotions as a result. They argue that identity threats arise when individuals realize 
that fundamental changes to their lives are needed to avert climate change and threats to self-
esteem occur when climate change complicity is realized (Crompton and Kasser, 2009). 
 
More recently Herrick (2018), illustrated that people’s sense of place and self impacts on how 
they think about climate change, which can affect their ability and willingness to respond to 
 
24 
climate change. He refers to this as an ‘imaginative intangibility’ to adapt to climate change. 
He illustrates that progress can be blocked if policy interventions on climate change signify 
forgone opportunities, physical relocation, changes in occupations and professions, and ways 
of life which are key determinants of people’s sense of self. He bases his conclusions around 
a case study from coastal Louisiana, hypothesizing that Louisianian residents threatened by 
potential future flooding would struggle to relocate, instead feeling a duty to protect their 
homes and occupations which embody their values and are an extension of their self-
identities. He concludes that climate adaptation policies would likely receive a better response 
if threats to self and identity are better acknowledged (Herrick, 2018). 
 
Similarly, Randall (2009) recognises that the expectation of individuals to adopt more 
environmentally responsible lifestyles can sometimes be perceived as potential threats to 
identity and status, which have emotional significance. Her work draws on the results of 
Carbon Conversations, small UK support groups which aim to achieve personal carbon 
reductions. She suggests that although losses are often predicted as long-term effects of 
climate change, they are rarely presented as a consequence of climate mitigation (Randall 
2009). Aligned with the psychoanalytical literature, indifference to climate change is perhaps 
more about the threat of personal losses associated with adaptation. She suggests that a 
misalignment of private and public narratives and an under acknowledgment of personal 
losses will hinder change and adjustment (Randall, 2009). 
 
Overall, the previous couple of sections have demonstrated how identity intersects with 
climate change, with contributions from several different domains. As mentioned previously 
one of the concepts which has drawn a clear connection to identity is ecological grief. This will 
be discussed next.  
 
2.3. 
Ecological grief, climate grief and the gap in empirical 
research 
 
Although several studies have suggested that climate emotions and identity processes 
overlap, the work of Cunsolo & Ellis (2018) was most explicit in describing this association. 
Rooted in sociology, the term ecological grief has been defined as “the grief felt in relation to 
experienced or anticipated ecological losses, including the loss of species, ecosystems and 
meaningful landscapes due to acute or chronic environmental change” (Cunsolo & Ellis, 2018). 
There are three climate-related contexts in which ecological grief has been reported. 
Ecological grief has been associated with “physical ecological losses (land, ecosystems and 
 
25 
species), with anticipated future ecological losses, and with disruptions to environmental 
knowledge and loss of identity” (Cunsolo & Ellis, 2018, 276).  
 
Alongside ecological grief, the term ‘climate grief’ has also been used, but while ecological 
grief appears to be clearly defined within the literature, climate grief lacks conceptualization. 
This may in part be caused by a lack of studies which specifically focus on climate grief 
experiences. Only three studies were identified which use the term (see Olsen et al, 2025; 
Zurba et al., 2024; Dennis and Stock, 2024). In the absence of a clear definition, scholars 
appear to be leaning on other climate-related emotional experiences to help understand and 
define climate grief. The emotions identified include climate anxiety (Pihkala 2024), ecological 
grief (Olsen et al., 2025) and solastalgia (Zurba et al. 2024). While this is useful point of 
departure, scholars have remain somewhat limited in their attempts to clearly define climate 
grief.  
 
While Dennis and Stock’s (2024) paper includes the word ‘climate grief’ in the title, they merely 
suggest that climate grief was interchangeable with other terms such as eco-grief and 
ecological grief. They offer no clear definition of climate grief, and lean heavily on other defined 
forms of grief, such as ecological grief, anticipatory grief and disenfranchised grief (Dennis & 
Stock 2024). Olsen et al., (2025) offered a more nuanced approach. In their study, they defined 
climate grief as “a form of ecological grief tied to climate-related loss" (pg. 1). This definition 
points to a hierarchical relation to ecological grief, as well as the notion of loss.  
 
Out of the studies identified, Zurba et al. (2024) provided the most detailed definition of climate 
grief. Suggesting a relation with ecological grief and solastalgia, Zurba et al. (2024) defined 
climate grief as a “general term used to encapsulate negative emotions such as cognitive 
dissonance, sadness, and other forms of pain that people feel when experiencing loss 
associated with a changing climate.” (Zurba et al. 2024, pg. 2). Although Zurba et al. (2024) 
provide a more thorough definition, with examples of emotions (e.g. sadness), the negative 
framing of these emotions remains problematic and may fail to address the full spectrum of 
emotions which may potentially be experienced.  
 
What is useful about Zurba et al.’s (2024) definition is that it includes the experience of loss 
as a key characteristic. The inclusion of loss in the definition of climate grief is borrowed from 
the concept of ecological grief, and this was also suggested by others, such as Olsen et al. 
(2025) and Gorman and Lewis (2025). In their paper, Gorman and Lewis (2025) describe 
climate grief as a feeling of “general malaise” (pg. 358) which can be associated with multiple 
climate change-related losses that may be highly individualized. This suggests that there is 
 
26 
some overlap between the concept of ecological grief and climate grief. Although both related 
to loss, the losses associated with climate grief, as opposed to ecological grief may be more 
expansive.  
 
Despite the lack of definition of climate grief, the approaches identified within these papers 
collectively contribute to the development a working definition of climate grief, which I propose 
as follows:  
 
Climate grief can be understood as a highly contextual, complex socio-
emotional phenomenon commonly associated with losses directly or 
indirectly related to climate change, some of which may be deeply personal.  
 
As a starting point, a more generalized conceptualization of grief experiences related to 
climate change remains important to account for losses associated with climate change which 
may not be directly related to ecological change. However, due to the overlap with ecological 
grief, it is important to consider studies on ecological grief more in depth in the following 
paragraphs.  
 
Since the concept of ecological grief was popularized in 2018, a significant transdisciplinary 
body of work has been developed. Although most contributions fall within the field of 
Psychology (Freeling and Preston, 2019) and Sociology (Arotoma-Rojas, et al., 2022), the 
spread of articles from the Arts (Brewster, 2020), Health (Noy, et al., 2022), Religion and 
Philosophy (Read, 2023) demonstrates how the concept has inspired several different schools 
of thought. Yet despite a flourishing body of work, there remains several gaps in knowledge to 
address.  
 
In 2018, Cunsolo & Ellis noted that the conceptual foundations of ecological grief were 
underdeveloped and questioned to what extent the experience of ecological grief would follow 
a similar trajectory to regular grief experiences. Cunsolo & Ellis (2018) reference the work of 
Kubler-Ross (1970), who developed a framework of regular grief experiences, suggesting that 
grief experiences typically consist of 5 stages of emotions, including denial, anger, bargaining, 
depression and acceptance (Kubler-Ross 1970). As one of the best-known models of grief, 
Kubler-Ross’s work has been highly influential despite its shortcomings. Indeed, the linearity 
of this model is problematic: it assumes that individuals go through specific phases of emotions 
in a particular order. In addition, it fails to capture the full spectrum of emotions which may be 
associated with climate grief.  
 
27 
 
Other models of interest include the work of Worden (2001). This is a more complex model of 
grief, based on Bowlby’s (1977) attachment theory. This theory is based on the premise that 
human beings naturally form strong bonds with others. These are developed in infancy and 
early life out of a need for security and safety. When these bonds are broken or threatened, 
they arouse a strong emotional reaction. Worden’s (2001) model proposes a series of tasks 
of mourning that can be worked through to process grief, with the assumption that the work is 
on-going and never complete. Using this model to understand climate related losses was 
initially proposed by Randall (2009), who illustrated how Worden’s model (2001) could be used 
to support individuals work through climate-related losses through her Carbon Conversations 
workshops.  
 
What drew me to Worden’s (2001) work was that it offered deep insights into the various 
manifestations of grief. His book ‘Grief Counselling and Grief Therapy’ (2001) provided 
detailed explanations around the different emotions which could be experienced as part of the 
grief experience, including sadness, anger, anxiety and helplessness. However, what made 
his work most appealing was the inclusion of other categories of manifestations which 
extended beyond the emotional sphere.  
 
Pointing to the embodied nature of emotions, this includes physical sensations, such as a 
sense of depersonalization, or a feeling of hollowness in the stomach. Worden (2001) also 
offers an overview of the different cognitions and behaviors which can accompany the grief 
experience. These can include amongst many others - social withdrawal, sleep disturbances 
or treasuring specific objects which served as reminders of the deceased. Furthermore, he 
suggests that the grieving processes can be mediated by several factors, including social 
variables (e.g. the availability of support) and the nature of the loss experienced (e.g. multiple 
losses) - all of which may potentially be relevant within the case of climate grief. Cunsolo & 
Ellis (2018) refer to the notion of cascading losses which I touch upon in later paragraphs.  
 
Overall, Worden’s (2001) work provides an encompassing perspective of the grief experience. 
He acknowledges that while the experience of grief can be highly individualized, grief and the 
process of mourning remain a social process. Yet to date, this model remains underutilized in 
understanding grief experiences related to climate change. Most recently, Pihkala (2025) 
suggested that the Dual Process Model of Coping with Bereavement, which is based on 
Worden’s tasks of mourning could be used to support individuals with ecological grief. The 
model appears promising in that it addresses not only the losses experiences, but also 
 
28 
restorative practices which can support coping. However, to date empirical evidence is still 
lacking on how these types of models can be applied to understand climate grief experiences.  
 
In addition to this gap, Cunsolo & Ellis (2018) also suggested that more work was needed to 
tease out the key drivers of ecological grief, one of which was identity. Since then, several 
studies which examine identity within the context of ecological grief have been published. 
Many of these studies were carried out amongst indigenous or rural farming communities, 
such as Ghana (Boafo and Yeboah, 2024), Finland (Markkula, et al., 2024), Kenya (Beyeler, 
et al., 2023), and Canada (Borish, et al., 2021). Although the geographical and cultural 
contexts were entirely different, collectively, the research findings illustrate how identity 
intersects with ecological grief largely in relation to the loss of livelihoods and cultural practices 
which form an integral part of an individual’s social and cultural identity.  
 
Yet ecological grief is layered and complex as it relates to loss. This has been highlighted by 
Cunsolo et al. (2020) who demonstrated that a singular ecological loss could lead to cascading 
forms of ecological grief, whereby the disturbance and disruption of all interconnected pieces 
can be related to just one loss. In that sense identity loss caused by ecological change, can 
trigger other unpredictable losses, such as loss of self-worth, purpose or direction.  
 
For example, a qualitative case study by Boafo and Yeboah (2024) amongst a farming 
community in Ghana found that the loss of stable weather patterns led to extreme drought and 
flooding which damaged agriculture through crop failure and pest infestation. As a result, local 
livelihoods became insecure, leading to a loss of income, as well as daily routines and farm-
related social activities, impacting on their identities. The research further highlighted how 
such losses led to the erosion of significant cultural traditions, as well as feelings of masculinity 
amongst male farmers who were no longer able to provide for their communities (Boafo and 
Yeboah, 2024).  
 
Similar patterns of loss have been identified in other communities on different continents. For 
example, a study amongst the Inuit population in Canada illustrated how the loss of land and 
Caribou due to changing weather patterns and receding sea ice led to identity loss amongst 
community members affecting their sense of self and self-worth (Cunsolo, et al., 2020). An 
older study from Australia by Rigby et al. (2011), showed how prolonged drought threatened 
traditional Aboriginal men’s roles. The extreme weather led to a loss of natural habitats and 
wildlife which affected the men’s ability to sustain their livelihoods and place within the 
community. The prolonged disruption to their way of life led to antisocial behaviour, including 
 
29 
increased alcohol, aggression, violence and suicide. The impacts reverberated throughout the 
Aboriginal community, bringing with it a culture of shame and mistrust.  
 
Collectively these studies illustrate how changes in the natural environment can lead to several 
cascading losses within communities. The losses may be unpredictable and can have 
negative consequences not just on the individual, but they can also damage the social fabric 
of an entire community. Some scholars (Kieft and Bendell, 2021) suggest that if losses are too 
severe, they may cause whole societies to collapse.  
 
To date, most studies on ecological grief have been studied amongst indigenous or rural 
populations whose livelihoods, traditions and cultures are more overtly intertwined and
