dependent on the natural environment. While these types of studies provide one illustration of 
ecological grief, Ellis & Albrecht (2018) highlight the need to extend the investigation of mental 
health impacts of climate change beyond indigenous and farming communities.  
 
So far, however, studies on ecological grief particularly from non-indigenous communities in 
the Global North are sparse, particularly from the United Kingdom. More recent studies have 
taken place in the USA (Dennis and Stock, 2024), Finland (Coppola and Pihkala, 2023), 
Canada (Zurba, et al., 2024) and Australia (Noy, et al., 2022). However, I observed that not all 
these studies were exclusively framed around the concept of ecological grief, and only two 
studies explicitly examined identity dynamics (Marshall, Nadine, et al., 2019; Coppola and 
Pihkala, 2023).  Furthermore, most of these studies were carried out with individuals who were 
directly and frequently exposed to climate change information through their roles (e.g. 
environmental activists).  
 
In their work Dennis & Stock (2024) illustrated how elderly US activists experienced feelings 
of grief alongside anger, sadness and pain. Losses were related to the destruction of natural 
habitats such as prairies and wetlands, some of which were held by indigenous tribes. Another 
study (Zurba, et al., 2024) amongst environmental activist youths reported similar kinds of 
emotions related to climate change. Feelings of sadness, anger and frustration alongside 
uncertainty and hopelessness were common. Using photovoice methods, this Canadian study 
illustrated how these emotions were often expressed in relation to several aspects of daily life, 
including school, family and social settings.  
 
The two studies suggest how engaging with every-day life can create difficult emotional 
experiences for those who are actively engaged in climate change. The importance of the 
social environment in helping to manage difficult emotions has also been acknowledged by a 
 
30 
study amongst environmental professionals in Australia (Noy, et al., 2022). Using qualitative 
approaches, Noy et al. (2022) illustrated how ecological grief alongside loss and anxiety was 
experienced by working professionals who were directly exposed to climate change through 
their work. The study revealed that a lack of organizational support within the workplace and 
within personal social networks exacerbated feelings of loneliness and isolation (Noy, et al., 
2022). 
 
Collectively, this body of work which represents the experiences from both indigenous and 
non-indigenous communities suggest that ecological grief could be shared experience. The 
different ways in which ecological grief is experienced however, points to the influence of 
sociocultural structures which differ according to each context. Yet the research on ecological 
grief also shared another attribute in that most of the studies were carried out using similar 
kinds of methods.   
 
From my review it appears that most of the studies which explored experiences of grief used 
qualitative methods, apart from Marshall et al. (2019) who used a quantitative research design. 
With regards to qualitative methods, some studies opted for a singular method such as a depth 
interview (Markkula et al., 2024), while others applied a combination of interviews and focus 
groups (Boafo & Yeboah, 2024; Noy et al., 2022). What was noteworthy of some of studies 
that took place amongst indigenous communities was the close involvement of the local 
community.  
 
For example, Arotoma-Rojas et al., (2022) consulted with a local community assembly before 
carrying out interviews, focus groups and participant observation in an indigenous community 
in Peru. While such practices are important in obtaining consent and community support for 
the research, Borish et al. (2021) took this approach further by creating a steering committee 
which consisted of several members of the local community. This committee was involved in 
multiple stages of the research and assisted in the development of the research questions. 
The study also applied innovative videography and documentary film approaches to capture 
and analyse the data, as well as share the results of the research.  
 
While conventional research methods, such as interviews and focus groups remain valuable 
tools to study complex emotions such as ecological grief, the creative and community inclusive 
approaches applied by Borish et al. (2021) are aligned with Lertzman’s (2015) call for the 
development of more innovative research methods within climate change research. In her own 
work, she applied the Biographic Narrative Interpretative Method (BNIM) (Wengraf, 2001) to 
study emotional responses to climate change. I discussed her work more in depth in section 
 
31 
2.2.4 of this chapter. Although her research was published over a decade ago, it appears that 
biographical or life story methods, whether BNIM or otherwise, have not yet widely been 
applied within the context of climate change research.    
  
While exploring the methods which have been used to study climate emotions in general, only 
a handful of studies used biographical life story methods. For example, Hamilton (2022) 
applied BNIM to study how emotional reflexivity was achieved and managed by participants 
engaged in climate change support programmes. In another example, Manuel-Navarett & 
Pelling (2015) used life story interviews to study tourism entrepreneur’s and workers 
experiences of hurricanes and tourism in a coastal town in Mexico. Although not classified as 
BNIM, their work illustrates the wider variety of life story methods available which can be 
applied within climate change research. For example, Cairns & Pihkala (2024), applied a life 
history interview method to explore how Buddhism influenced how activists experienced and 
coped with climate emotions. Although the study discussed several climate emotions, 
including anxiety and anger, ecological grief was of particular interest. Here, they illustrated 
how several participants used Buddhist meditative practices to acknowledge and cope with 
their feelings of grief (Cairns & Pihkala, 2024). 
  
Overall, my review of the literature has identified that while some studies have used life story 
approaches such as BNIM to study climate change related subject matter, BNIM has not yet 
been applied to study ecological grief. While Comtesse et al. (2021), specifically advocate for 
applying more quantitative approaches to study ecological grief, I propose that it may be 
worthwhile to explore grief experiences using a qualitative biographical life story method such 
as BNIM. I will discuss the advantages of using BNIM in more detail in Chapter 3 where I 
describe the rationale behind my methods.  
 
While this section on ecological grief has clearly laid out several gaps in knowledge pertaining 
to study population and methods, the final section of this literature review will discuss the value 
of making climate change research more representative by including more women’s 
perspectives which are currently lacking in climate change research.  
 
2.4. 
Lack of women’s voices in climate change research  
 
How we experience the world around us and our emotions is in part determined by the social 
structures and norms in which we are embedded. From that perspective, the gender identity 
we hold influences our experiences, and how we respond to different circumstances. It is 
 
32 
therefore important to consider how climate change may impact differently on different 
genders. 
 
Although there is a lack of gender specific1 studies within the climate emotions literature, a 
body of work which explores the impacts of climate related weather events demonstrates how 
climate change impacts differently on women compared to men. These include studies on 
acute and long-term events such as flooding (Tunstall, et al., 2006), cyclones (Ayeb-Karlsson, 
2020) and drought (Hanigan, Schirmer and Niyonsenga, 2018). Studies from the Global North 
and Global South collectively show how climate related environmental events impact on the 
psychological and emotional wellbeing of men and women, which is often influenced by local 
sociocultural norms.  
 
2.4.1. Women’s experiences in the Global South 
To date, a growing number of studies, particularly from the Global South illustrate how extreme 
weather events caused by climate change, such as flooding in Ghana (Adams and Nyantakyi-
Frimpong, 2021), cyclones in Bangladesh (Ayeb-Karlsson, 2020) and drought in Uganda 
(Bryson, et al., 2024) can lead to emotional distress. Yet these types of events affected men 
and women in different ways and this was often influenced by the prescribed social roles and 
responsibilities of each gender.  
 
For example, Perkins et al. (2017) illustrated how food insecurity was related to depression in 
both men and women in rural Uganda. However, they found that from a social network 
perspective, food insecurity had a much more profound impact on the severity of psychological 
distress in men than in women. The study showed that women experienced less shame and 
stigma than men, because the inability for men to provide food for their families affected their 
social status and challenged their value (Perkins, et al., 2017). However, other studies show 
that in times of water scarcity, women may be more emotionally affected. This is because, in 
some areas procuring water for the home falls under the responsibility of women (Mushavi, et 
al., 2020). Both examples illustrate not only how climate change impacts men and women 
differently, but also that the type of environmental pressures matter.  
 
 
1 It is worth highlighting that alongside a gap in gender specific studies (particularly women), there also appears a lack of 
gender nonbinary studies within the context of climate change. This is problematic as a more recent study by Aylward et al. 
(2025) on the impact of climate change on young people in Canada, showed that gender nonbinary participants experienced 
a higher severity of climate emotions, such as hopelessness and worry. Overall, this calls for more inclusive and nuanced 
research on climate change.  
 
33 
Although all genders will be impacted by climate change, some studies show how women may 
be more vulnerable than men. This may be caused by the way in which climate could increase 
existing inequalities between genders. An example includes a study by Ayeb-Karlsson (2020), 
which looked at how Bangladeshi villagers responded to cyclones and how these were 
influenced by existing power structures and social norms. While men, perceived as ‘protectors 
of the family’ struggled with their failings to shield their families from the cyclone, women’s 
vulnerabilities vastly increased. Due to a lack personal resources, women were more likely to 
end up in shelters where many suffered from sexual abuse, leading to loss of honour which 
sometimes led to suicide (Ayeb-Karlsson, 2020). These studies located in the Global South 
suggest that when women have less access to resources and are financially dependent on 
men their vulnerability increases - which during times of prolonged instability caused by 
environmental disasters can be exacerbated.  
 
Yet these studies located in the Global South suggest that women may also be more 
vulnerable due to their child caring responsibilities. A recent study from Bryson et al. (2024) 
showed how bearing and rearing children increased women’s vulnerabilities during extreme 
weather in Uganda. Their responsibility to care for born and unborn children increased 
emotional distress in times when access to food supplies were diminished due to ongoing 
drought. Anxieties about future famines, as well not being able to access specific types of food 
from indigenous lands further impacted on women’s emotional wellbeing (Bryson, et al., 2024). 
Similar experiences were observed in a study from Ghana, which showed that flooding events 
increased the vulnerabilities of women because of their family caring responsibilities (Adams 
and Nyantakyi-Frimpong, 2021).  
 
Collectively these studies illustrated how climate change, and the type of environmental 
changes it causes, impacts on men and women differently.  Although all genders are affected, 
these studies in the Global South suggest that women are often more vulnerable due to 
existing power inequalities as well their responsibilities to care for their families. Similar, but 
somewhat contradictory results have been found in studies in the Global North, which will be 
discussed next.  
 
2.4.2. Women’s experiences in the Global North  
Although the Global South may be more vulnerable to climate change impacts due to less 
resources, communities from the Global North are also being affected by climate related 
environmental changes and natural disasters. As communities will be impacted in different 
ways, it is important to consider how experiences from women living in the Global North may 
differ or overlap with women in the Global South. Several studies located in the the Global 
 
34 
North have shown how flooding events (Tunstall, et al., 2006), bushfires (Bryant, et al., 2014) 
and hurricanes (Mills, Edmondson and Park, 2007) negatively impact on psychological 
wellbeing of those affected. Yet, it appeared that men and women did not always have the 
same experiences.  
 
A series of studies were carried out regarding the impact of flooding events in a region in the 
United Kingdom. The first of these studies in 2006 across 30 locations in England Wales 
showed how after the floods receded, community members reported feeling anxious, stressed 
and suffered from sleeping problems and flashbacks (Tunstall, et al., 2006). However, later 
studies showed that the psychological impacts were more frequently experienced by women. 
This included conditions such as PTSD, depression as well as anxiety (Mason, Andrews and 
Upton, 2010; Paranjothy, et al., 2011). Similar to women’s experiences in the Global South, 
the increased frequency of psychological distress was attributed to social norms. In a study 
located in the Global North, Tunstall et al. (2006) suggested that greater emotional investment 
in the home and the responsibility of childcare and the elderly made flooding events more 
difficult for the women.   
 
However, socioeconomic factors also matter. These often reflect the inequalities which persist 
between genders within society. A study from Germany showed how compared to men, 
women were not only more emotionally affected by floods - but they also took longer to recover 
their day-to-day life (Bubeck and Thieken, 2018). Bubeck and Thieken (2018) also attributed 
these outcomes to the fact that women were more likely to be responsible for the household, 
which meant that they were more frequently exposed to effects of the flood on their home. 
Men, on the other more easily resumed their day-to-day work, as they tended to work outside 
of the home environment which had not been affected by the floods. Collectively, these types 
of studies suggest that perhaps social inequality and gendered roles and norms can increase 
vulnerabilities for women on both sides of the global divide.  
 
However, in exploring the literature further, not all studies located in the Global North showed 
that women were more likely to be psychologically affected than men after a climate-related 
event. For example, women were not more likely than men to suffer from depression after the 
Australian Black Saturday bushfires (Bryant, et al., 2014) and women were also not more likely 
than men to suffer from PTSD or depression after hurricane Sandy (Lowe, et al., 2015). Other 
contradictory findings have also been found in studies related to drought. For example, studies 
which looked at the psychological impact of drought in rural Australia found no relationship 
between drought and emotional wellbeing in women at all (Powers, et al., 2012) or specifically 
in older women  (Powers, et al., 2015). While another study a few years later did demonstrate 
 
35 
that older women experienced distress after drought – they were less likely to do so than 
women from a younger cohort (Hanigan, Schirmer and Niyonsenga, 2018).  
 
While these types of quantitative studies generally do not aim to explore in depth the 
experiences of the participants, collectively, the contradictory results may indicate that the 
impacts of climate change could be variable. Several factors may not have been considered, 
such as the level of community support provided, disaster preparedness, or perhaps the 
strength of social ties in the community. In addition, none of these studies focused exclusively 
on the experiences of women, and thus there remains a gap in understanding how climate 
change affects women and how their gender identity plays a role.  
 
Apart from these studies above, there appears to be a lack of studies which specifically 
examine how climate change impacts on the emotional wellbeing of women from the Global 
North. This gap became quite apparent when considering the existence of a significant body 
of work around male farmer suicide in drought-stricken areas of Australia (Alston and Kent, 
2008; Alston, 2012; Kunde, et al., 2018; Perceval, et al., 2018). Also known as ‘The Big Dry’, 
the ongoing drought, in Australia since the 2000s has been associated with an increasing 
number of suicides amongst men in rural communities (Ivan C. Hanigan, et al., 2012). While 
some quantitative studies explored factors such as remoteness (Brew, et al., 2016), or existing 
mental health problems and personalities (Judd, et al., 2006), a growing body of qualitative 
work demonstrates how sociocultural normative structures of masculine and rural identities 
(Alston and Kent, 2008) (Kunde, et al., 2018) can contribute to deteriorating emotional 
wellbeing and even suicide. Collectively, these studies clearly demonstrate how socially 
constructed gender identity’s influence emotional responses to climate change.  
 
Only one study which goes further into depth how climate change impacts on women 
specifically was carried out by Boetto & McKinnon (2013) in Australia. In their study, with 
women service providers, Boetto & McKinnon (2013) suggested that the social roles and 
responsibilities which women held affected how women were being impacted by climate 
change. From an identity perspective, the women saw themselves as buyers, food preparers, 
household managers and carers, responsible for making ends meet in the home. Boetto & 
McKinnon (2013) also suggested that many of the practices to adapt to climate change on a 
consumer and household level, such as decreasing water use and using energy efficient 
lightbulbs, often also thus fell under the responsibility of women. In addition, women were 
more likely to be targeted by consumerist ads, which conflicted with environmentally 
responsible behaviours and choices.  
 
 
36 
Overall, this section has demonstrated that in both studies located in the Global North and 
South, social roles and norms influence the way in which different genders are impacted by 
climate change. Childcaring and home life responsibilities, alongside social inequality can 
create worse psychological outcomes for women. However, this appeared to be less 
conclusive for women in the Global North as several studies failed to show that women were 
more affected than men. This points to a need for more depth research into how climate 
change impacts on all genders. However, the dominance of studies on men, clearly points to 
a significant gap in the literature on women’s experiences and from women from the United 
Kingdom in particular.  
 
2.5. 
Proposed research questions 
 
Based on the gaps identified in the literature review, the proposed research questions are as 
follows: 
 
1. What are the climate grief experiences of UK women, as told through their life stories? 
 
2. To what extent can a life story method be used to explore emotional responses to 
climate change?  
 
 
 
37 
2.6. 
Summary of research contributions 
 
Upon answering these two questions, the following section briefly summarizes the four key 
research areas which this thesis contributes to.   
 
1. Contribute to our understanding of non-economic losses of climate change 
Currently there exists a lack of research concerning the non-economic losses of climate 
change. While most studies within the Loss and Damage domain have focused on economic 
losses, studies on subjective, non-tangible losses, such as identity are limited (McNamara, 
Karen E. and Jackson, 2018). By focusing on emotional experiences and as nontangible 
losses this study will contribute knowledge to the domain of Loss and Damage. 
 
2. Contribute to our understanding of climate grief experiences in a non-indigenous 
population, the United Kingdom  
Based on this literature review, it appeared that the emotion of ‘ecological grief’ (Cunsolo and 
Ellis, 2018) most clearly articulated an association between identity and emotions. However, 
to date studies on ecological grief have focused primarily on indigenous or farming 
communities who have a direct relationship to the land (Borish, et al., 2021; Boafo and Yeboah, 
2024). Considering that climate change will affect communities in different ways, this research 
will explore climate grief experiences in a completely different context. To date no study on 
climate grief, which I base on the concept of ecological grief, has taken place in the United 
Kingdom.  
 
3. Contribute to understanding of women’s experiences in climate change 
Within climate change research, there remains a gap in studies which exclusively focus on 
women’s experiences. Although several studies focus on the experiences of men (Kunde, et 
al., 2018; Perceval, et al., 2018), the emotional experiences of women remain largely absent, 
particularly in the Global North. As emotional and identity dynamics are influenced by social 
norms and behaviours, women’s experiences will be different than men’s. As women make up 
half of the global population it is important to obtain a more nuanced picture of the emotional 
impacts of climate change.  
 
4. Contribute to an innovation in research methods through an adaptation of BNIM  
Based on this literature review, it was identified that while qualitative methods appear most 
suitable to study climate grief experience, to date it very few studies have used life story 
 
38 
methods such as the BNIM within climate change research overall. However, there is an 
urgent need to innovate social science research methods in the environmental sciences 
(Lertzman 2015). Hence, this study will contribute to this gap in knowledge, by applying an 
adaption of BNIM to study climate grief experiences and conducting an evaluation of the 
method.  
 
 
 
39 
3. 
Methods 
 
When it comes to environmental research within the social sciences, Lertzman (2015) argues 
that methodological innovations are needed. This is especially pertinent when it comes to 
studies that focus on behaviour, engagement and perceptions – which are largely driven by 
unconscious processes. I suggest that this is also needed when it comes to the study of 
complex climate emotions, such as climate grief.  
 
Considering the complexity and emotive nature of climate grief and the need to uncover what 
may well be hidden aspects of identity, meant that the subject matter of this research can be 
considered as what is referred to as ‘deeply rooted’. Deeply rooted refers to phenomena that 
requires a profound understanding of the respondents themselves. It may concern events or 
experiences which could be emotional or distressing, which may be suppressed (Ritchie, 
2003).  
 
Although both qualitative (Cunsolo, et al., 2020) and quantitative methods (Marshall, Nadine, 
et al., 2019), have been used to study climate emotions, such as ecological grief – an 
investigation into a deeply rooted experience requires an open and creative approach. While 
more novel approaches are emerging (see Borish, et al., 2021), I was particularly drawn to the 
work of Lertzman (2015) who used an adaptation of the Biographic Narrative Interpretative 
Method (BNIM) to study environmental melancholia.  
 
BNIM is a qualitative, biographical research method which encompasses a formulaic approach 
across two to three interview sessions. The first interview consists of one single question, the 
SQUIN (Single Question Aimed at Inducing Narrative), whereby the participant is invited to 
share their whole or part of their life story in their own words without interruption. The non-
directional nature of the interview process allows participants to freely associate and release 
emotions and experiences which may be hidden and uneasily uncovered if using more 
conventional methods. Lertzman (2015) used this approach in combination with Free 
Associative Interviews (FANI). Based in psycho-analytical orientation, FANI also appeared 
particularly promising in helping to understand climate grief experiences.  
 
To study BNIM more in-depth, I participated in a workshop with Tom Wengraf in March 2021, 
who developed several manuals on the method. Based on that training I was initially interested 
in applying the full data collection method, however, after carrying out a pilot, I modified my 
approach, particularly around the format of the second interview. Within that exploration, I 
 
40 
developed a novel adaptation of BNIM through the creation of Visual Biographical Timelines 
and added photo elicitation interviews. The first section in this chapter will describe this whole 
method in detail, illustrating how BNIM, or the adaptation thereof can indeed be considered 
as ‘high preparation, high-risk, high-gain and high-analysis operations’ (Wengraf, 2001, p. 5). 
This will then be followed by an overview of how I determined my sample and recruited the 
women participants.  
 
Considering that life story methods seek to uncover deep emotions; ethics were an important 
part of this research. Most participants shared traumatic events during their life story interview, 
and although several pre-cautions were taken, I questioned whether more ethical 
considerations are needed when using BNIM. This topic is addressed in Chapter 5, where I 
discuss the value, limitations and ethics of using full SQUINS. The chapter will close with an 
overview of the analytical approaches I applied to analyse and interpret the data I collected. 
 
As a reminder, the two questions which I sought to answer with this research were:   
 
1. What are the climate grief experiences of women, as told through their life stories?  
 
2. To what extent can a life story method be used to explore emotional responses to 
climate change?  
 
 
3.1. 
Data collection methods 
 
The following section outlines in more detail how the data collection was carried out. The 
approaches were largely inspired by BNIM and FANI. Both methods have previously been 
used to study climate emotions (Lertzman, 2015) and experiences of loss (Sekechi, 2020). 
This section will begin with a foundational overview of both approaches, followed by a more 
detailed account of the data collection method applied.   
 
3.1.1. Qualitative approaches: BNIM & FANI   
To study climate grief experiences, I used a qualitative approach - which is used to provide an 
in-depth understanding of research participants, their experiences and perspectives. While 
there is not one way to define qualitative research, it is widely acknowledged that qualitative 
research “is a naturalistic, interpretative approach concerned with understanding the 
meanings which people attach to phenomena (actions, decisions, beliefs, values, etc.) within 
 
41 
their social world” (Snape and Spencer, 2003, p. 3). I will first introduce the two approaches 
which informed my methods, namely BNIM (Wengraf, 2001) and FANI (Hollway and Jefferson, 
2000) – both of which are based in psycho-analytical orientation.  
 
The Biographic Narrative Interpretative Method, commonly referred to as ‘BNIM’, is 
considered a biographical psychosocial research method. BNIM in its original form was first 
introduced by German-born Rosenthal who used the method to study the generational impact 
of the Holocaust on its survivors and their family descendants (Rosenthal, 2010). Following 
this original work, the method was further described and systematized by Wengraf (2001) who 
wrote a manual on this life story method. The appeal of BNIM is that it is typically used to 
understand recent and present-day experiences, practices and choice points in people’s life 
stories. Biographical approaches, such as BNIM, have also been shown to be highly effective 
in understanding and analyzing change that is more incremental and embedded in the 
mundane (Henwood, et al., 2015). Furthermore, BNIM also helps to provide an understanding 
of the society which participants are part of, as well as their social relational networks 
(Wengraf, 2021b).  
 
In addition to what BNIM offers, I also considered the position of the participants and how 
engaging in stories about climate grief could arouse difficult emotions and defense 
mechanisms. For this I turned to psychoanalytical based methods, such as the Free 
Associative Narrative Interview (FANI) (Hollway and Jefferson, 2000). FANI is useful in that it 
reminds the interviewer that participants don’t always have the capacity to fully know and 
understand themselves. In their approach, Hollway & Jefferson (2000) challenge the notion 
that participants ‘tell it like it is’ and that they are willing to share, often very personal, 
information with a stranger interviewer. From this perspective, participants are not seen as 
rational actors, but psychosocial subjects and there is complexity in understanding research 
subjects.  
 
In their work, Hollway & Jefferson (2000) suggest that participants are defended beings, in 
that they may interpret interview questions differently as to how they were intended by the 
researcher. To protect the more vulnerable aspects of themselves, participants many mask or 
disguise painful emotions. In other instances, they may not know why they feel or experience 
certain things (Hollway and Jefferson, 2000). Overall, this implies that there is thus 
considerable complexity in understanding research participants. Similarly, the researcher may 
also be blinded by their own biases and perceptions when conducting the research. All these 
points were thus taken into consideration when carrying out the research. The next section 
will outline in more detail the method.  
 
42 
3.1.2. The interview sessions – A BNIM adaptation 
BNIM first crossed my path through the work of Lertzman (2015) who applied an adaptation 
of BNIM to study environmental melancholia. BNIM data collection consists of a formulaic 
approach to data collection, consisting of two or three interview sessions, with each interview 
session designed to build on the previous. Overall, the first interview begins with an open life 
story question, using a SQUIN. The second session follows a structured approach whereby 
the interviewer can only ask questions related to topics raised in the first session. The third 
interview is optional and can be used to ask any questions on new topics.  
 
In their work, Lertzman (2015) and Sekechi (2020), adapted the BNIM approach, and used a 
semi-structured interview format during the second interview, instead of the format 
recommended by BNIM. In the third interview, Lertzman (2015) introduced an advertisement 
for participants to comment on. Although I was aware that BNIM could be adapted to suit the 
needs of the research, I was initially drawn to the more conventional approach for the second 
interview. However, after carrying out a pilot with one participant, I decided to change the 
structure of the second interview session, by including a semi-structured interview format. I 
elaborate on this adaptation in section 3.1.3, where I discuss the pilot. The following sections 
will expand on my data collection methods more in depth.  All the interviews were held online 
via Zoom or Teams, which I will reflect on later in this section.  
 
Pre-Interview telephone conversation 
To start building rapport with participants and to provide additional information about the 
research, I set up a preliminary phone call with each potential participant. During this call, I 
provided topline information about the research, discussed the documentation which I would 
be emailing them, such as the Participation Information Sheet and the Participant Consent For 
(see Appendix A and B). I also used this time to schedule in the date and time for the first 
online interview session.  
 
The first interview: SQUIN (Single Question aimed at Inducing Narrative) 
The first BNIM interview opens with the SQUIN, the Single Question aimed at Inducing 
Narrative (Wengraf, 2001). During this interview session, one single opening question is 
asked, inviting the participant to tell their life story.  A SQUIN can be asked in two different 
ways, either full or partial. A full SQUIN is completely open, whereby a participant is asked 
their whole life story. This is the best version according to Wengraf (2001), as it gives the 
participant complete reign over what is shared. On the other hand, partial SQUINs, set specific 
boundaries. They are centered around a specific phase of life, a biographical strand (e.g. 
 
43 
career) or a historical phase (e.g. COVID) or a specific issue (e.g. migration), depending on 
the aims of the study (Wengraf 2001).    
 
Several assumptions led me to adopt a full SQUIN for this research. First, I was interested in 
understanding the deeper experiences of climate grief which could be related to any aspect of 
someone’s life. Second, emotions are not static, and climate grief could have gradually 
evolved over the course of one’s life. Third, a full SQUIN would prompt participants to think 
more broadly, rather than narrowly focus in on their climate grief alone. Fourth, the approach 
would allow me to better understand a participant’s own system of relevance, key events and 
turning points, as well as the wider context in which to embed and better understand their 
experiences of climate grief. The SQUIN that I asked every participant was as follows:  
 
 ‘I would like you to tell me your life story and all the events and experiences 
which have played a role in who you are today. Start wherever you like. 
Please take the time you need. I’ll listen first, I won’t interrupt, I’ll just take 
some notes afterwards.’   
 
For the interview sessions, the participants were invited to talk as long as needed, and I only 
took notes. The purpose of the note taking during the interview was to be able to prepare for 
the second set of questions. These would be used during the second interview - or could also 
be used in case the first interview lasted under 15 minutes. The style of note taking during the 
first interview followed a specific format as suggested by Wengraf (2001). The note taking 
involved writing down cue phrases, using the exact same wording as the participant and 
ordered in the same way they were mentioned. These cue phrases were used to prompt for 
more narratives in the second interview, following a specific format. In addition, the noting 
down of cue phrases was also helpful in retaining my focus while listening to each participants 
life story.  
 
The first interview started with an introduction to the research, where I reiterated the purpose, 
reaffirmed confidentiality and asked the participants permission to record the interview. In 
addition, they were reminder that they were free to stop the interview at any time without any 
consequences. Once the participants completed their interview session, they were thanked 
for their time and a second interview was scheduled in. The interviews lasted anywhere 
between 36 minutes and 1 hour and 55 minutes, with most hovering around the 1-hour mark. 
  
 
44 
Preliminary analysis of first interview 
Once the first interview was concluded, I shared the video recording with Penguin Translation 
Services, who provided a written transcript of the interview. This data was analysed and used 
to prepare for the second interview. The aim of this analysis was twofold:  
 
1. To develop a visual chronology the life story, which I called a Visual Biographical 
Timeline (VBT) 
2. Develop a set of questions specific to the participant, or Particular Incident Narratives 
(PINS).  
 
Visual Biographical Timeline  
For the second interview, I developed a Visual Biographical Timeline (VBT) of each 
participant’s life story. BNIM uses a text-based version, called a Biographical Data Chronology, 
predominantly used for interpretation once all the data has been collected. I instead chose to 
make this visual and use it as a basis for the second interview. The purpose of this was to 
ground the participants’ climate grief experiences into their life story.  
 
To create the timelines, I read the transcripts and listened to the interview video recording. I 
pulled out key pieces of data to place on the timelines. These were made using an online 
programme called Time Graphics. The decision on what to place on the graph was mostly 
straightforward as I tended to focus on objective information, paying attention to events and 
life milestones mentioned by the participant. The distinction between what constituted as a 
single ‘event’ or a ‘time period’ was sometimes a bit more challenging, as single events can 
signal the beginning or ending of a period. In these cases, I included both. While I plotted the 
data as accurately as I could, based on the information I had from the first interview, I decided 
that the exact dates of certain time periods and events would not be that relevant to this 
exercise. I was more interested in understanding the overall sequence of their lives and the 
type of information which the SQUIN elicited, and what the participant felt was relevant enough 
to mention.  
 
When the timeline was complete, I identified several themes and proceeded to colour code 
the timeline, according to theme for ease of understanding. An example of the themes and 
colour coding is provided in figure 2. Figure 3 is an example of Sarah’s timeline. All VBTs can 
be found in Appendix C. To protect the participants’ anonymity, all VBT’s in thesis have been 
anonymised.

47 
Although beyond the scope of this research, this specific method lends itself to much further 
exploration. Examining an individual’s life story enables the extraction of wider themes and 
frameworks relevant to a person’s life, and it is possible to physically see how climate grief 
can evolve across the life story in relation to other personal or global events. For example, 
during the semi-structured part of the second interview, I asked participants to share with me 
– when they first heard about climate change and when climate change became relevant to 
them, to better understand where in their life story climate grief appeared be most salient and 
how it evolved over time. This information was added to the timeline for analytical purposes 
after the second interview.  
 
Development of PINs 
During the second interview, the conventional BNIM approach requires further exploration into 
specific events, or experiences, known as PINs (Particular Incident Narratives) (Wengraf 
2019). These are questions aimed at eliciting deeper narratives about a particular topic.  Within 
BNIM, there is a formulaic approach to selecting and developing PINs. However, based on my 
experience with the pilot I did not follow this approach to the letter. I elaborate on this further 
on this decision point in section 3.1.3.  
 
For the second interview, I developed a small set of questions, based on PINs. I developed a 
framework to select topics for the questions, and I used the same phrasing of the participant 
from their first interview to develop the sentence structure of each PIN. I also asked the 
questions in the same order in which the topics had been mentioned in the first interview.  
 
The framework to select the topics for questioning was as follows: 
 
• 
Events or experiences which appeared to bear relation to their experiences of climate 
grief or other climate emotions. 
• 
Events or experience which appeared to bear relation to climate change.  
• 
Other key life turning points, emotional events which appear relevant to the participant 
themselves 
 
Thus overall, the bespoke analysis between the first and second interview per participant, 
consisted of the development of the VBT and the unique set of PINs.  
 
 
48 
The second interview  
The second interview took place within 1-2 weeks of the first interview and consisted of three 
distinct parts. During the first part of the second interview, I showed the participant the Visual 
Biographical Timeline, which we reviewed the timeline together for any inconsistencies. I also 
provided the participant the opportunity to remove or add anything relevant to the timelines as 
well as provide their overall opinion on what they saw. Any simple changes to the Visual 
Biographical Timeline were made in real time.  
 
I then proceeded to ask the set of PINS which I had developed specifically for each participant. 
For examples of PINs, see Appendix D or chapter 5. This was then followed by the semi 
structured format interview questions, which can be found in Appendix D. Once the second 
interview was completed, participants were invited to carry out a photo elicitation task in 
preparation for the third and final interview. The details of the activity were discussed with the 
participants and written information was shared with the participants via email as well. 
 
Third interview: Participant-driven photo elicitation interviews 
While a third interview is optional with BNIM, I felt that incorporating visual methods would be 
useful in helping participants to access deeper emotional experiences. I therefor turned to 
photo elicitation interviews, which have been used to study regular grief experiences (Stutey, 
et al., 2016; Creighton, et al., 2017). The use of visual stimuli during interviews could prompt 
deeper emotional connections to memories and thoughts, which in turn could help participants 
provide richer accounts of their experiences. This was ideal for the third interview session, 
whereby the photographs would promote further discussion and encourage alternative 
dimensions that may not have been previously considered (Collier, 1957). 
 
While a distinction can be made between participant and researcher-driven photographs 
(Bates, et al., 2017), in this case, participant-driven techniques were used, whereby 
participants were invited to take or select photos themselves. At the end of the second 
interview, participants were asked to take 5-10 photographs in their immediate environment, 
of objects, scenes or places which resembled their climate grief and other associated climate 
emotions. In addition to obtaining a deeper understanding of their grief experiences, I wanted 
to tease out how climate grief was experienced in the ‘every day’.  
 
The participants took the photos using their phones and shared the images with me a few 
days before the interview session, either via WhatsApp or email. I reviewed the photographs 
before each interview. The photographs were used to guide the final interview and in that 
 
49 
sense were viewed as a communication medium between the researcher and participant, 
rather than an empirical representation of their reality. During the interviews, the photographs 
served as a ‘language bridge’, whereby participants were more able to relay complex thoughts 
or emotions (Collier, 1957, p. 858).  
 
During the interview, the participants were asked to describe how they found the assignment 
to better understand the context in which the photographs were taken. I then displayed the 
photographs on our shared screen, and we discussed each photograph. To ensure that the 
research remained participant-driven, participants were prompted to select specific images 
which they would like to elaborate on. Several of these photographs are presented in chapter 
4. All the final interviews took place within 4 weeks of their second interview.  
 
3.1.3. Additional practices and considerations 
The following subsections briefly outline some additional practices which were adopted as part 
of this research, namely a specific type of field journal recommended by BNIM (Wengraf, 
2001), a pilot interview, as well as some considerations around online interviewing.  
 
DS-FA (Dated Situated-Free Associative) Notes 
During the fieldwork I adopted a specific type of journalling, recommend by Wengraf (2021). 
According to Wengraf (2021), these are referred to as DS-FA notes, or Dated Situated-Free 
Associative notes. In other words, the notetaking should be ‘Dated-Situated’, implying that 
there should be an objectivity to the notes to account for the date and moment in time – a 
physical description of where the researcher is at. This is then accompanied by Free 
Associative notes, which are there to provide a more subjective description about the 
experience of the researcher. The DS-FA notes formed a crucial part of the research interview, 
and I took DS-FA notes before each interview, as well as after each interview.  
 
This method was important, as this research assumes that the researcher is the ‘instrument 
of knowing’ (Hollway and Jefferson, 2001). The aim of this style of notetaking it twofold. With 
regard to the present situation, it can be used as a tool to clear the mind and work out any 
thoughts which may be present before the interview situation. In addition, these types of notes 
can also be beneficial in the future, as a resource to look back on, as your own personal 
biography or learning journey – with the aim of achieving reflexivity (Wengraf, 2021b). These 
notes were also then used in the overall analysis of the method.  
 
 
50 
The Pilot 
While the previous section outlines the method I applied for the research, I also carried out a 
pilot. Although I had originally planned to follow a conventional BNIM approach to the data 
collection, I pivoted away from this approach during the preliminary analysis phase, in between 
the first and second interview. The following section provides an account of the experience 
and a further clarification of the changes which were made.  
 
The pilot was carried out in September 2022, with one participant. The participant was 
recruited via a group of students who were in the process of completing their Masters in 
Sustainability at the Global Sustainability Institute at Anglia Ruskin University. To trial the whole 
experience, the participant was led through the entire research process. Starting with an 
invitation email, the participant was asked to fill in the online recruitment survey, review the 
Participant Information Sheet and return the Consent Form, and shortly thereafter participated 
in all three interviews. As several email interactions had taken place, the pre-interview 
telephone conversation was not deemed necessary and did not take place.  As the participant 
lived in another part of the country, a face-to-face interview was not feasible, and the pilot 
interviews were held online.  
 
The first interview was a success. The interview, starting with a full SQUIN was received well, 
although she was slightly baffled at first: “Oh my God. OK, so that's the main question for the 
entire interview? Wow Ok. Um, well guess I’m going to start at the beginning then…?” Despite 
the initial surprise, the participant was able to share her life story in a largely coherent and 
structured way, from beginning to end. The interview question elicited narratives about several 
deeply emotional experiences. While there were other memories, she did not appear ready to 
share, the foundations had been laid to explore these during the second interview. Coming to 
a natural close, the first interview ended in just under an hour. As required in BNIM, I took DS-
FA notes both before, during and after the interview session.   
 
Whilst preparing for the second interview, several amendments to the conventional BNIM 
approach to the second interview took place. I explored several approaches to creating the 
timeline. I first read the transcript and noted down any objective data which I could use. I 
extracted this data onto sticky notes, but I quickly realised that this would be more challenging 
to analyse digitally and share with others. I thus proceeded to plot the objective data on Time 
Graphics, which became the basis for the VBTs. This was a key adaptation made because of 
the pilot.  
   
 
51 
Despite the coherence of the participant’s life story observed during the interview session, 
there were still many inconsistencies about the sequence of events. Hence, the format of the 
second interview was also amended to accommodate that. I decided that the first 20 minutes 
of the second interview should be used to review the timeline together. The aim of this would 
be threefold. First, I would be able to demonstrate to the participant that I was listening to their 
story, which would hopefully have a positive impact on our rapport. Second, it would allow me 
to fact check information and add in any missing details: the exercise naturally elicited more 
information regarding these gaps, leaving little need for further questioning. Finally, the 
exercise would instil a sense of continuity in the research process, which is important 
considering the two weeks in between the first and second interview.   
  
To honour the participant’s control over the research process, I initially sent the participant the 
link to the online timeline during the interview, so that she could share her screen and review 
the timeline at her own pace and direction. However, asking a participant to navigate in an 
unfamiliar online programme, and reflect on what they were seeing at the same time, proved 
to be somewhat complicated and distracting. In the interviews that followed I decided to guide 
this process myself by sharing my screen and going through the timeline with them.   
 
In addition to the visual element of the VBT, the pilot was also useful in that it prompted me to 
change my approach to the PINs during second interview. PINs must be based on the topics 
mentioned during the first interview, using the participants’ words and phrases to carefully craft 
follow up questions. In addition, these questions would also need to be structured in a specific 
format. BNIM stipulates that the first topic raised by the participant must be selected, as well 
as the last, and the questioning must occur in the exact same order in which the topics were 
raised during the first interview, using the same language – a practice which is referred to as 
the ‘gestalt’. This concept is based on the idea that the freer a behaviour is from external 
constraints, the more easily participants will be able to express their internal dynamics 
(Wengraf, 2001).  
 
I did not follow these guidelines to the letter for several reasons. First, I struggled with the way 
in which the PIN questions needed to be formulated. Figure 4 below is an extract from BNIM 
Sketchbook 333 (Wengraf, 2021b) which I needed to use to formulate my questions. As this 
type of instruction did not come natural to me, I only followed the broader guidelines of PINs. 
Hence, my own version of PINs used the same wording and phrasing of the participants and 
were asked in the same order in which the topics were raised in the first interview.  
 
 
 
52 
Figure 4: BNIM Notepad (Wengraf, 2021b) 
 
 
 
However, I also needed to rethink how many PINs I would ask. This is because during the 
pilot, the participant made no mention of climate change or climate emotions during the 
retelling of her life story – even though the research had been framed and presented as being 
about climate change and identity. This led to me to modify my selection criteria of PINs and 
scale down the number of PINs, but it also encouraged me to develop a semi-structured 
interview. The second pilot interview, thus consisted of the three parts: a review of the VBT, a 
small set of PINs and a semi-structured interview, directly related to the topic of climate change 
and climate emotions.  
 
Although the pilot inspired me to adapt the second interview format, another option could also 
have been explored. In some cases, SQUINs can be adapted into a ‘partial SQUIN’ (Wengraf, 
2001). This implies asking participants to focus on one part of their lives. After careful 
consideration, I decided against this approach as the key point was to better understand each 
participant’s whole life story. In addition, I wanted to understand the potentially gradual nature 
of climate grief. Starting the first interview with a partial SQUIN, would likely leave out the 
breadth of context which would be critical to examining climate grief experiences.    
 
53 
After the second interview was completed within 1.5 hours, the photography exercise was 
discussed, and a new date was set for the final interview. The participant agreed to email the 
photographs a few days before the final interview. As a reminder, I sent her the question via 
email as well. The participant sent 10 photographs in total, focusing on a wide range of 
emotions, including loss and grief. The photographs were reviewed beforehand, alongside the 
transcript of the second interview. All the photographs were discussed during the third 
interview using a topic list of open-ended questions. The final interview was completed within 
4 weeks of the second interview as originally planned, and no changes were made to this 
format. 
  
Overall, the pilot interview was a success in that it contributed to several changes all related 
to the second interview which was originally only supposed to consist of PINs. Instead, the 
format was changed to include the visual representation of the VBTs, the joint discussion of 
the VBTs, the selection criteria and scaling down of the PINs and the development of a semi-
structured interview. As the changes were made during the preliminary analysis phase, in 
between the first and second interview, the data from the pilot was included in the final dataset. 
 
Online Research - benefits and disadvantages 
All the interviews were held online which had both advantages and disadvantages. The 
advantages were that I was able to interview women who were living in various locations in 
the United Kingdom. I was also able to manage my time better, as I did not have to travel. In 
addition, it appeared to have benefitted the participants, as they were interviewed in their own 
homes - which I believe made most of them more comfortable.  
 
However, what was challenging at times, was the difficulty in reading non-verbal cues clearly. 
In addition, the interviews might have been more straining, as my focus and concentration 
relied predominantly on my hearing and seeing faculties. In some instances, there also 
appeared less room for informal conversation and I was not able to easily pick up other 
contextual information which I might have been able to better in their home environment. 
However, on balance, having the interviews online did not appear to have negatively impacted 
on the research.  
 
 
 
 
 
54 
3.2. 
Participant Recruitment 
 
Within qualitative research, the aim of selecting a sample is not to measure prevalence or 
scale, but to ensure that the participants represent the necessary characteristics which define 
the object of study (Ritchie, Lewis and El am, 2003). One key characteristic of the research 
participants is that they are all women.  The decision to carry out the research with women 
only was guided by a gap in women’s voices in the climate change literature as identified in 
the literature review in chapter 2, and that emotional experiences are gendered. The following 
sections outline how this research further defined the research sample and how the 
participants were recruited. 
 
Defining the research sample and ensuring diversity 
To select participants, this research applied a purposive sampling approach. This implies that 
the women selected for the research were chosen based on features, characteristics or 
experiences which would allow for a deeper exploration of the topic of research. From that 
perspective, the research would be able to meet its objectives - but it was open enough to 
include for a diverse range of experiences (Ritchie, Lewis and El am, 2003). 
 
All the participants would be required to have some experience with climate grief. Since 
climate grief is a novel concept and regular grief experiences can be expressed in several 
different ways (Worden, 2001) a broad approach was used to define it. In addition, grief 
responses to climate change may be hidden (Norgaard, 2011; Lertzman, 2015). Therefore, in 
addition to the term, ‘climate grief’, more generic language was used, i.e. the language of 
‘difficult emotions related to climate change’, to define this required experience. An additional 
aspect to consider was the time commitment required from participants for the three 
interviews, as well as the level of their ability to communicate verbally to engage in some 
introspection about their lives and experiences with climate grief.  
 
Overall, this research was conducted with women who:  
 
• 
Had experience with climate grief, or any other difficult emotions related to climate 
change.  
• 
Were based in the United Kingdom.  
• 
Were available for three in-depth interviews. 
• 
Had the ability to converse about their lives and emotional experiences. 
 
 
55 
In addition, I was also interested in speaking to women at different phases of their life span. 
Considering that I was working with a small sample, I did not want to limit my study to a specific 
group, such as only students, mothers or pensioners. This means that additional indicators 
such as age, employment status, household composition, were used – to ensure that I would 
be speaking to a diverse group of women. 
 
Sample size and recruitment campaign 
In general, qualitative research samples are small because they don’t measure prevalence or 
incidence but rather seek a rich and detailed account of a particular phenomenon. Considering 
the multi-interview stage research process and the resource intensiveness of the interviews, 
BNIM also typically works with very small number of respondents. Hence, this research was 
aiming to interview between 10-15 participants.  
 
To select participants for the research study, I initially proposed to generate a sample frame. 
As a rule of thumb, the sample frame should be up to 3-4 times the study sample (Ritchie, 
Lewis and El am, 2003), which meant that this research would need to generate a sample 
frame of approximately 45-60 potential participants. To better understand whether the potential 
participants in the sample frame matched the characteristics I was looking for, I developed a 
short screening questionnaire, which was hosted on Jisc Online Surveys. A copy of this 
questionnaire can be found in Appendix E.  
 
To find potential participants, I carried out a 4-month recruitment campaign in 2022. I first 
carried out an internet search to identify organizations which would attract or cater to my 
audience pool. For this initial search, I took a broad approach and sought organizations which 
were focused on climate change, women or sustainable living. Potential organizations were 
identified using a basic Google Search, using combinations of key words such as 
‘sustainability’, ‘environment’, ‘climate change’, ‘organization’ and ‘United Kingdom’.  I first 
included the key word ‘women’ in my search, but this approach resulted in a list of retailers 
selling sustainable clothing or other ethical products. By excluding the term ‘women’, I was 
able to obtain a much more suitable list of organizations to reach out to. 
   
I was primarily interested in organizations that appeared to have a substantial audience or 
membership with whom they would regularly engage. Identifying the organizations’ scope and 
level of engagement with their audience remained challenging, unless it was clearly specified 
to whom they catered to, such as the ‘Women’s Environmental Network’ or ‘Students 
Organizing for Sustainability in the United Kingdom’. Organizations focusing only on children, 
such as ‘Eco-Schools’ were excluded, as well as climate activist groups such as ‘Extinction 
 
56 
Rebellion’. I also avoided thinktanks such as the ‘Green Alliance’ or other business to business 
organizations without a membership base. Overall, I was drawn towards organizations that 
catered to the general UK population with an affinity to the environment, climate change or 
sustainable living.  
 
It is important to highlight here that my rationale for excluding climate activist organizations as 
a recruitment channel was because a significant proportion of studies which explore climate 
emotions have already been carried out with activists. Most recent examples in my literature 
review include Cairns and Pihkala (2024), Zurba et al. (2024) and Dennis and Stock (2024). 
Hence the experiences of individuals who specifically identify as activists are already well 
represented within academic research. To obtain a broader understanding of the climate grief 
experience I felt it was important to seek emotional experiences from a broader cohort of 
women who might not necessarily identify as activists or be affiliated with an activist 
organization. However, while I excluded climate activist organizations as a recruitment 
avenue, I did not exclude any individual who identified themselves as ‘activists’ from 
participating in my study. Three participants identified themselves as ‘activists’ on the 
recruitment questionnaire and all three participated in the research.  
   
Using this broad recruitment approach, I developed an initial list of 36 UK-based organizations. 
The full list can be found in Appendix F. Most were national organizations, although some 
international and local were included. A broad range of organization types were represented, 
from charities to professional networking sites, as well as community, religious and lifestyle 
organizations. I specifically reached out to organizations catering to the BAME community. 
Several environmentally focused blogs were also included.    
 
Once I had collected all the email addresses, a first email invitation was sent in August 2022. 
This email contained a link to my page on the university website, a research flyer (see 
Appendix G) and the recruitment survey which I had created beforehand. A follow up email 
was sent in September 2022, and a final email in January 2023. Overall, the email invitations 
generated a response rate of 30%. I was invited to post an ad in one LinkedIn group and 
develop an ad for two different digital newsletters, which the organisations distributed to their 
members. Other organisations supported me by circulating the flyer on their social media 
channels, sharing it internally via email or posting it on their internal bulletin board. Towards 
the end of the recruitment campaign in January 2023, I also attempted to recruit more 
participants via a snowballing method. I contacted by email the five participants who had 
already participated in the research by that time. I provided the research flyer and asked them 
to share it with any women they knew who would be interested.  
 
57 
Overview of the participants  
Overall, the recruitment campaigns resulted in 22 completed recruitment surveys. Out of this 
pool, eleven participants were selected to participate, all of whom were eligible and ended up 
completing all three interview sessions.  As my pool of participants was somewhat smaller 
than the sample frame that I had anticipated, my initial selection process was predominantly 
focused on ensuring there was sufficient diversity with regards to age. Although I had reached 
out to several organisations representing members from the BAME (Black Asian Minority 
Ethnic) community, I was not able to recruit any potential participants from this community. All 
the participants were White - English, Welsh, Scottish, Northern Irish or British, or from Any 
other White background.

responses to the recruitment questionnaire can be found in Appendix H. To respect their 
anonymity, I have omitted information regarding the list of identities they provided in the 
recruitment questionnaire, as well as other identifying markers.

3.3. 
Ethics 
Ethics form a critical part of scientific research, and in line with sound research practice, this 
study underwent a rigorous ethics process to ensure that the study would not harm the 
wellbeing of the participants or the researcher. This study design was reviewed and approved 
by the relevant Ethics Committee at the Faculty of Science and Engineering at Anglia Ruskin 
University. A copy of my ethics approval letter has been provided in Appendix I.  
 
The following sections further elaborate on how this research adopted ethical approaches, by 
means of practices such as informed consent, participant anonymity and confidentiality and 
protecting both the participant and researcher from harm during the interview process. Specific 
attention was also paid to the ethical considerations of using the interviewing technique in 
accordance with BNIM  
 
Informed consent 
Prior to participation, research participants were requested to provide their consent by means 
of a signing a Participant Consent Form and a Participant Information Sheet. The consent 
form contained clear and relevant information about the study, such as the purpose, members 
of the research team, how their information would be used, how they would remain anonymous 
and what was expected of them during the research, as well as any other practical details. 
Prior to every interview, participants were reminded that their participation was voluntary, and 
that they could drop out at any time without any consequences. As all participants were over 
the age of 18, they were of legal age to provide their own consent. A copy of the Participant 
Consent Form as well as a Participant Information Sheet has been provided in the Appendix 
B and A, respectively.  
 
Anonymity & confidentiality 
This research study took steps to respect the anonymity of the participants. Anonymity implies 
that the participants cannot be identified. All the data presented in this thesis were anonymized 
 
59 
and adequate steps were taken to ensure that any combination of unique details were modified 
or omitted to ensure that participants could not be identified. Confidentiality implies that the 
research team can link responses to individuals – but that the research team has an obligation 
to keep that private.   
 
Several steps were taken to ensure anonymity. Pseudonyms were created in the presentation 
of the final thesis. In addition, identifying markers were also omitted (e.g. birth dates, place 
names, reported identities) or generalised, particularly in the Visual Biographical Timelines. 
The responses provided by the participant were not confidential as several quotes are used 
within this research. However, participants will remain anonymous.  
 
Audio recordings were stored securely on a password protected computer and backed up on 
hard drive. Consent forms were kept separately from the data. Personal data will be destroyed 
after the PhD has been completed and awarded by 31/12/2026 and recordings will be deleted 
by 31/12/2026.   
 
Protecting participant and researcher from harm  
Life story methods, such as BNIM have a way of revealing sensitive and deeply personal 
information about participants, which may sometimes be unexpected. As this research 
concerns climate grief experiences and participants had the opportunity to share openly about 
all aspects of their lives, which meant that some shared difficult experiences. Hence, it was 
important to consider how to best manage participant emotions as well as my own. This 
section outlines how I prepared and mentally anticipated my responses to emotionally charged 
interviews.  
 
It is worth mentioning that, although I was aware that interviews could be emotionally charged, 
during the interview process itself I still found myself feeling surprised at the intimate details 
which were being shared. Several participants shared traumatic events during their life story 
interview, which made me question whether more ethical considerations are needed when 
using BNIM. In this section, I predominantly focus on how I prepared for the interviews, whilst 
I go into much more detail about my experience in chapter 5 where I discuss the value, 
limitations and ethics of using full SQUINs.  
 
Ethical considerations using BNIM 
When I first started exploring BNIM, it was suggested that BNIM was more ethical than most 
structured interview styles (Wengraf, 2021a). This was based on the fact that the direction of 
 
60 
the first and often the second interview was controlled by the participant. In that sense, 
participants could share as little or as much as they wanted. Yet Wengraf (2021a) also 
acknowledged that BNIM was a powerful tool, and several suggestions were made to prepare 
for the interviews. These are briefly summarized below.   
 
In terms of managing difficult emotions and causing no harm to the participant, Wengraf 
(2021a) stipulates that the role of the researcher is to always provide emotional 
acknowledgement to the participant’s narrative and allow the participant to tell as much or as 
little as they feel comfortable with. However, as the first two interview sessions require the 
researcher to relinquish control over the direction of the interview, the participant may share 
information about events that may be unexpected and perhaps emotionally difficult to hear for 
the researcher (Wengraf, 2021a). Therefor the researcher must also be prepared for such 
occurrences, not only for their own protection, but also for the participant. Unexpected, 
emotionally charged topics should be approached sensitively and the researcher should allow 
for difficult topics to emerge as they come up. The researcher should also be aware, as much 
as possible, of their own emotional reactions and shouldn’t let their own discomfort influence 
direction of the interview. While there is balance to this, avoiding certain topics altogether risks 
devaluing the emotional experience of the participant which can be harmful to them. In addition 
to this guidance, I also sought insights from other sources to prepare for the interview 
sessions.  
 
Managing emotional interviews in the wider literature 
According to Legard, Keegan and Ward (2013) there are a few ways in which researchers can 
practically prepare for sensitive interviews to benefit both themselves and the participants. For 
example, they recommend that at the start of every interview, the participant should be 
reminded of confidentiality to help put them at ease. In addition, they also recommended 
having available information from relevant institutions to provide to participants, if needed after 
the interview is finished. Hence, I had this information at hand. For serious urgent cases, I had 
the details of the Samaritans, a UK based organisation which provides free support to any 
individual who needs it.  
 
There are also a few other ways in which I mentally prepared for emotionally charged 
interviews. Prior to the interview sessions I kept in mind that the participant has agreed to be 
interviewed – and that the participant provided their consent and had been informed about the 
research. In more emotional situations, I remained empathetic and acknowledged the distress 
of the participant - but tried not to convey my own emotions or feelings. During the interview, 
 
61 
I also tried to remain alert to the participant’s body language and change in tone of voice to 
anticipate strong emotional responses. However, this was somewhat more difficult with the 
online format. At times when participants appeared distressed, I recommended to take a 
break. However, it was also important that I considered my own wellbeing.  
 
Protecting the researcher 
According to Lewis (2003), researchers should also be protected from any harm throughout 
the research, particularly during the field work phase. Researchers may be at risk if they are 
conducting interviews in people’s homes or if they are researching emotional and sensitive 
issues. (Lewis, 2003). Considering that the interviews took place online, harm through contact 
or travel was limited in this case.  
 
However, the emotional toll which sensitive interviews can have on researchers was 
unexpected. Although I had prepared to take more time in between the interviews, I found it 
difficult to take enough time in between the interviews to recharge. Due to the PhD being part 
time, I was limited to specific times and days for the interviews, and as I was working on the 
days in between, overall, not enough time was taken to recharge.  
 
3.4. 
Analysis  
 
While much onus is often put on how to manage data analysis, which primarily consists of 
organising, labelling and reordering, less has been written about the intellectual processes 
which can be used to understand the data and generate research findings (Spencer, Ritchie 
and O'Connor, 2003). On a practical level, the purpose of analysis is to find answers to the 
research questions, whereby these answers can be found in the various themes and ideas 
which emerge in pattern-like formations within the data. Others suggest that it is helpful to 
understand data analysis as a ‘process of meaning making’ (Merriam and Tisdell, 2015, p. 
105). 
 
The approach I took to analyse the data can best be described by what Merriam & Tisdell 
(2015) refer to as “recursive and dynamic”. The methods I had designed to capture the data 
required me to start analysing the data after the very first interview and continued in between 
the interviews. The DS-FA notes taken before and after each interview included reflective and 
analytical musings and I analysed the life stories to create the Visual Biographical Timelines 
as well as the tailored interview questions which I used for the second interview. While I 
described my approach of the preliminary analysis of the Visual Biographical Timelines and 
 
62 
the tailored interview questions in section 3.1.2, this section will outline my broader approach 
to the data analysis which I undertook to answer my research questions:  
 
1. What are the climate grief experiences of women, as told through their life stories?  
2. To what extent can a life story method be used to explore emotional responses to 
climate change?  
 
3.4.1. Analytical process for climate grief experiences  
Although BNIM offers both data collection and interpretation methods, I was less inclined to 
adopt the data analysis approach. This was also technically not possible, as I had adapted 
BNIM - and BNIM data interpretation can only be applied if BNIM data collection methods have 
been applied in full.  In addition, BNIM case study interpretation is generally applied to smaller 
research samples (e.g. 8 in Flynn, 2019), and in larger samples, these are carried out by a 
group or panel of researchers. A more detailed analysis of how BNIM has been applied across 
the literature is presented in section 5.2. Furthermore, as I was also more interested in 
understanding cross cutting themes and patterns, a thematic analysis was deemed more 
suitable to achieve that aim.  
 
The methods that I used to collect the data resulted in an overwhelming volume of rich, largely 
unstructured data. After all the interviews had been completed, I was tasked with working 
through 42 hours of interview data, entailing 304,557 words across 549 pages. It is worth 
mentioning that 114 photographs were also included as part of the third interview. However, 
the images were used as a language bridge, rather than an object of analysis itself. A more 
detailed overview has been provided in table 2. The overview excludes my DS-FA notes, which 
were part of the analysis of BNIM, which I discuss in section 3.4.2.
