Wengraf (2001) describes his method as “high-analysis operations’ (Wengraf, 2001, p. 5) - 
and this proved to be true. It was therefore critical that I adopted a systematic approach. 
Although Lertzman (2015) analysed her data manually, I opted for a more digital approach 
and enlisted the support of NVivo. To interpret the climate grief experiences of women, I 
applied multi-stage analytical approach, starting with the indexing of data. I then applied an 
emotion-based analysis to all the interview data and developed analytical frameworks. To 
further support with the interpretation, I used Worden’s model (2001) on regular grief 
experiences and developed visual concept maps. The next sections will elaborate on these 
approaches.  
 
Indexing and applying an emotion-based analysis 
To organize the raw data, I loosely followed the Thematic Analytical Framework Method, which 
is a matrix based analytical method with the aim of systematically classifying qualitative data, 
according to themes, concepts and other emergent categories (Ritchie 2003). Considering 
that I had already analysed the data from the first interview and created the Visual Biographical 
Timelines, here I used NVivo and indexed all the data from the second and third interviews 
from all the participants. The indexing of topics were guided by the semi-structured interview 
format that I used to conduct the interviews during the second interview. Examples of the 
broader topic categories included: 
 
• 
Causes for climate emotions 
• 
Climate emotions 
• 
Contextual factors 
• 
Emotional experiences 
• 
Impacts of climate emotions 
 
To analyse climate grief experiences more in depth, I adopted an emotion-based analysis. I 
first started with the inclusion of all the emotions that were expressed in relation to climate 
 
64 
change. This is based on the premise that grief can be expressed through multiple different 
emotions, cognitions and behaviours as has been identified by notable scholars on regular 
grief experiences (Kubler-Ross, 1970; Worden, 2001). The narrative units that were included 
in the analysis on grief experiences therefore had to meet two criteria. Firstly, they had to in 
some way be related to climate change and second, they must have included an emotive 
element.  The narrative units for inclusion in the emotion-based analysis were identified 
according to any of following three criteria:  
 
1. The narrative unit was a direct response to a question that I specifically asked within 
the context of climate change.   
2. The relation to climate change was clarified by me, in dialogue with the participant 
during the interview (e.g., how does this relate to climate change?)  
3. The narrative unit contained direct or indirect verbal inferences to climate change (e.g., 
getting hotter)   
  
The emotive element was primarily identified through the words expressed by the women 
(e.g., I am sad) and assigned a code. By ‘code’ I imply a word that symbolically assigns a 
salient attribute to piece of narrative text (Saldana, 2021). To make more concise the emotions, 
they were then categorized according to the five basic emotions: anger, disgust, fear, and 
surprise (Ekman, 1999). More complex emotions, such as powerlessness, anxiety and guilt 
were categorized separately. The approach to interpreting complex emotions was slightly 
more nuanced.  In these cases, the verbal expression was used as a guide, but these 
passages sometimes had an underlying tone to them. For example, a participant might 
conclude that they are powerless or feel hopeless, but the overall tone of the passage 
conveyed as sense of sadness. The coding tree for fear is provided in Appendix J and I have 
included an example of a coded interview in Appendix K.  
 
One key challenge I experienced during the analysis was that some women expressed 
multiple emotions in the same passage, with the emotion shifting as they spoke. These 
passages were indexed using multiple codes. However, in terms of analysis, they were 
sometimes interpreted as the last emotion they named.  This is because it was through talk, 
through which the women appeared to discover and (re) label their emotions, with the last 
emotion often appearing to be reflective or conclusive. Using talk to discover their emotions, 
meant that the narrative units ended up being quite long and emotional experiences were 
often encapsulated in a much wider narrative.  
 
 
65 
After the first round of coding was carried out, each emotion category was analysed separately 
and had their own analytical framework. The criteria for selecting narrative units remained the 
same. To give an example: for anger, 30 narrative units were included in the analytical 
framework. The narrative units varied greatly in length, from 95 to 875 words, with most 
somewhere in between. An excerpt is provided in table 3.

68 
Using Worden’s model for data interpretation 
To further guide the interpretation and demonstrate how the emotions expressed related to 
climate grief, I turned to Worden’s (2001) work on regular grief experiences. I used this model, 
where possible, to add further interpretation to the analytical framework for each emotion. I 
discussed this model in detail in my literature review. In the case of anger for example, there 
appeared only one source of anger identified in Worden’s Model (2001). In climate grief, the 
primary source of anger appears to be distinctly related to the notion that the losses incurred 
by climate change could be prevented. However, the causes of anger differed depending on 
the situation, as well as who the anger was directed towards.     
 
Although Worden’s Model (2001) appeared useful to interpret anger in climate grief 
experiences, it was less helpful for the other emotions. For example, in the case of sadness, 
Worden’s Model (2001) does not provide an explanation for why an individual would feel this 
emotion, assuming that this is self-explanatory. In regular grief, it is the loss of a loved one 
which initiates the grief experience. In the case of climate grief, however, this sense of loss is 
somewhat more ambiguous and requires further examination. A sense of loss frequently 
evoked a sense of sadness, not just in words, but in change of tone which made this a central 
part in the analytical framework for sadness. The various types of losses mentioned then 
required further examination. Furthermore, passages around powerlessness and 
hopelessness evoked a tone of sadness and thus these two emotions were also included in 
the analytical framework for sadness.  
 
One of the caveats of this process is that I did not incorporate Worden’s Model (2001) within 
the methods, rather the model was applied in hindsight, during the analytical phase to interpret 
the findings. This means that I could have missed some key elements that would have been 
relevant to Worden’s model, by simply not asking. On the other hand, I may have been able 
to capture a more realistic representation of climate grief, by not insinuating anything specific 
during the interview process and keeping the style of questioning relatively open ended.  
  
Concept maps 
Once the coding and analytical frameworks were created for each emotion, I then proceeded 
to develop concept maps to help visualize and interpret each primary emotion separately. The 
concept maps were revised as new ideas and themes emerged, through the analysis and the 
writing process. Examples of the several iterations have been provided in figure 5 below.  
Although these maps were helpful during the analytical process, in the end, the final versions 
of the maps are mainly used in helping to communicate a topline overview per key emotion. 
 
69 
Hence, a concept map is provided at the beginning of each emotion section in chapter 4 to 
help guide the reader.  
 
Figure 5: Iterations of concept maps (sadness & sense of loss)  
 
 
 
Revisiting the Visual Biographical Timelines 
After the initial structuring of the key sections and the findings chapter, setting out the key 
themes, based on the structures identified in the analytical framework tables, I then went back 
to the original interviews and the VBTs I had created to support with the interpretations. This 
proved to be very beneficial in better understanding and bringing to life the positions of the 
participants with regards to why they were feeling a particular way, or how their life experiences 
appeared to have had contributed to their own meaning making. At this point I also saw more 
clearly how the participants were all on very different journeys with regards to the climate grief, 
that were not necessarily dictated by age. While age was a factor for example, with regards to 
their positions on children, in terms of their emotional experience, there was great variance 
across the participants. All the VBTs can be found in Appendix C.  
 
3.4.2.  Analytical process to evaluate an adapted version of BNIM  
To answer my second research question: To what extent can a life story method be used to 
explore emotional responses to climate change? I adopted two different approaches. This 
 
70 
consisted of a systematic literature review using the PRISMA framework (2020) as well as a 
thematic analysis.  
 
Systematic literature review  
To answer my second research question, I first carried out a systematic literature review to 
better understand the state of the art regarding the application of BNIM in scientific research. 
I applied the PRISMA Framework (Page et al., 2021) to guide the literature review, to clearly 
identify the screening and exclusion process of the literature. The Prisma Flow Diagram is 
depicted in figure 6. First, I will provide a brief descriptive summary of the review process.   
 
Using Web of Science, I used the following search terms to identify relevant literature: 
“Biographic Narrative Interpretive Method”, “BNIM”, and “Biographic Narrative Interviewing 
Method”. I purposely chose quite specific, and limited phrasing to ensure that the research 
articles were guided by the method designed by Wengraf (2001). The assumption was that if 
these terms were not used, then it was unlikely that the author would have made any mention 
of the method in sufficient detail – even if it had been used in some shape or form.   
 
This initial search yielded 93 articles. I was surprised at the lack of articles specifically 
mentioning the use of this method and therefore sense checked the approach using another 
database, SCOPUS, and it yielded a very similar result. I then decided to add an additional 
search term for ‘Wengraf’, which only resulted in 4 new relevant records. Starting with a new 
total of 97 records, 11 records were automatically excluded, as they consisted of empty 
entries. 86 records were then screened by reading their abstracts. These were reviewed for 
relevance and 34 non-relevant (off topic, e.g. chemistry related) were excluded. Report 
retrieval was attempted for 52 records largely through online journals. In a handful of cases, 
the authors were contacted directly via email to request access. 1 article was excluded 
because only a Spanish version was available. I then included one additional source which 
inspired this thesis, Environmental Melancholia by Lertzman (2015) – and so a final total of 52 
sources were included in this literature review.    
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
71 
Figure 6: PRISMA 2020 Flow diagram for systematic reviews (dashed boxes are my additions 
to the original diagram)  
 
 
 
 
 
72 
Once all the articles had been identified, each was read and analysed using the framework 
below. In addition to understanding across which disciplines, thematic areas and populations 
BNIM had been used, I was particularly interested in the application of the method.   
 
• 
How and to what extent had BNIM precisely been applied in the study?  
• 
Was BNIM used for primarily data collection and/or interpretation?  
• 
Which components had been used and how (e.g. SQUINS)? 
• 
What, if any, types of amends were made to the method?  
• 
What were the perceived advantages and limitations of using the method?  
• 
How did the researcher and or the participants themselves experience the method?  
 
Evaluation of the SQUIN  
To evaluate the strengths and limitations of my BNIM adaptation, more specifically the 
application of the full SQUIN, I carried out a two-stage analytical approach: a thematic analysis 
across all cases and then examining two selected cases more in depth. I will summarize my 
analytical approach below.  
 
To better understand how participants experienced the SQUIN, I carried out a thematic 
analysis across all the interviews. Here I focused on the narrative units which pertained to the 
participants’ direct, initial responses to the SQUIN. These responses were coded, using codes 
such as: ‘surprised, ‘hesitant’ or ‘neutral’. In addition, I also examined how participants closed 
their life stories when they finished telling them. These responses were coded, using codes 
such as: ‘positive, ‘neutral’, ‘unsure’ or a combination of codes.  
 
In addition to direct responses to the SQUIN I also analysed other comments which were 
about other parts of the research process, such as the photo elicitation activity. At the 
beginning of the third interview, I asked participants: ‘How did you find the exercise 
overall?’  These responses were coded using codes such as: ‘positive’, ‘difficult’, ‘interesting’, 
‘daunting’, ‘easy’ or ‘fun’. Furthermore, I also included in my analysis responses which were 
provided in response to two questions I asked at the end of the last interview, which were: ‘Is 
there anything else that you want to share – that’s been left unsaid?’ And: ‘Is there anything 
you want to ask me’? Responses were coded, using codes such as ‘interesting’, ‘grateful’ and 
‘curious’. Collectively, these responses were analysed within an analytical framework, an 
excerpt of which is provided in figure 7.

Based on this initial review, I selected two cases for deeper exploration. These were selected 
on the basis that they were somewhat different or unique compared to the rest. For example, 
Julie’s case was selected because it was a good illustration of an ethical dilemma which can 
arise as the result of a SQUIN. May’s case, on the other hand, was selected as it was 
illustrative of the interpretative work which is sometimes required when using BNIM.  
 
By analysing several data sources collected during and outside of the research setting, I was 
able to obtain a much wider vantage point to evaluate my method more effectively. As I was 
examining my own reflections as well, I took the analysis further by incorporating psycho 
analytical perspectives, largely based on the work of Hollway and Jefferson (2000). This was 
particularly useful in providing a much deeper level of understanding of interview context 
dynamics.  Overall, this analytical approach allowed for unique insights to emerge to better 
understand the benefits and limitations of the method I applied.  
 
 
77 
4. 
Findings Part I: Climate grief experiences 
 
In this chapter I will present empirical evidence that responds to my first research question: 
What are the climate grief experiences of women, as told through their life stories? In 
answering this question, I sought to obtain a rich understanding of the various ways in which 
climate grief can be expressed and experienced. Through these findings I will illustrate that 
although climate change is often presented as an environmental issue, it could also be seen 
as a social phenomenon. This is underpinned by the notion that grief and the experience of it, 
is a social process (Worden 2001). Although experienced on an individual level, emotions are 
not individual experiences: they are felt in relation to someone or something else.  
 
What this findings chapter offers is a deep dive into the experiences of eleven women who 
grapple with difficult emotions related to climate change. Altogether, the depth, longevity and 
tenacity of these difficult emotions, the behaviours they influence and impact they have, could 
be seen as indicators of climate grief. As a first ever attempt to explore and document climate 
grief in women in the United Kingdom, the aim of the study was not to diagnose or label each 
woman with a grief ‘condition’, rather the aim of this research was to contribute to our 
understanding of the difficult emotions associated with climate change, that could form part of 
a climate grief experience. For ease of reading however, I will refer to the emotional, cognitive 
and behavioural blend of the experience, as the ‘climate grief experience’ - but this is not 
meant to be a definitive experience for any of the women participating in the study.  
 
It is important to highlight that not all women resonated with the word ‘grief’, with some not 
finding it accurate enough to describe their experience. However, the cacophony of difficult 
emotions they did express still suggest that a grief experience could be present, at least in 
part. Every woman’s experience was different, coloured by their past experiences and life 
phases. Nonetheless, in this findings chapter, I attempt to better bring to light what a climate 
grief experience looks like in women. 
 
As with regular grief, no climate grief experience is the same and the characteristics may 
consist of a wide range of emotions, cognitions, and behaviours (Worden, 2001). Although 
cognitions, emotions and behaviours overlap and influence one another, the analysis 
presented here is angled on a specific emotion.  To better understand each emotion, each has 
been analysed separately using Worden’s Model (2001) to support with the interpretation. In 
cases where Worden’s Model (2001) fell short, I have also provided psychoanalytical 
interpretations, which appeared particularly useful in understanding anger and fear responses. 
 
78 
In addition, I have also woven in the unique histories of the women to provide further context 
behind their emotional experience. The deeper analysis of how life stories can contribute to 
understanding the climate grief experience is elaborated on the second findings chapter.  
 
As for this chapter, the following sections will present the findings of three overlapping 
emotions associated with the climate grief experience. These are (1) sadness & sense of Loss, 
(2) anger, and (3) fear. These three emotions were selected for presentation, as they were 
most frequently expressed by the participants. More complex emotions, such as Guilt and 
Anxiety are also integrated where appropriate. Considering the fluid nature of emotions, I was 
able to categorize and weave in almost all the emotions mentioned in relation to climate grief 
into the three dominant emotions presented in this chapter. Other emotions that were 
mentioned, such as joy and disgust, were acknowledged but not included.  
 
It is worth highlighting that some of the women sometimes held mixed emotions. Eleanor, for 
example, shared a photograph of a meal taken outdoors in England in November, which for 
her was both joyful and alarming. In these cases, I tried to unpick the most dominant emotion 
present, narrowing in on what was felt, rather than what the individual wanted to feel or decided 
was more acceptable. Yet this sometimes remained elusive, confirming that it is entirely 
possible to hold two opposing emotions at the same time. Others, such as Rose likened her 
emotions to the comings and goings of the tide, expressed through a photograph she took 
(figure 8). The difficult emotions were not always there, but they did return.  
 
Figure 8: Photograph, by Rose 
 
 
79 
Before we proceed, I think it’s important to highlight the depth of some of the emotions which 
were expressed during the interviews and how impactful these were on the lives of some of 
the participants. I felt that through the analysis, the gravity of some of the emotions was getting 
lost, as can sometimes occur when one moves from vocal expressions to letters on a page. 
The following passage from Sophie, who describes how her emotions around climate change 
have impacted on her personally, is a good reminder of how climate grief can saturate 
everyday living. 
“…It’s just there, it’s behind every buying decision I make, it’s behind the 
work that I do, it’s behind the clothes that I wear, the food that I eat, so it’s 
permeated into every part of my life so that it’s not, like I say it’s not 
necessarily a conscious thing, it’s just what I do and who I am, but that is 
routed in those feelings of what can I do, and I must do, what I can do […] 
and that all stems from that feeling of fear and anxiety and grief and anger...” 
(Sophie) 
To further retain the feelings of the emotions expressed during the interviews, I will start each 
section with a passage from a participant to better convey the embodiment of the emotions. 
While some participants struggled to articulate in words the depth of their emotions, others 
were able to express them using metaphors and other visually powerful analogies. Keeping in 
that trend, I removed the ages from the participants in the text, to not distract from the content. 
The emotions of sadness & sense of loss are presented first.  
 
4.1. 
Sadness & sense of loss 
 
“When I feel really terrible, the weight of it and the grief, it’s like, it’s water 
inside me, drowning me.” (Rose) 
 
In regular grief experiences, sadness (caused by the loss of a loved one) is one of the most 
common emotions (Worden 2001). In my analysis of sadness within climate grief experiences, 
key words such as “depressed”, “despair”, “sadness” and “hopeless” were the verbal cues 
used to identify this emotion. This emotion was often coupled with a sense of loss and for the 
purpose of analysis, sense of loss was categorised under the emotion of sadness. Considering 
the focus on loss in the literature (Randall, 2009; Barnett, et al., 2016), I was particularly 
curious about this specific emotion. Two thirds of all the passages categorized under sadness 
mentioned loss.    
 
80 
While sadness and loss appear to belong to the same emotional category, the relationship 
between the two are not always entirely clear. In Worden’s model (2001) the relationship is 
presumed self-explanatory, whereby the assumption is, that the death (or loss) of a loved one 
naturally brings about feelings of sadness. Within the context of climate change however, 
examining these losses are critical as they are less well understood. For example, losses may 
not be attributed to a clear single entity, could be more ambiguous and perhaps even multiple.  
 
While loss resonated with many of the women as part of their climate grief experience, 
through-out the interviews, I was aiming to understand sense of loss on a much deeper and 
more personal level. This is because initially there appeared some ambiguity between loss as 
a concept and ‘sense of loss’ as an emotion - and I was more interested in the latter. I found 
that the more personal and direct the loss, the closer I came to the emotional experience – 
and I was surprised at some of the different interpretations provided.  
 
The losses described in this section for the most part, appear to fall within the realm of non-
economic losses, which was highlighted in chapter 2.2 of the literature review as a gap in 
knowledge. Overall, the findings indicate that most women experienced multiple losses in 
relation to climate change. While losses related to the environment and future had already 
been identified in the literature review, this research identified two new categories of losses. 
The first includes a loss or shift in social networks. This was often related to a lack of 
understanding of their beliefs about climate change and their behaviours, leading to isolation. 
The loss of positive emotions such as hope and power identified in my findings, are also a 
new contribution.  
 
In addition to what I did find, I think it’s also important to briefly touch upon what I didn’t find 
and therefore chose not to expand on. While losses related to lifestyle have been suggested 
in the literature (Randall, 2009), in this study, changes that the participants had made to live 
in a more climate friendly way, such as giving up flying were not seen as a loss. Charlotte, 
who limits her flying to twice a year, reframed her lifestyles change as a ‘sacrifice’, or choice, 
rather than a loss. Similarly, Sophie sees any lifestyle change, even if it means depriving 
herself “as a win”. Nonetheless, it is plausible that most of the participants in the study had 
been involved with climate change for so long already, that environmentally conscious living 
was simply part of life. 
 
The following section is divided into four subsections. Figure 9 below, provides a guidance 
summary of the subsections. The first subsection will discuss losses related to the 
environment and what this signifies to the participants. This will be followed by future losses, 
 
81 
which tended to overlap with losses related to future generations including children. The third 
subsection will delve deeper into the losses related to personal relationships, followed by a 
final subsection on the loss of hope and power. Where useful I have contextualised the findings 
with life story details to further contribute to our understanding of these losses.  
 
Figure 9: Summary map: Sadness & sense of loss in climate grief experiences 
 
 
 
4.1.1. Environmental losses 
Climate change is generally perceived as an environmental problem with environmental 
impacts. Thus, I expected that the women would talk about the natural environment during the 
interview sessions. Overall, they observed that the natural environment was changing around 
them and during the photo elicitation exercise many presented images of their gardens, trees, 
places and landscapes which had significant meaning for them. Many of the women indicated 
that they experienced a strong connection to nature and the natural environment. However, 
when talking about their emotions around sadness, and loss in particular, only half of the 
women explicitly mentioned the natural environment. Thus, although the environment is 
regularly mentioned in the literature around loss and climate change (Cunsolo and Ellis, 2018)  
and does play a role in the climate grief experience, it did not appear as central a loss as 
 
82 
anticipated. Although climate change can lead to physical losses in the natural environment, 
this didn’t necessarily arouse an emotional sense of loss or sadness in all the participants.  
 
Overall, there appeared a layering of environmental losses, which loosely group into two 
overlapping categories: tangible and non-tangible losses. Here, tangible losses refer to losses 
which are directly observable and distinct, while non-tangible losses were more abstract, and 
sometimes signify a meaning or attachment to a tangible loss. In most instances, the 
environmental losses mentioned were tangible and concerned the decline of species and 
biodiversity, as well as the degradation of habitats, places, and natural landscapes. For a few 
participants however, environmental decline also inferred intangible losses such as beauty, 
knowledge, and predictability. I will now discuss each in turn. 
 
Tangible environmental losses 
All the participants were deeply knowledgeable and aware of the environmental impacts of 
climate change and mentioned several different kinds of interconnected environmental losses. 
However, there was a difference between the losses which they were personally witnessing 
and losses which they had learned about through information. Although the latter sometimes 
appeared less emotive, a recent study (Leviston, et al., 2023) examining the experience of 
solastalgia showed that direct experience of environmental loss was not always a prerequisite 
to feel emotionally moved by a loss.  
 
One of the oldest participants, Audrey is an environmental educator and is very knowledgeable 
about the impacts of climate change. When asking her what she felt most sad about in relation 
to climate change, she said:  
“The animals that are becoming extinct, I think elephants, the usual things 
that tug at your heartstrings, the whales, the elephants, the polar bears, that 
kind of thing…” (Audrey) 
Although she cares deeply for the environment, having spent most of her life dedicated to 
educating the next generation on climate change, her repertoire response of ‘the usual things’ 
indicates some emotional distance or perhaps fatigue about the subject. Her overall account 
is layered with cynicism. As she enters her retirement, she is deeply disappointed by the lack 
of progress she has been able to make in keeping environmental education a priority in 
schools. This is likely a factor in the emotional distance she displays, as she later conveys a 
more deeply personal loss that is part of her climate grief experience.  
 
83 
In another example, Margaret who recently gave up a corporate career to become more 
involved in the climate movement also provides a more fact-based response. When I first 
asked Margaret if she remembered a moment in her life when she felt a sense of loss around 
climate change, she responded by saying:  
“Reading things about like the Great Barrier Reef and the sort of mass 
bleaching events and you think it seemed like it’s gone for good and then 
you get the Australian government trying to not talk about it because they 
don’t wanna put a dent in the tourism. But then it seemed like when the 
temperature of the ocean went down, for whatever reason, they did come 
back to life. But I felt like that was a huge loss, not ‘cause I wanna go there, 
but just, you know, you think of the life that lives in something like that.” 
(Margaret) 
Like Audrey, Margaret spoke about environmental losses which were learned though 
information and were taking place outside of the United Kingdom.   
 
In other cases, participants drew connections between losses learned through information, 
occurring in other places and losses occurring within the United Kingdom. When talking about 
climate change losses, Rose associates this with the loss of natural landscapes in the United 
Kingdom, not due to climate change but to the development of the highspeed rail, HS2. Like 
climate change, the highspeed rail is a contested issue, as it affects the natural landscape, 
replacing natural habitats and threatening biodiversity. She then relates this back to other 
natural landscapes that are being threatened by climate change, which she had seen on 
television.  
“And then there are places in the world that I’ve seen that are outstanding, 
and if you look on the television sometimes you see landscapes which are 
outstanding and you couldn’t buy those. If you were there, that would be the 
most beautiful place that you could be in, and yet they are also under threat. 
If they’re not under threat of being built on, they are under threat from 
flooding or drought or something like that. So … it’s the loss of those places 
as well, even though I can’t go to them, most of them, knowing that they are 
there is a very amazing thing.” (Rose) 
Although one can be moved by environmental losses learned through information, these types 
of emotional experiences appeared slightly less personal.  
 
 
84 
Other participants however, provided direct experiences of environmental losses. Their 
interpretation of loss was centred around the different plants and the specific animals they 
noticed were disappearing from their gardens, such as daddy long legs, birds, and hedgehogs. 
An example from Eleanor below mentions what in her day-to-day life makes her feel sad about 
climate change. 
“Yeah, I mean the fact that the garden birds are so much fewer than they 
were, that reminds me every day. When I was a child, we used to have a 
much bigger variety of garden birds. The insects are fewer….” (Eleanor) 
In this case, Eleanor draws on her childhood memories to observe and interpret the changes 
in the natural environment more closely. This type of personal awareness of environmental 
loss is also shared by Sophie. Working mainly outdoors she also shares direct accounts of the 
environmental changes that are taking place.  
“Also, when I’m <pause> like when I’m spending time in the woods for work, 
and a few years ago I would have seen lots of bees flying around and 
butterflies, and within a few years the difference is just stark. Things like the 
number of insects and the amount of birdsong and things like that. I think 
when you are connected and aware of the natural world, you can’t help but 
notice those things. You can’t help but … it’s in your face, it’s just in your 
face. And yeah, and so that’s a loss, that’s a big loss”. (Sophie) 
In both cases, the participants are experiencing the impacts of climate change in a more 
upfront and personal way as they notice wildlife disappearing from their direct environments. 
While Eleanor observes an environmental loss between a childhood past and the present 
moment, Sophie’s time perception of environmental decline appears much starker.  
 
Although Sophie did not perceive environmental loss to directly threaten her livelihood, this 
was mentioned by Julie, who also spends a significant amount of time working outdoors. She 
explains:  
“I’m losing? <Pause> I’m not sure again if it’s quite what you meant, but I’m 
losing summer, so most of the summer now is way too hot for me. So I mean 
I have got a health issue as well that possibly has got to do with it, but in the 
summer I will wake up really early, as soon as it’s light, ‘cause that’s cool 
enough to be outside and doing stuff, but once it gets to about 11, from 11 
till 3 or something I certainly can’t work. So, it’s quite limiting […] So that is 
a loss. So, I’m losing a lot of the time that I would always have been outside 
planting and working and whatever.” (Julie) 
 
85 
In this example, Julie provides a direct example of how climate change can lead to a tangible 
loss to her livelihood. As a horticulturalist by trade, this was the only case whereby a loss of 
livelihood was mentioned. In addition, she spoke about what could be considered as a 
cascading loss, whereby one loss could trigger other losses (see section 2.3 of the literature 
review). Later in her interview, Julie mentions that her inability to be outside during the day for 
long periods of time in the summer could also impact her health, through a lack of vitamin D. 
Health-related impacts were also suggested by May. She explained that the loss of 
environmental knowledge, a more intangible loss, could negatively affect health outcomes due 
to the role that the environment plays in addressing health issues.  
 
Although the participants engaged with climate change and its environmental losses to 
different degrees, when talking about tangible environmental losses, the difference between 
knowing about the losses through information, or witnessing them personally appeared a key 
differential. Although it appeared that the direct experience of loss was more emotive and 
‘real’, the data suggests that sense of loss is can also be experienced through information 
alone.  
 
Nontangible environmental losses 
Tangible losses related to the environment tended to overlap with nontangible losses. A 
handful of participants who felt a sense of loss in relation to the environment, illustrated what 
this type of loss means to them on a more abstract level. These types of losses were more 
challenging for participants to articulate, potentially as they reflected more deeply personal or 
socially held values.  
 
May, who comes from a long lineage of family farming feels that there is more to environmental 
loss beyond the physical decline and disappearance of wildlife. She feels a deep sense of 
despair around the loss of species and connects this to intangible concepts of both beauty 
and knowledge that will never be known. When asking her to unpack her feelings around loss, 
she responds:  
 “I suppose every generation thinks that they know an awful lot, but how do 
you know how much more there is to understand? And we’re only just 
starting to understand fungi and their relationship with trees and how trees 
seem to converse with each other, and I just think the loss, I just think saying 
goodbye to species at the moment, we’re just saying goodbye to them, 
they’re finishing, they’re going, and we’re never going to have known what 
they did contribute or what they could have contributed...” (May) 
 
86 
Having a deep respect for the natural environment, May’s sense of loss described in this 
passage appears to concern a missed opportunity for what could have been learned about 
the natural environment. Here nature is being personified, as if she was perhaps saying 
goodbye to a friend she never met. Environmental losses also hold a deeper meaning for 
Sarah. Like May, Sarah is sensitive to the impacts of climate change and connects this to what 
she is personally witnessing in her own back garden. In her case, the sadness of the loss 
around declining diversity in her backyard is compounded by the notion that there are many 
losses occurring that we aren’t aware of. She says:  
“So, when I hear about biodiversity loss and then I apply it to my own back 
garden, that just feels like loss. And even sadder that we might not even 
notice what we’re losing.” (Sarah) 
Lack of awareness is a common source of frustration for many of the women, as we will see 
in the section on ‘Anger’, yet this underlying theme is also present in Sarah’s feelings around 
loss. Thus, the sadness felt here is potentially interwoven with feelings of pity towards society. 
Nonetheless, the oblivion to what is being lost, is for Sarah a key source of her sadness. Other 
examples which illustrate a more deeply layered meaning behind environmental losses is 
shared by Sophie. She says:   
“And so yeah, so you’re losing the … predictability and the stability of how 
things should be and how the seasons should work and how nature should 
work, and yeah, and I feel that very personally…” (Sophie) 
For some participants like Sophie, the changing climate signifies a physically real, but 
potentially metaphorical loss of predictability and stability. Although most participants did not 
interpret nor articulate this as clearly as Sophie, the uncertainty which climate change 
represents could potentially lie at the root of the climate grief experience for some women. 
This need for certainty does appear to be a common thread throughout this research, and 
perhaps for some of the women, changes in the natural environment threaten that attachment, 
resulting in sadness and fear. For others, like Rose the disruption of the natural rhythm of the 
natural world could have long term psychological implications as well. Although it was beyond 
the scope of this research to examine these, it is plausible that too much future uncertainty, 
could lead to lack of confidence in future planning. This may especially apply to younger 
women, who still have many life decisions to make, as what can be observed in the youngest 
participant, Eva, discussed in section 4.2.2. 
 
To sum up the findings with regards to environmental losses, I observed that although all 
participants were knowledgeable about the association between climate change and 
 
87 
environmental decline and were aware that the environment was changing, not all expressed 
a sense of loss or sadness. For those who did, environmental losses sometimes had a much 
more layered meaning beyond the physical losses they were witnessing, such as loss of 
beauty, knowledge, and certainty. Overall, however, environmental losses did not appear to 
play the most prominent role for most participants in the climate grief experience.  
 
While environmental decline plays a central role in many studies on ecological grief (Boafo 
and Yeboah, 2024; Markkula, et al., 2024), the lack of poignancy in this study could potentially 
be explained by a few reasons. Firstly, none of the respondents were directly dependent on 
the natural environment for their livelihoods, apart from one participant. In some cases, 
environmental losses were perceived mostly through information, and thus they were not 
directly confronted with it on a day-to-day basis. Second, considering the high climate literacy 
of the participants, it is plausible that for some, environmental losses were so assumed that 
there was no strong need for them to be distinctly acknowledged. Third, a sense of loss in the 
climate grief experience simply wasn’t centred directly around the environment, but other more 
intangible concepts – such as their imagined futures. These are described next.  
 
4.1.2. Future losses  
Although climate change is currently unfolding, it is still sometimes perceived as a tomorrow 
phenomenon with future consequences. For the women participating in this study, sense of 
loss was related to the future, often associated with future generations and their children or 
grandchildren. As women are the “ones that bring things into being”, according to one 
participant (Eleanor), future losses appeared to play a prominent part of the climate grief 
experience for most of the women. Understandably, life phase was indicative of the 
perceptions held, and thus the following sections are presented accordingly.  
 
Overall, women with children expressed a much stronger sense of loss coupled with fear for 
their children’s futures, whereby the loss concerned that of a ‘good future’ and a loss of 
meaningful experiences. For the women still deciding to have children, there was also a 
shared sense of loss, but for the youngest, this appeared more centred around an accelerated 
decision-making process. For the oldest participants, with (potential) grandchildren, losses 
concerned the role of grand-motherhood and a future vision they had for their children. In 
these women, however, I observed a tendency to devalue their own emotions, with some 
struggling to balance their morality with their personal desires.  
 
88 
Women with children living at home 
Overall, two of the three women who still had children living at home were fearful and felt a 
sense of loss for their children’s futures. Sophie who has two teenage children, was the most 
explicit about her sense of future loss, she says:  
“I think one is the loss of the future, if that makes sense. Just that … what it 
could have been and what it still could be, if we get our act together, but I 
just don’t think that’s going to happen. So yeah, just definitely that loss of 
what could have been.” (Sophie) 
Although sounding somewhat hopeful, what Sophie alludes to here is that a “good” future is 
likely no longer attainable, which generates a sense of unease. This is confounded by the fact 
that she has children, who will be experiencing that future for themselves. Sarah who has four 
young boys at home also shares in this sense of loss of future for her children. 
 
She formulates this through one of her own childhood memories of snorkelling. A sense of 
loss starts with an environmental loss, that then cascades into a sense of loss for her own 
children who may not be able to have the same experiences of the natural environment that 
are so valuable to her.  
“[…] I just remember going out on the boat and then we put our snorkels on 
and we put our … faces into the water, and I had to sit up again. […] I had 
to take my face out again because I was just so shocked at the vibrancy of 
this underwater world. It was incredible, just the depth of the reef and the life 
on it and all the colours. It was really vibrant … and almost like overwhelming 
so I had to come up and take a minute and then went back down, and I’ve 
never forgotten that. […] And so one of my moments of real climate grief 
actually was around the fact that my sons might not be able to do that, so 
that was a really significant … memory for me.” (Sarah) 
However, the sense of loss she feels for her children, was also about a loss of innocence.  
During the photo elicitation exercise, Sarah shares a photograph of a small group of 9-year-
old girls who are making banners for an upcoming climate change protest. Sarah shares that 
she feels a great sense of sadness and responsibility towards them. While questioning her 
further on this she says:  
“Yes, and the innocence or that space of childhood that should be without 
worry…. It’s a loss of that innocence and that worry-free childhood that you 
want children to have.” (Sarah)  
 
89 
Interestingly, for Margaret, whose decision not to have children is unrelated to climate change, 
also feels a sense of loss of innocence and naivety. Talking about her interpretation on loss, 
Margaret uses the loss of flying to illustrate a deeper, more subjective loss. She says:  
“I think a certain amount of innocence and naivety is gone, you know, that 
kind of freedom to jump on a plane and go somewhere without worrying 
about, you know and, ‘Oh, yeah, let’s go and see Venice.’ I’ve never been 
to Australia, New Zealand. We’ve been to Canada three times, and I think 
I’ll never go back there again ‘cause I won’t get on a plane. So, in a way, it 
sounds quite selfish but that ability to travel without… ‘cause I didn’t used to 
worry about things, it didn’t used to occur to me...” (Margaret) 
Although Margaret is older, her experience suggests that the loss of innocence and naivety 
could also be applicable to adults, and not just children. This is another example, of how 
certain losses can be multifaceted and cross-generational.  
 
For the women who did have young children, however, such as Sophie and Sarah, loss of the 
future was poignant especially regarding how their children would experience it. In both cases, 
I observed that the women also struggled with the extent to which they should engage with 
their children on the topic of climate change. Although the children appeared actively involved 
in climate change, the mothers were burdened by the additional anxieties it aroused in their 
children. While for mothers, a sense of loss was felt on behalf of their children’s future, for 
women in earlier life phases, a sense of loss for the future around children was more centred 
around their own imagined future of becoming a mother. These are presented next. 
 
Women of childbearing age 
Family planning is for most women a major life decision and the two youngest participants in 
the study illustrated the conflicted nature of the decision-making process. Although at different 
stages of their decision making, both women are reluctant about having children and 
experience a sense of loss because of their decision.   For the youngest participant, however, 
that sense of loss is compounded by the lack of choice she felt she had. Eva explains:  
“… I think I’ve pretty much decided that I don’t want to have kids because of 
how uncertain the future is looking and I wouldn’t necessarily wanna bring 
kids into[…] And so I feel that sense of that potential possibly has been taken 
away from me, even though I’ve kind of taken it away myself. I think that’s 
one of the main parts that I sometimes feel quite sad about, even though I 
 
90 
don’t necessarily know if I wanted to be a mum anyway…[…] I think that’s, 
in terms of my personal imminent life, the main one where I think that’s a 
fairly certain loss in my future.” (Eva) 
For Eva, her sense of loss is not only centred around not potentially having children, but a loss 
of choice and perhaps a sense of agency over her own decision making. Furthermore, her 
potential desire of becoming a mother had not yet fully matured, and thus in a way, her life 
choices are being limited or entirely lost, due to climate change.  
 
Jessica, who has had more time to consider motherhood, explains how climate change played 
a role in her decision to not have children. She provides multiple personal reasons why she 
doesn’t want to have children, only later coming to the realisation that the environment had a 
more significant role to play than she anticipated. She reflects:   
“…I don't see the point in producing more humans, so that's an 
environmental decision. I think that actually was quite a big part of the 
decision making than I realized actually. I feel very strongly about 
overpopulation. So if I did want to have children, it would be through children 
that had already been born, or supporting those that need a home.” (Jessica)  
Coming from a very traditional family, for Jessica, the decision to not have children weighed 
heavily on her. In her negotiation of ruling out motherhood due to climate change, she 
manages to strip away the mother role to find an alternative solution. In later accounts she 
presents other ways in which she would like to “nurture” others instead of having children.  
“…I think I just made the decision, in the last couple of years that actually 
there are so many other avenues where I can help nurture people. In a 
different way and it doesn't have to be through a child.” (Jessica) 
Although Jessica has sought alternative pathways to fulfil a motherhood type role by finding 
ways to nurture others, the fact that she has embarked on this exploration, implies that there 
was, or remains a sense of loss. For the eldest women in the study, their life phase presented 
other challenges. Their sense of future loss is described next.  
 
Women with grown-up children (and grandchildren) 
A handful of women in the study were considerably older with grown-up children, some of 
whom had children of their own. In some cases, however, their children were either limiting 
the number of children they had, with others deciding not to have any children at all due to 
environmental reasons. Although these women were outwardly accepting of their children’s 
 
91 
decisions, recognizing it was not theirs to make, two distinct but overlapping losses emerged. 
One related to the vision they had imagined for their children and the other on the role they 
would have had as grandparent. A common thread amongst all these older participants was a 
tendency to devalue their own emotions.  
 
Being part of an environmentally conscious family, May is likely to only have one grandchild. 
While she respects the decisions of her children, she does perceive this as a loss. She 
explains: 
“Well … it’s just the way it plays out, doesn’t it? But Thomas is [her son] by 
far the most intelligent in the family, I’d say he’s cleverer than his dad, and 
so he believes that it’s the right thing not to bring more children into the world 
for whatever reason. All you can do is respect that. But it’s a much more 
powerful thought than I would have had really. From the point of having 
children, I would have been quite happy to have as many as you could, 
‘cause I love children and it’s all lovely and you want a family. You know, 
mother hen situation. But you realise that you can’t do that for the planet, 
you can’t do it for … you just can’t do it. It’s like a dream, isn’t it? It’s like a 
fairyland dream.” (May).  
Her reference to it being a dream, demonstrates the loss of an imagined future she had for 
her family and for herself, as being a ‘mother hen’. It is not a choice she would have made for 
herself. This is shared by Rose who has a couple of grandchildren. She is relieved that she 
didn’t know about climate change before she had her own children, because she really desired 
them and probably would not have had them.  
 
Eleanor, who doesn’t have any grandchildren due to environmental reasons, similarly, 
understands her children’s positions, but appears conflicted. She explains that she has no 
wish for grandchildren, although she would “normally”, and would be delighted if she did, but 
also understands why it hasn’t happened. I believe her true response was revealed after I 
asked her if she felt that she would be missing out if she didn’t have any grandchildren. She 
says:  
“ <Pause> I don’t actually feel I would miss out that much, because I feel … 
you know, I’m busy, I’m active, although funnily enough, it’s really 
interesting, every time I go anywhere now, when my girlfriends and I go out 
for a meal or whatever, there’s a table full of young women with babies – I 
get, <Gasps> ‘Oh wow, this is ama…’ I always go up to them, I always go, 
 
92 
‘Oh, it’s so lovely to see you all.’ <Laughs> That doesn’t translate into a wish 
to have my own. Just love ‘em and … As somebody said to me yesterday, 
funnily enough, she said, ‘It’ll change the first time you hold one of them.’ 
<Chuckles> I’m sure that’s true.” (Eleanor) 
Even though Eleanor is accepting of the life she currently has, her account comes across as 
hopeful to maybe one day become a grandmother still. For most of the older participants, there 
is a clear understanding as to why their children are not having children, and this is perceived 
as a loss on behalf of their children. For some, this is also perceived as a loss for themselves, 
although this was often subdued. They prioritized their morality, perceiving it is ‘right,’ to have 
(m)any children.  
 
Family planning and becoming a (grand) mother is for most women an important life decision 
and considering the uncertain future which climate change can bring, it was thus 
understandable that this would play a role in the climate grief experience. Loss of future was 
shared by most of the women participating in this study and was experienced through the lens 
of their (grand) children. Nuances were dictated by life phase, with women with younger 
children still living in the family home, expressing that sense of loss most acutely. Considering 
that this type of loss was shared by most women in the study suggests that climate grief 
encompasses other factors which are not related to environmental decline. This becomes 
even more apparent when considering the relational losses which were mentioned in women’s 
experiences. These types of losses will be discussed next.  
 
4.1.3. Social relationship losses and shifts 
One of the unexpected losses mentioned in relation to the climate grief experience is one that 
relates to social relationships. By social relationships I refer to any personal relationships that 
the participants have, either with family, friends, co-workers, or any others. The findings 
demonstrate that most participants had undergone or were currently experiencing a loss or 
shift in their personal relationships. This type of loss was not always identified through a direct 
line of questioning around their sense of loss, but rather as a more holistic observation of their 
accounts. Many participants spoke about a lack of support and understanding from family 
members and others as part of their climate grief experience. And while it wasn’t always their 
grief that put a strain on their social relationships, it was sometimes simply their emotional or 
behavioural expression of care for climate change that wasn’t received in a positive way.  
 
Overall, I got the sense that many of the participants felt quite isolated by their emotions, as a 
result of not always being able to share openly how they felt with others close to them. As 
 
93 
each of the women’s experiences are unique, it was challenging to divide this section into 
equal parts, however, the following structure has been made for ease of reading. The first 
subsection describes the loss of connection that some women experience in relation to 
younger family members and the second subsection focuses on spousal and familial conflicts. 
Finally, this section closes with some experiences shared by participants relating to the loss 
of respect and credibility in the eyes of others.  
  
Loss of connection with younger family members  
Many of the women participating in the study were older, either having children or 
grandchildren. For most of these women this implied having a guiding influence and role in 
younger family members’ lives. This took the form of sharing with and educating their 
youngsters on climate change. Rose for example, sees her job to prepare and educate her 
grandchildren on environmental issues, as means to protect them. She says:  
“…I think the best thing is for them to understand and connect with the 
natural world and have a pleasure in that because even if modern 
infrastructure has collapsed, the natural world will still be there and if we 
know it and understand it, it will sort of protect us in some way.” (Rose) 
In Rose’s case, and a few others, grandchildren and children appeared receptive to their 
(grand)mother’s passions and actively participated in protests and other climate events 
together. For some participants however, this wasn’t the case, and it appeared as a sore point 
of contention, potentially aggravating the grief experience.   
 
Audrey, the eldest participant, interpreted my question around a sense of loss quite differently 
to the rest, as a loss she was experiencing within her own family. During the interview she 
spoke at length about her son who lives in Dubai and about her disapproval of his own family’s 
high consumption lifestyle. She shared a photograph (figure 10) of a tower block with 
fireworks, which was sent to her by one of her grandchildren.  
 
 
 
 
 
94 
Figure 10: Photograph, by Audrey 
 
 
She feels that through their different lifestyle, she has somehow lost them, particularly her 
granddaughters. Her account below has been summarized for ease of reading: 
“… I’ve got two granddaughters there [Dubai] and they’re the only 
granddaughters I’ve got; all the rest are grandsons, and in a way I feel I’ve 
kind of lost them, because I don’t have much of a connection. Because I 
don’t live for consumption as they live for consumption….And there is a loss 
because I can’t really relate to them, and if I talk to them about it, they glaze 
over.” (Audrey) 
For Audrey, whose entire professional and personal life revolves around educating others 
about the natural environment, not being able to share her experiences with her 
granddaughters creates a deep sense of loss. While her grandsons are more receptive, the 
inability to share her knowledge with her only female lineage is particularly difficult. This is 
intensified by the fact that coming into her retirement, Audrey feels that she hasn’t been able 
to make any positive change in the environmental education sector. Leaving her legacy with 
her own grandchildren is perhaps a last hope for her.  
 
 
95 
While Audrey struggles to connect with her granddaughters, Julie faces similar experiences 
with her own children. Although they were both environmentally active when they were 
younger, they now have no interest, especially her daughter. When asking Julie about her 
feelings of loss around climate change, she feels that she has lost her children. She says:  
“Hmm <pause> well, probably not as you’re meaning, but just loss of … the 
son who was campaigning for rainforests, for years, where all his friends 
thought he was mad because they didn’t even know what a rainforest was 
at primary school. So the loss of … compassion I suppose and interest, from 
both my kids, ‘cause Natasha [not her real name], was into eco stuff as well, 
when she was younger. And I’m really worried about that if that is a general 
theme….” (Julie) 
What’s interesting about her response, is that although she implies that it’s not what I’m after, 
potentially referring to environmental losses, her interpretation, unknowing to her is shared by 
others. The desire to share her knowledge, especially with her children around climate change 
is important to her and the non-receptiveness of her children worries her. Her children’s 
apathetic disposition is a great source of frustration and a source of conflict in the household. 
To further illustrate the situation in her household, during the photo elicitation interview, Julie 
shares an image of her teenage daughter lying in bed and on her phone, half glancing up to 
give her an angry look.  
 
Yet the loss of connection with her children also appears to extend beyond the four walls of 
home. Julie reveals that her social network is small, and she struggles to find commonalities 
on the topic of climate change with those close to her, including her close friend and brother-
in-law. As a response, Julie is actively involved in a few climate groups, confident that she will 
“find her tribe”.  
 
In both cases, a loss or shift is taking place in their relations with younger family members. 
The perceived lack of care and understanding of their disposition and climate emotions is 
difficult for the participants to bare. Underneath appears a desire to pass down and share their 
knowledge with the next generation. The loss of connection with the younger female 
generation appears particularly sensitive for both participants. Despite her frustrations, Julie 
raised concerns about her children’s emotional capacity to deal with climate change and felt 
the need to protect them. The pressure on future generations is highlighted quite clearly here. 
While no children participated in this study, Eva’s case illustrates how younger generations 
can feel very overburdened by the climate crisis and even betrayed by older generations.  
 
96 
Overall, these two examples illustrate how climate grief can create temporary or permanent 
rifts within families between older and younger generations.  
 
Inter-family issues (in-laws)  
While conflicts within families and with in-laws can be a regular part of life, this topic was a 
reoccurring pattern for some participants when talking about their climate grief experience. 
Most participants in relationships mentioned that their partners were supportive of their 
emotional experience, with only minor conflicts erupting over requests to adopt sustainable 
lifestyle behaviours. However, in some cases it was apparent that significant conflicts had 
taken place and in-laws could be less supportive. And although these disputes appeared 
relatively minor in longevity and resolvable sometimes even being met with humour, they do 
contribute to our understanding of the climate grief experience and how isolating it can feel.  
Here I will present two examples of Rose and Margaret, who both have in-laws whose high 
consumption lifestyle they disapprove of. Their accounts, however, greatly contrast in their 
emotional intensity. Different in age and life phase, Rose has been involved with the climate 
movement for a very long time, while Margaret is relatively new. This difference appears to be 
a factor in how they manage confrontations about their climate grief.  
 
After leaving a corporate career at large tech corporation, Margaret became very passionate 
about climate change. Her partner was very supportive during a deeply stressful time when 
she felt the gravity of her climate emotions, hitting her “like a Tsunami”. The lifestyle changes 
which Margaret wanted to incorporate into their lives as well as the intensity of her emotions 
created a rift with her partner. The emotional lows became so desperate that her partner 
advised her to go back to work, and they had discussions about ending the relationship all 
together. She said to him:     
“This is who I am and if you don’t like it then that’ll be the end of it’ So that’s 
the choice. It’s like you kind of have to get on board with it and I’ll try and 
understand things from your point of view, but I’m not gonna tell you that I’m 
gonna go back into working in IT and not talking about climate change ‘cause 
it’s what matters to me.” (Margaret) 
Margaret’s conflict with her partner about her climate emotions and the changes she is making 
illustrate how this can impact on the women’s relationships with others. While Margaret didn’t 
lose the relationship with her partner, she was prepared to, because of the depth of her 
emotions. The conflict that she experienced with her partner, also extended to her in-laws. In 
the early part of her climate grief experience Margaret felt quite panicked: “Oh my god, 
 
97 
everybody needs to know about this, everybody needs to be doing something about it!”. Her 
emotions were not at all supported by her in-laws who responded quite callously, by telling her 
that they didn’t want to know about it and didn’t care, because they didn’t have any 
grandchildren. She recalls how after an evening out together, she brings up climate change in 
conversation and is immediately shut down: 
“… but I was saying something about climate change and it was probably 
about ten o’clock at night and she just said, ‘Oh, I don’t wanna hear this!’ 
And she just went to bed. And, I mean that’s not the way she generally acts, 
so that made me think, well, it’s like I’m being silenced and I think when 
you’re grieving something when you’re upset about something it’s nice if 
your family and friends hear you out and have a conversation about it…” 
(Margaret) 
While Margaret’s case was most clear and severe in terms of the backlash she experienced 
with regards to her climate emotions, this passage is somewhat exemplary of many 
participants who lack social support for how they feel about climate change. In reality, the 
situation described above reveals more about her sister in-laws’ position than it does about 
Margaret’s, illustrating that climate change remains a highly sensitive topic within certain 
settings. This makes it increasingly difficult for those suffering from climate grief to express 
how they feel.  
 
Although Rose is over a decade older than Margaret, her experience with her in-laws is 
different but similar. Rose has been involved with the climate movement for a very long time 
and has experienced very deep and heavy emotions in relation to climate change. Like 
Margaret, Rose’s partner struggled with some of the lifestyle changes they had to make 
because of her emotions. He has now come to the same realisation as her, “that we’re pretty 
doomed”: something she’s been telling him for years.  
 
While Rose’s partner is accepting of her - what she describes as “eccentric” behaviours - she 
shares a couple of stories illustrating how her in-laws perceive her. In response to climate 
change, Rose adopted several lifestyle behaviours which are often under scrutiny of her in-
laws. Having a habit of taking home leftovers from restaurants to avoid waste, she describes 
how on one occasion, she missed a dinner to which her brother-in-law mockingly responds: 
“Oh, never mind. Rose missed out on taking the cheese home.” (Rose). While the story is 
followed by a small chuckle from Rose, she lets on that her husband still sometimes feels 
embarrassed about her behaviours and that she doesn’t have social circle to share her quirks 
and emotions with.  
 
98 
While Rose’s example is much less heated than Margaret’s, the repetitive nature of the stories 
she shares throughout her entire account, suggests that this feeling of being different is a 
common theme in her life. As Rose has made her lifestyle changes a long time ago already, it 
is likely that she has learned how to deal with the reactions she receives from others of her 
behaviours, practicing them privately or using humour as way to waive off negative or 
condescending reactions. She says:  
“If you’re always constantly restrained by social norms, I just think life must 
be so dull, you know? ‘cause I’m not doing anything wrong. I’m not doing 
anything damaging or destroying anything. It’s just I’m strange and people 
find it difficult. But that’s their problem really, isn’t it? <Laughs>” (Rose) 
While lifestyle changes don’t need to be part of the climate grief experience, I think they are 
important to mention here, because they often occur because of strong climate emotions. They 
signify that the individual is making behavioural adaptations to their life that corresponds with 
a change in their emotions and perspectives. For example, Jessica also mentioned that she 
had made several low carbon lifestyle changes when she felt very low about climate change, 
but became overly obsessive about trying to fit them into her life when she struggled to make 
it work. Although Jessica did not appear to receive any direct social repercussions regarding 
her choices, by finding individuals who supported her life choices, Margaret and Rose did.  
 
Compared to Margaret, Rose appears further along the path of lifestyle changes as part of her 
climate grief experience, and it is likely that her social network has somewhat adapted to her 
new disposition. Both cases, however, illustrate how the climate grief experience impacts on 
close relationships, especially with partners, and how they can be put in jeopardy because of 
the strong emotions involved. Although not identified in this research, in worst case scenarios, 
climate grief could potentially lead to a permanent loss of certain social relationships with those 
who do not understand nor have the capacity to carry the intensity of their emotional 
experience. The final section provides further illustration, as to how climate grief experiences 
can lead to a sense of isolation based on external reactions to their experience in the case of 
work colleagues and other social settings, particularly relating to feelings of ridicule.  
 
Ridicule and loss of respect  
All women participating in the research have held regular jobs at one or multiple points in their 
lives, with some women dedicating most of their professional career to educating or raising 
awareness about environmental issues. Two participants who stand out are, Audrey and 
Sophie both of whom have worked in the educational system. Audrey is retired, and Sophie 
 
99 
has started delivering outdoor learning lessons on a freelance basis. Both cases highlight the 
difficulties they experienced working within the school system on climate and environmental 
issues, illustrating the lack of space for addressing climate emotions and how expressing their 
emotions can put at risk their respect and credibility in the eyes of others.  
 
Sophie has been involved with climate related issues ever since she was a teenager and her 
emotions have played an important role in her career choices, leading her to take 
environmentally focused roles within the educational sector. Although Sophie did feel that she 
was able to drive her message across on certain topics, she was labelled as an ‘eco warrior’ 
which she felt was patronizing and put a strain on her work relationships. People would say to 
her “Oh that’s Sophie on her soapbox”, making the workplace at times somewhat unpleasant. 
Finding a place to express climate emotions beyond the four walls of home remains 
challenging. When asking her about this in more detail, she says:  
“…I don’t … it’s difficult, because <pause> I think people, if you talk about 
these things to people, people feel that you’re being over dramatic or that 
you’re being silly or too sensitive or whatever.” (Sophie) 
A lack of space to express emotions and feelings of ridicule because of their climate disposition 
and related emotions and behaviours was unfortunately quite common for most of the 
participants. Although not directly related to her own personal experience, May describes how 
her husband was ridiculed for wanting to install heat pumps at their local church. 
 
Audrey who has come to the end of her career working in environmental education, is 
unfortunately a good illustration of the emotional burnout one can experience working on 
environmental issues. While it would be impossible to unravel her climate emotions from her 
experiences in the school system, when I ask her how her emotions have impacted on her 
relationships to others, she says: “Well a person of ridicule to some extent, you know, ‘Why 
would you do this, why would you not do that? Why do you believe that you can have any 
influence?” (Audrey). Audrey is emotionally drained and questions whether all the effort she 
put in had made any difference at all. Attending protests make her feel quite powerful, albeit 
only temporarily. She says:   
“I mean it’s stupid to feel that you’re making a difference, but it just kind of 
satisfies that need to have your voice heard, even though nobody’s listening 
to you really. If that makes sense.” (Audrey) 
For most of the women, their climate change message seems to be falling on deaf ears which 
makes their experience incredibly isolating. Caring about climate change becomes a difficult 
 
100 
burden to carry and its emotional load is made increasingly difficult by the lack of support both 
at home, in personal relationships as well as in the workplace and other social settings. From 
the interviews I got the sense that the women had experienced deep periods of loneliness and 
isolation. Although this is perhaps not a conventional sort of loss in the way that the literature 
refers to it, the findings do illustrate that a sense of loss can extend into personal and social 
relationships as well. Sometimes these losses appear a bit more marginal and temporary as 
personal relationships need to be re-adjusted and recalibrated according to the changing 
emotions, views, and behaviours of the women. However, in other cases the loss appears 
rather abrupt. Whatever the circumstance, the experience of climate grief appeared to have a 
significant impact on the ways in which the women are able to relate to others and the world 
around them. Thus, for some women, climate change also signified a loss of positive emotions, 
such as hope and power, which are described next.  
 
4.1.4. Loss of hope and power  
 
“We all need that [hope], don’t we, […] otherwise we’d all just give up.” 
(Sarah)  
While a sense of loss as part of the climate grief experience was more commonly attributed to 
the environment, the future, or social relationships, half of the participants explicitly expressed 
a loss of hope and power. By understanding feelings of hopelessness and powerlessness as 
a loss of positive emotions, a much broader understanding of the climate grief experience can 
be realised. Grief experiences typically focus on the presence of negative emotions, such as 
sadness and anger (Kubler-Ross, 1970; Worden, 2001), but not the absence of positive 
emotions.   
 
The loss of hope and power often appeared to coincide, and they were by far the most difficult 
emotional expressions for me to absorb. I elaborate more on this when I evaluate my methods 
in chapter 5. Difficult emotions such as anger and sadness, which were coupled with a sense 
of hope sustained the emotional equilibrium for some of the women. However, when difficult 
emotions were coupled with a loss of hope or power, a negative spiral was often imminent 
during the interviews. Overall, I felt that hope and power functioned as emotional buoys, 
keeping some of the women afloat amidst a barrage of difficult emotions.  
 
Within the climate grief experience, the loss of hope and power was largely driven by 
comparative associations between the participants and the forces of nature and the reliance 
 
101 
on larger actors to mitigate climate change. While some women initially experienced relief of 
their difficult emotions whilst engaging in climate friendly behaviours, their reliance on the 
collective to have a significant impact often led to a loss of hope. Many of the women engaged 
in protests to regain their sense of hope and power through collective expression, but this also 
appeared short-lived.    
 
Loss of hope and power: Feeling small 
For the women who expressed a loss of hope or power, this tended to be associated with 
comparative statements about themselves in relation to the larger complexities surrounding 
climate change, the force of nature and those on who they relied for mitigation actions, such 
as the government. Overall, feelings of powerlessness within the context of climate grief, 
meant feeling small, unimportant and a perceived inability to have an impact.  
 
Climate change can cause severe weather and natural disasters and those who experienced 
this on a more personal level were confronted with feelings of powerlessness in the wake of 
such an event. Margaret who recently moved to the countryside with her partner and 
experienced flooding, explains:  
“We had a few flash floods here, which is very unusual, and you can’t do 
anything about it, not in the instant that it happens. You can try and shore 
up your home so that maybe it isn’t as vulnerable, but unless you rebuilt it 
on stilts <laughs> water’s gonna come in. I think it makes you feel powerless 
and I think in modern life we’re not really used to that.” (Margaret) 
Her reference to modern life is particularly interesting as in her experience, climate change 
signifies a shift in perception. Perhaps alluding to a rebalancing of power in the order of things, 
between people and the environment. Her sense of powerlessness is felt in relation to the 
natural world which is becoming more unpredictable. This was also a common experience for 
Sarah, who upon noticing the change in water levels in her hometown, felt “a sense of 
powerlessness”.  
 
While some women felt a loss of power in association with nature, others felt this in relation to 
the wider systems and institutions that were perceived to be responsible for climate change, 
or on who they relied for mitigating climate change, such as the government or big industries. 
Jessica, for example, feels a loss of power in relation to the wider systems of capitalism, the 
fossil fuel industry, and the overall functioning of society. She says:  
 
102 
“And I think I feel, feel like it’s such a big thing that it’s very, I feel very…. I 
feel very miniscule and powerless to be able to make the change that I feel 
like I could have the potential to make…” (Jessica) 
For other women, the loss of hope became imminent through the realisation that climate 
change is a collective problem, which requires the participation of others. The women are 
reliant on others who have more power and influence than they have and there are limits to 
what they can achieve on an individual level. This realisation is then coupled with a sense of 
hopelessness when they perceive other actors to be disengaged with the problem. Margaret 
who has recently started coaching others to live more climate friendly lives says:  
“I do feel an element of hopelessness about the fact that climate change 
affects everybody and therefore that means that everybody’s really got to be 
involved in mitigating it and that’s the whole world. That seems like a really 
tough problem...” (Margaret) 
In comparison to the experience of difficult or negative emotions, it is the loss of positive 
emotions that I felt were more detrimental. Listening to their accounts, many of the women 
were often keen to share at length all the climate positive activities they were involved in, the 
books they had read. It appeared that through these actions, the women were looking to regain 
a sense of hope, power, and agency that they had lost due to climate change. To that extent, 
some of these actions and behaviours could perhaps be seen as a coping mechanism. The 
intersection between lifestyle changes and protests and the loss of hope and power is covered 
next. 
 
Intersection between lifestyle changes and loss of hope and power
