Within the climate grief experience, some of the women were wrestling with a sense of 
hopelessness and powerlessness and these sometimes intersected with the adoption of 
climate friendly lifestyle behaviours and protests. Most of the women had already made 
significant lifestyle changes, however, it is plausible that some of the women were using those 
behaviours as an attempt to regain positive emotions of hope and power. Taking action made 
some of the women feel more empowered. However, their relief appeared temporary, when 
reminded of the complexity of the problem, leading to a loss of hope. This was the experience 
of Rose:  
“… We are trying to make different choices. So we’ve cut down to one car, 
it’s a very small car, I’m trying to use it less, use the bus more, bike more, 
walk more. We’re trying to … we had our house insulated. We’re trying to 
 
103 
make these choices that we hope make a little bit of difference and yet it just 
feels so small compared to the big things that need to change...” (Rose) 
Despite making lifestyle adjustments, this passage has a hopeless undertone, and this was a 
recurring theme for many of the participants. While many of the women, like Rose, were 
already actively involved in making lifestyle changes, oscillating between hope and despair, 
some of the women had become more involved in public protests.  
 
While engaging in protests wasn’t for everyone, those who did often perceived it as one of the 
biggest actions they could take with regards to climate change. Within the context of hope and 
power, protests had a very strong emotional charge and I felt that some of the women were 
engaging with these actions to regain some of the positive emotions that they had lost. 
Sarah, who regularly attends protests with her younger children describes her experience:  
“I often feel really powerless, and it just feels so huge and as if the people 
that can really make the significant changes are not. And so, when you’re 
just an ordinary person you feel quite powerless. I love protests because you 
come together into a collective and I always find them really hopeful. They 
make me happy inside because you feel this sense of being part of a 
movement and you’re there with lots of other people who clearly care about 
things and want to see change. It counteracts a lot of my own feelings of 
despair and hopelessness…” (Sarah) 
After participating in protests, Sarah says that she always ‘comes back buzzing’ and feels 
more powerful because her voice has been heard. However, such experiences can be fleeting. 
The youngest woman in the study, Eva, recently went on a march and feels conflicted. She 
reflects:  
“It was amazingly organised and brilliantly executed and shows the power of 
collective action and … yeah, it was an amazing thing to be a part of in many 
ways … but I think just the whole time, I just couldn’t … it just felt like we’ve 
done this before … we’ve done this … before so many times! And I think just 
feeling so frustrated that we still have to! <Laughs> And that there’s so little 
else that we feel we can do…” (Eva) 
Although Eva was generally positive about the event itself, the fact that they are continuously 
needing to do them signals to her that they are not effective. For her, going to this protest 
appeared to have the opposite effect than on Sarah, fuelling her loss of hope. Audrey, the 
oldest participant also attends protests as much as she is physically able to but is quite cynical 
about them. She says:  
 
104 
“Demos always make me feel kind of buoyed up, and I suppose again it’s 
feeling of what can you do? Well, I’ll go on a demo and that … I mean it’s 
stupid to feel that you’re making a difference, but it just kind of satisfies that 
need to have your voice heard, even though nobody’s listening to you really. 
If that makes sense.” (Audrey) 
Although the experience is not the same for each participant, engaging in climate friendly 
behaviours and participating in protests is sometimes used as a way to regain a sense of 
power, providing a sense of hope that a wider collective shares the same experience.  
 
While the climate grief experience is largely comprised of the presence of negative emotions, 
such as anger and sadness, it is important to highlight that it may also include the loss of 
positive emotions of hope and power. As previously highlighted, the loss of positive emotions 
is missing from regular grief models (Kubler-Ross, 1970; Worden, 2001), and this finding 
provides a broader understanding of the climate grief experience.  
 
4.1.5. Section summary 
Overall, this section on sadness & sense of loss has demonstrated that the women experience 
multiple losses in relation to climate change. The climate grief experience is driven by a sense 
of loss in relation to the environment, which include both tangible and intangible aspects. 
Future losses were also observed, and these were particularly poignant for most women who 
associated these with future generations and their own children and grandchildren. Novel 
contributions about the climate grief experience regarding losses were also found. These 
included the loss or shift in personal relationships, often leading to social isolation. In addition, 
the loss of positive emotions, such as hope and power which often served as emotional buoys 
amidst difficult emotions, also contributed to a much deeper understanding of the climate grief 
experience. The most prominent of these difficult emotions, of anger, is discussed next. 
 
 
 
105 
4.2. 
Anger 
 
“‘A sword shall pierce your heart.’ And that’s how I feel about it, that a sword 
is piercing the heart, mostly of women because we’re the ones that are 
creative, we’re the ones that bring things into being.” (Eleanor) 
Following sadness & sense of loss, anger was the most dominant emotion for almost all the 
women participating in the study. They expressed this verbally, using terms such as “angry”, 
“cross”, “frustrated”, “irritated” and “outraged”. According to Worden (2001), anger is frequently 
mentioned in regular grief experiences. Anger can either come from two sources “1) from a 
sense of frustration that there was nothing one could do to prevent the death; or 2) from a kind 
of regressive experience that occurs after the loss of someone” (Worden, 2001, p. 12). In 
regular grief experiences, anger that is caused because of a loss, needs to be directed towards 
the deceased to bring it to a healthy conclusion (Worden 2001). However, handled 
ineffectively, this anger is directed towards others who are then blamed for the death. The 
rationale here is that blaming others means that someone or something is responsible and so 
the loss could have been prevented.  
 
Within this research, it wasn’t possible to assess whether the anger related to climate grief 
was due to the result of a regressive experience, however, there were clear indications that 
climate grief stemmed from the first source, i.e. a sense of frustration that too little action is 
being taken to prevent climate change. In climate grief experiences, anger was centralized 
around the personal behaviours that could prevent climate change – and the inability or lack 
of willingness of others to perform those behaviours that could avert climate-related losses in 
the future. This was also observed in a large quantitative study in Norway, where anger about 
climate change primarily concerned the lack of human action to mitigate climate change 
(Gregersen et al., 2023). Hence, anger related to climate change is centred almost exclusively 
around social factors such as the beliefs and behaviours of others, making climate change a 
social issue and not just an environmental one.  
 
Yet from a psycho-analytical perspective, in my own work, I also found that anger directed 
towards others may also be disguised as a defence mechanism. In this case, climate denial 
and an unwillingness of others to adopt climate friendly behaviours can also be interpreted as 
a lack of acceptance for the women’s values, which may threaten their sense of self. Another 
critical finding is that in some cases, anger was directed inwards. According to Worden (2001), 
this also occurs in regular grief experiences and can become a maladaptive response to grief. 
While this type of emotional response appeared to be less common, the fact that this was also 
 
106 
much harder to detect suggests that defence mechanisms could be at play. In a few instances 
the women also expressed their anger on behalf of others, for example for the communities in 
the Global South. However, this was often associated with other emotions such as sadness 
and guilt.  
 
The following paragraphs further elaborate on anger as part of the climate grief experience, 
guided by the summary map depicted below in figure 11. This map is a visual representation 
of the ways in which anger is expressed within the context of the climate grief experience, 
where the categories on the right of the arrows represent who the anger is directed towards.   
 
Figure 11: Summary map: Anger in climate grief experiences 
 
4.2.1. Anger towards others   
Overall, the findings show that anger responses stemmed from the notion that climate change 
could have been prevented. By far, most of the anger occurred in social settings and was 
directed towards others, such as family members or friends, non-climate friendly objects or 
other unknown members of society. Anger was also directed towards the lack of action of 
government institutions, the Church, as well industries such as the fossil fuel and food industry, 
who were perceived to be the main contributors to climate change. Underlying this emotion, 
was a perceived ‘lack of care’ within society, despite growing awareness of climate change.  
 
107 
Friends and family 
For most of the participants, feelings of anger and frustration were directed towards other 
people in their direct social environment such as family members or friends. The women were 
angry at others who were not adopting any climate friendly behaviours or who flat out denied 
that climate change was happening. Many shared stories describing the conflicts that erupted 
with friends and families about these issues.  
 
A general lack of interest from close family members or relatives appeared a common source 
of frustration and conflict, particularly around climate denial. For example, Julie describes her 
frustrations about the mindset of one of her close friends and lack of interest of her own 
children on the issue.  
“I’m giving talks on composting and sustainable gardening and growing your 
own food and flowers and stuff, so that we’re reducing carbon footprint. And 
people listen and go home and carry on as normal. And I try to get some 
response from my kids and I don’t get that. Even my best friend is very much 
like, ‘What do you mean, climate change? There’s no such thing as climate 
change.’ So, I’m bloody frustrated!” (Julie) 
Rose describes a similar type of interaction with an older woman who she regularly visits and 
cares for. During a discussion about the changeability of weather, the older woman doesn’t 
share Rose’s concerns and responds to her by saying (in Rose’s words): “Sometimes it’s like 
this and sometimes it isn’t”. The older woman’s casual response, upsets Rose and she 
describes how she felt in that moment. 
“…And I sort of felt that … that moment of anger again, when I felt people 
are so silly. They don’t seem to recognise what’s going on. And she 
reminded me of lots of people who I have conversations with, who dismiss 
it as climate change as … oh it’ll be alright and oh it’s not that bad and oh, 
it’ll all get sorted out in the end, and are sort of burying their heads in the 
sand or denying it, and how that makes me feel, it makes … that makes me 
cross as well!” (Rose) 
In other cases, anger towards others was centred more specifically around the lack of action. 
More specifically, this included people not adopting climate friendly behaviours. For example, 
Charlotte shares her frustrations about the non-climate friendly behaviours of her direct family 
members, specifically around the use of non-climate friendly products.  
 
108 
“I think sometimes like frustration with my partner, if he’s done a shop and 
he’s come back and, you know, there’s loads of plastic packaging, whereas 
I mean I’m not saying I never buy anything in plastic packaging, that would 
be an absolute lie, but I do make quite a big effort and I will sometimes think, 
‘Oh, I fancy that but actually I’m not gonna have it because of the way it’s 
packaged’. So, for me, I prioritise not as the absolute number one thing, but 
I do make quite a big effort. So, when I, yeah, when I see all the crazy plastic 
and sometimes the way things are wrapped, yeah. So, it would be more 
around those things where it can make me feel quite upset, quite angry, just 
like why is that still happening when we all know that it’s a problem now, you 
know?...” (Charlotte)  
When talking about climate change, the women often drew associations with other 
environmental issues or practices, such as recycling or plastic packaging. The frustration here 
is derived from the fact that Charlotte is trying to make more climate friendly choices, while 
her partner is less inclined.  
 
Overall, the degree of conflict about the non-climate friendly beliefs and behaviours of family 
members and friends differed per person and situation. While some of the women joked about 
the trivialness of arguing over plastic-free dishwasher tablets and chemical free shampoos, 
others experienced a division emerging within the household. This was especially apparent 
when sharing moments of personal transformation during which the women began to make 
significant lifestyle changes, and these were met with resistance and confusion by others. 
However, these moments were not always shrouded in anger, but rather a deep sense of 
sadness & loss, discussed in section 4.2.3.   
 
According to regular grief models (Worden 2011), anger must be directed somewhere and due 
to the proximity of family members and friends, they may on occasion simply have been a 
target by default. However, I felt that in some cases it was more likely that the women had a 
desire to share and connect with their loved ones. Denying climate change and a lack of 
motivation to adopt climate friendly behaviours, may have been perceived as a lack of 
acceptance for the women’s beliefs and values. Social acceptance is important for a strong 
sense of self, and oppositional behaviour from others in social circles or close relationships 
may threaten their sense of self. In some cases, such threats may provoke a defence 
mechanism, leading to strong emotional responses such as anger.    
 
109 
While these emotional responses felt more intimate, feelings of anger also extended beyond 
immediate social circles and was sometimes directed towards non-climate friendly objectives 
and members of society in general, while engaging in regular social activities.  
 
Objects and members of society 
While some women expressed their anger directly at others, naming family members or 
friends, in some instances, the direction of anger was less personal and was more broadly 
directed at others, or society in general. In most of these instances, anger responses were 
triggered while out shopping and engaging in social activities. Many women expressed anger 
and frustration towards non-climate friendly products in shops and in some cases the unknown 
individuals that would potentially buy them. During the photo elicitation interview, several of 
the participants presented at least one photograph of an object that they came across while 
out shopping that angered them. Audrey shared at length the frustration she experienced while 
out shopping. She shares a photograph (figure 12) of her visit to the local mall. 
 
Figure 12: Photograph, by Audrey 
 
 
 
 
110 
She directs her anger towards individuals who were exhibiting behaviours that she perceived 
to be non-climate friendly and in direct opposition to her values.   
“....But it’s just like, I feel like shouting at them all and saying, ‘You can’t carry 
on like this, you can’t keep on and on, going to Primark and buying bags and 
bags of stuff that you’ll wear once and then throw away!....it just represents 
all that is bad about consumerism to me.” (Audrey)  
Rose shares a similar experience, directing her anger towards the products, and somewhat 
indirectly towards the individuals who are assumed to be purchasing them.  
“This makes me angry, when I go into shops and just see the completely, 
utterly pointless stuff that is being produced, that people buy, that it’s not 
even stuff that is useful or you need. It’s just rubbish that is being churned 
out and filling up the shops and this has been an ongoing thing for many 
years for me.” (Rose) 
Sophie expressed her anger directly at a product while she was out shopping with her son. 
She shares a photograph of the product during our third interview, depicted in figure 13 below. 
 
Figure 13: Photograph by Sophie 
 
 
 
111 
The product provoked enough anger in her, to cause a physical response.   
“Yeah, just the ridiculousness of it, that the whole thing, the fact that it’s £100, 
which is a lot of money, for a completely pointless and ridiculous … I don’t 
even know what. A decoration, a lamp, I don’t know. And it was just the 
pointlessness of it that just really got to me and was just like … I don’t know, 
it just … it’s just that lack of a sense of urgency anywhere, that is just … we 
can still carry on making these ridiculous, frivolous things, using up finite 
resources for this, so your house can look … I mean it’s not even pretty, is 
it? It’s just awful!” (Sophie)  
For many of the women, the behaviour of others and the social activities they were engaged 
in appeared to be in direct opposition to their own beliefs and values. If not apparent in their 
own social circles, confrontations occurred while partaking in regular necessary activities such 
as shopping. In these instances, the scale of opposition to their own disposition around climate 
change was particularly triggering. The irony here is that grocery shopping is a regular part of 
life, and thus there is little the women can do to avoid these types of situations all together. 
While non climate friendly products and the assumed individuals that are using them were a 
common source of frustration, in some cases the anger was clearly directed at the industries 
who were perceived to be responsible for climate change and the government for their lack of 
action to mitigate climate change.   
 
Government and corporations 
In some cases, the anger associated with climate grief was more narrowly directed. A few 
participants explicitly directed their anger towards industries, such as the food and oil industry 
who are perceived to be responsible for climate change. In other instances, anger was directed 
at the government for their lack of urgency and action, despite being aware that climate 
change has been known about for decades. Such expressions of anger place the responsibility 
of climate change and action elsewhere.   
 
Sophie clearly expressed her frustration towards oil companies who were perceived to be 
responsible or at least key contributors to climate change. This was a shared experience 
amongst participants generally. Again, the source of anger is centred around prevention, 
whereby companies are being accused of being aware of climate change but not taking any 
action to respond appropriately.  
“And this is what I’m saying about oil companies and these big corporations 
who are knowingly complicit in this and just don’t seem to care. They just 
 
112 
don’t care. And that … if that doesn’t make you angry, then … <sighs> you’re 
not really a human being, are you? How could that not make you angry? …” 
(Sophie) 
Some of the participants also expressed anger towards the government for not taking any 
action against climate change. During the photo elicitation exercise, May shared a photograph 
(figure 14), of a page advert to communicate some of the emotions that she was feeling.  
 
Figure 14: Photograph, by May 
 
 
The words in the ad were anger provoking (e.g. attack), and she explains what the photograph 
meant to her and why it makes her feel angry.  
“It’s like a shout, isn’t it? You know, ‘PM, when are you gonna wake up?’ We 
just can’t have it anymore. I thought to have so many charities and 
organisations backing it as well, it was just a really strong message and that 
was a page advert in The Telegraph. It was a big statement I felt, and it was 
a few weeks ago before COP, but you know, we can’t… It’s a statement, 
‘We cannot afford to have…’ so it just makes me sort of cross really that we 
keep saying it but are we really actioning it?” (May) 
 
113 
The disconnect between knowledge about climate change and the lack of action taken 
appeared a key source of anger for the participants. In these instances, however, I also felt 
that the anger overlapped with a perceived lack of agency of the participants and loss of power 
previously discussed in section 4.2.4.  
 
Overall, anger was associated with the behaviours and beliefs of others which conflicted with 
those of the women in the study. Anger was directed outwards in obvious ways, either a person 
or an institution, or in some cases more broadly. To some extent one could argue that due to 
the collective complicity of climate change, directing anger outwards is a way of protecting the 
self.  
 
However, defence mechanisms may also arise in social settings, whereby the oppositional 
behaviours of others could threaten a sense of self, leading to strong emotional responses 
such as anger. Building on this perspective further, I sought to unpack how in some cases, the 
direction of anger appeared to be directed towards the self. 
 
4.2.2. Anger towards the self 
Anger is a common emotion in regular grief experiences; however, it is important that the 
anger is directed somewhere. However, in some cases, anger can be retroflected, whereby it 
is turned inwards. Anger that is directed towards the self, is perceived as a maladaptive 
response to grief and can be experienced as other emotions such as depression, guilt or 
lowered self-esteem (Worden, 2001). 
 
In a few cases, the participants exhibited this direction of anger as well. However, it was quite 
subtle and therefore relatively difficult to detect. In these cases, I had to rely on subtle changes 
in pronouns which masked the direction of anger, as well as explore anger in relation to 
emotions which were more self- focused, such as those of guilt and complicity.  
 
Hidden and subtle  
Anger that was directed towards the self was easy to miss. None of the participants directly 
stated that they were angry at themselves within the context of climate change, but this was 
sometimes hidden beneath the deeper layers of narrative. Jessica spoke extensively about 
her struggles with trying to live in a climate friendly way, and tended to direct her anger, 
predominantly at corporations. In the following passage, one could easily miss the real source 
of her anger, which is directed at her own ability to make a difference.   
 
114 
“The whole food industry needs to change massively. And so I think that’s 
probably my biggest gripe and annoyance from a personal level… If I were 
to look at it from a social scale, like the thing that makes me more angry, is 
my inability to make impact on huge corporations that are manipulative and 
essentially blackmailing politicians into making decisions that go against 
some of the policies they’ve made, you know…” (Jessica) 
She later explains how she became overwhelmed by her emotions, when she became more 
and more aware of climate change. She goes through a period of trying to make significant 
lifestyle changes but then starts to question herself and her role in climate change, with some 
of that anger directed towards herself.  
“…And I was just like, … what am I doing? How am I ever gonna make 
change? How could I ever be big enough to combat stuff like this? And it just 
makes you so.. Angry. Because there’s very little you can do as an individual, 
that you just have to learn to accept it.” (Jessica) 
Without the wider context or back story of this specific participant, one could easily miss the 
self-directed anger layered within the narrative. However, the above passage clearly directs 
the anger at herself through the consistent use of self-pronouns.  
 
In another example from Rose, she explains how she feels angry about her sister’s decision 
to drive an SUV. After explaining the reasoning behind her argument with her sister, she 
reveals that her anger is more about how she feels about herself. She says:   
“…But it’s things like that, and I suddenly find I’m responding to something, 
when I get caught off-guard, I say something. I mean I can’t judge ‘cause we 
can’t all live perfect lives, and I find sometimes I know people who are so 
strict about some things I’m too frightened to tell them about what I do, only 
because I can’t do everything perfectly… I’ve got a vegan friend, and I would 
love to be vegan but it’s so complicated with so many other people involved 
in my life. I can’t achieve that now. I just do my best, I have to do my best. 
But I think if you know that people are doing their best, no one’s gonna get 
it perfect but if we all did our best then it would make a difference.” (Rose)  
Although Rose directs her anger towards her sister’s non climate friendly choices, the passage 
above reveals how her emotions mask her fears about her own perceived shortcomings 
around adopting climate friendly behaviours. While the examples above more clearly show 
how anger responses can be more about the self, than other people’s behaviours, in some 
cases this was even more subtle. 
 
115 
Since climate change was perceived to be a collective problem, participants tended to use 
collective pronouns such as ‘we’ in their accounts. Using the pronoun ‘we’ implicates the self, 
but in a less direct, more gentle way. In some instances, it appeared that although ‘we’ was 
used, it felt more like the anger expressed was directed towards the self. For example, during 
the photo elicitation interview, Eleanor shared an emotionally charged video about climate 
change made by a Northumbria religious community. She describes the video as “a hard-
hitting, no holds barred video about what we’re doing to the planet and just praying, Lord have 
mercy!”. After showing the image of the video still she captured from the video (figure 15), she 
reflects:  
“…And that still says it all really, ‘There is no planet B.’ And these angry 
women, young women, and men, youngsters, demanding that we make 
some changes in the way we run ourselves.” (Eleanor) 
Although she uses the pronoun ‘we’, using that video as a bridge to illustrate her anger, 
suggests that the anger is self-directed. Note that she says ‘we’ twice. This is also supported 
by the fact that she ends the sentence with ‘ourselves’, denoting her personal responsibility in 
climate change.  
 
Figure 15: Photograph, by Eleanor  
 
  
After discussing the video still, Eleanor offers to play the video. We watch the video together 
in silence. It consists of several images from environmental disasters and other scenes of 
violence. Yet the undertone feels somewhat hopeful. The music is uplifting, and it captures a 
sense of community, despite the negativity of the imagery. I ask her about the song which is 
played in the video, Kyrie Eleison, and what it means.  
Eleanor: Kyrie Eleison, Lord have mercy, Christ have mercy. So, you know, 
whoever you call the divine or the universe or whatever, you know, we’re 
 
116 
gonna call on that bigger force to help with this because we can’t do it on 
our own, we need… but I just have a sort of basic trust there is something 
about this amazing planet that will sort stuff out but we’ve gotta help with 
that.  
Me: What does it mean when you say have mercy? 
Eleanor: We are in a terrible fix, and we need help, compassionate help from 
something bigger than ourselves. Doesn’t mean to say we sit here and wait 
for it to come ‘cause we’re part of it. Does that make sense? 
Me: It does, it does. I’m thinking that when they say sort of like, ‘forgive me’, 
how does that – 
Eleanor: Forgive us. Yeah, well, for what we’ve done to the planet and to 
each other. There were pictures of war there, weren’t there? And we’ve 
brought these pandemics on ourselves by this factory farming and, you 
know, trying to get the flesh of creates in a wasteful sort of way, you know 
profligate way. We need forgiveness for our behaviour. We need to change 
our behaviour.”  
In her account, Eleanor indicates a personal sense of responsibility with regards to climate 
change, but also a sense of self blame, as she asks for forgiveness. The use of collective 
pronouns, particularly in the last segment denote an inward emotional reaction.  
 
Anger that turns into guilt  
In regular grief models, anger that is directed towards the self can sometimes be experienced 
as other emotions such as guilt (Worden, 2001). Around half of the participants shared feelings 
of guilt, although they remained more subtle to detect. As a more complex emotion, guilt also 
tended to overlap with other emotions such as a loss of power and sadness.  
 
Although this research did not focus on examining any differences between older and younger 
participants, complicity and guilt appeared somewhat more common in the older women. The 
older participants frequently drew on earlier memories and shared stories about how they first 
heard about climate change and the sense of frustration they felt around the lack of progress 
made.  
 
Eleanor, who idolised her father growing up reflected on a memory she had of him reading 
Limits to Growth (1972) when she was growing up:  
 
117 
“And I look back and I think, why didn’t I pay attention? Why didn’t we pay 
attention? It was all there. It’s happened the way they said it would happen.” 
(Eleanor)  
This sense of complicity and self-blame was also shared by others, such as Sarah who 
witnessed how the countryside in the UK has changed since she was a child. Reflecting on 
the decline of the trees in a well-loved part of the countryside, Sarah says:  
“It’s like the slaughter of the countryside, and it just breaks my heart every 
time I see it, and then at the same time I’m sitting there and I’m driving past 
in my car thinking, ‘I’m driving a petrol engine. Why am I part of this?’ We’re 
all part of this. Part of the problem.” (Sarah) 
Guilt and complicity were common emotions in Sarah’s grief experience. In a later account, 
she reflected on the flood maps which warned of flooding in her hometown.  
“It’s low-lying. And so there was this sense of … almost an inevitability 
because it resonated with that knowledge that I had tucked away 
somewhere and I just felt … like it made sense, it rang true. But at the same 
time this sense of why have we not done more? We knew. And yeah, and 
also just this sense of powerlessness always, you know?” (Sarah) 
Other participants, such as Charlotte was less explicit about her feelings of culpability. In the 
passage below, Charlotte exhibits subtle anger at herself for not knowing about climate change 
sooner. What follows shortly after, is a sense of blame directed towards herself.   
“Charlotte: … I think it’s quite strange that I felt quite unaware of so much 
of it for so much of my life. 
  
“Me: In what sense? 
 
Charlotte: Well, in almost every sense really […] I mean when you look 
back they’ve been often in documentaries and things they’ll be references 
to so-and-so saying this in 1970 and Prince Charles saying this in 19-
whenever. You know, I think, well, how can I have been so unaware of all 
that really, have missed almost that whole conversation. And yeah, that 
makes me a bit sad, a bit angry because it feels like there was a lot of time 
[…]” 
Overall, some of the participants expressed feelings of guilt because they felt that they could 
have done more to prevent climate change. Guilt from this perspective shows how anger can 
 
118 
be directed inwards instead of outwards. However, anger towards the self was more subtle 
and hard to detect. From a psychoanalytical perspective, anger towards the self was more 
hidden not always overtly expressed, potentially due to an innate desire to protect the self. 
However, anger was also sometimes expressed on behalf of someone else.  
 
4.2.3. Anger on behalf of others 
While most of the anger appeared to be directed towards others, or towards the self, in a few 
cases the women expressed anger on behalf of others. This type of anger was primarily 
centred around the injustice of climate change, which unequally impacts on communities in 
the Global South. During the photo elicitation interview several participants presented 
photographs of appeals by charities who were campaigning on this specific subject matter. 
While anger was a shared experience, feelings of injustice on others also often coincided with 
other feelings of sadness and powerlessness – but also a sense of guilt.  During the photo 
elicitation interview, Sophie shared a photograph of a woman walking through a flooded street 
(figure 16).  
 
Figure 16: Photograph by Sophie 
 
 
 
 
119 
“Sophie: Yeah. I mean these people have had the smallest impact, they live 
hand to mouth most of the time and just they’re not responsible for the 
consumerism in the northern hemisphere, and yet are losing their homes, 
their farms, their livelihoods, their lives.  
Me: And in terms of emotions, is that more around a sense of injustice or 
what other emotions could be attached to that from your perspective?  
Sophie: Yeah, definitely injustice, but again anger again, because it’s not 
their fault” 
Others also shared their frustrations around the unequal impact of climate change. However, 
in many cases social justice issues also appeared to overlap with other emotions, such as a 
loss of power. For example, when asking Margaret to unpack her emotions on behalf of people 
in the Global South, she says:   
“…When you see people at the COP who are speaking so emotionally about 
what’s happening to their … like in the Pacific Islands and things like that, 
about what’s happening to where they live, and still you can’t get leaders of 
some countries to agree to drastic, immediate action. They don’t seem to … 
well even if they do care, money comes first and their power comes first and 
that really makes me angry and makes me really, really sad. And you feel 
like going up there and apologising to them, but what can you do? You’re 
powerless in helping them really. I think that’s an emotion that I feel” 
(Margaret)  
In another example, a sense of guilt prevails. Eleanor compares her privileged life to others 
when she considers how she feels about the inequal impacts of climate change.  
“Y’know, in a way it makes me just feel I’ve had too much, not that I’ve lost. 
In the past I’ve been on cruises and I’ve flown round the world and all that 
stuff, and I’m like well what did all that cost? What were you doing, having 
those amazing experiences, when you were totally disregarding the state of 
the planet? And the state, in particular the state of the people in the worst-
hit countries and the poorest countries. I get very angry with these COP 
meetings.”  (Eleanor) 
Overall, a sense of social injustice around climate change was a common thread for many of 
the participants who felt emotions on behalf of others. Emotions were often mixed and 
commonly expressed on behalf of communities in the Global South. Participants often spoke 
about climate change in relation to other social justice-related movements or events, such as 
 
120 
the refugee crisis or racial injustice. Overall, the ability to feel emotions on behalf of others 
speaks to the collective nature of emotional experiences, but also how they may reflect more 
deeply held emotions of the individual, particularly in the case of guilt.   
 
4.2.4. Section summary 
Overall, most participants expressed anger as part of their climate grief experience. Anger was 
primarily directed outwards, towards the unwillingness of others and society at large to adopt 
the beliefs and behaviours that could prevent climate change. Although Worden’s (2001) 
model is useful for interpretation, psycho analytical approaches appear increasingly valuable 
for unpacking this specific emotion.  
 
From this perspective anger can be seen as a defence mechanism whereby the oppositional 
behaviours of others (i.e. climate denial) can be perceived as a lack of acceptance for the 
women’s views on climate change. As social acceptance is important for a sense of self, 
opposing behaviour from others in social circles may threaten their sense of self, leading to 
strong emotional responses such as anger.  In other cases, anger was also directed towards 
the self, often taking the form of guilt. However, this emotion was often more subtle and harder 
to detect. From a psycho-analytical perspective, this may not always be overtly expressed, 
potentially as way to protect the self.  
 
Although rooted in defence mechanisms, the fact that anger is expressed in relation to others 
illustrates that climate change is a social phenomenon. Furthermore, the perceived collective 
implicitness of climate change can create a rift between those that are making changes and 
those that aren’t perceived to be making enough.  
 
4.3. 
Fear  
 
“It’s like this cold feeling comes all the way through your body, like heavy, 
cold, physical feeling that this is the end and maybe it’s like knowing that 
you’re gonna die.” (Rose)  
The third most common emotion that the women expressed was fear. However, compared to 
the previous two emotions, the women were least likely to express this feeling. Verbal 
expressions, such as ‘frightened’, ‘fear’, ‘alarm’, ‘scary’, ‘dread’, and ‘foreboding’ were used to 
identify this emotion. In reviewing Worden’s model (2001) to interpret fear, it came to my 
attention that his description of anxiety and its root causes, overlapped with this study’s 
 
121 
participants descriptions of fear responses. Hence, I found it helpful to use Worden’s 
interpretation of anxiety, to better understand fear responses in climate grief experiences.  
 
According to Worden (2001), anxiety caused by the death of a loved one, can come from two 
different sources: first, the individual may not be able to survive on their own. Second, their 
anxiety could be related to a heightened sense of their own mortality. Within this study, it 
appeared that fear responses were predominantly related to the risk of death. This points to a 
limitation in the application of Worden’s model (2001) to interpret climate grief experiences, 
but also to the overlapping nature of emotions. While several of the women expressed fear, 
which in Worden’s model (2001) is more closely aligned with his interpretation of anxiety, most 
women did not report any feelings of anxiety.  
 
From a psychoanalytical perspective, fear is perceived as defence mechanism to danger, and 
within the context of climate grief, the findings show that fear appeared to be associated with 
the threat of death. However, not all women who experienced fear, were as explicit or articulate 
about this association. For example, some of the participants chose to convey their fears 
through images of starving animals. This may be explained through the defence mechanisms 
which can be aroused by fear responses, as well as some of the cultural taboos around 
discussing death and mortality in general. In several cases, fear-based responses appeared 
to be provoked by media imagery or public narratives about climate change. In one case this 
was also coupled with fear experienced in early childhood.  
 
The following paragraphs further elaborate on fear as part of the climate grief experience, 
guided by the summary map depicted in figure 17 below, with the left depicting the emotion 
and the right the main cause.  
 
Figure 17: Summary map: Fear in climate grief experiences 
 
 
 
122 
4.3.1. Threat to life  
Considering our dependency on the natural environment for survival, it was not surprising that 
several women reported feelings of fear as an emotional response to climate change. Feelings 
of fear were often associated with a threat to life. As a reaction to danger, the women 
expressed fears about the changing weather in relation to heat, flooding, as well as political 
instability and food shortages – all of which would threaten human wellbeing. While not all the 
participants were so explicit in their words about fear of death, this was often implied. 
 
For the youngest participant in the study, fears were centred around uncertainty and potential 
societal collapse. When I asked Eva, what her most dominant emotion was in relation to 
climate change, she says:  
“Eva: I think fear.  
Me: Yeah? 
Eva: Yeah. The future’s a scary thing anyway and then when it includes the 
possibility of the collapse of human civilisation it’s extra scary. Just 
uncertainty and not knowing at what point in my life I might start to feel the 
effect, how much I’ll be able to do for it and whether, for the rest of my life, 
it’ll start actually being pretty easy life or whether it’ll… yeah, I don’t know. 
There’s no way of knowing really.” 
While in this case, a broad reference was made to the end of human civilisation, in other 
instances fears were associated with several interrelated causes. For example, Sophie 
describes her fears in the following way:  
“I feel anxious about the growing instability and political instability and 
geographical instability. I think as clean water becomes harder to … not 
having enough water for everybody, not having enough food for everybody, 
I mean like we’ve seen this year, food prices rising and empty shelves and 
stuff. And it’s due to unprecedented weather…” (Sophie) 
The fears that Sophie describes, particularly those related to food and water directly threaten 
the survival of human beings. The list of fears, closely resemble the notion of cascading losses, 
described in section 4.2, where one loss triggers other loss of interconnected losses.  
 
For Sarah, her fears are somewhat more ambiguous, as she feels that climate change 
threatens our current way of life. She says: 
 
123 
“I’m afraid of extreme weather and I’m afraid of the impact that these things 
are going to have on our ability to live in the way that we know. For example, 
food supplies or… things that we take for granted. We’ve seen, haven’t we, 
food shortages this month related to weather. Sometimes I do feel that sense 
of foreboding, again, the news reported that, what haven’t we had?” (Sarah)   
In both cases, the women’s fear responses are a reaction to future-oriented danger, which 
could threaten the survival of humanity.  
 
Instead of talking directly about food shortages or global instability, other participants used 
examples of starving animals to convey their fears about climate change. While Eleanor, who 
is an avid bird watcher, shared a photograph of the bird flu pandemic expressing her fears 
around death, Rose connected her fears of death to starving animals in Africa.  
 
Figure 18. Photograph, by Rose  
 
 
Her account begins with a photograph she shares depicting tomatoes growing in her garden 
in autumn (figure 18) saying that she’s ‘terrified’ about losing the seasons, as well as the cold 
weather which she enjoys. She then connects these fears, to the death of wild animals which 
she saw on television. She says:  
“Like there was something on the television during the COP thing about in 
the Kenyan National Park and they showed all these wild animals which are 
literally starving to death and dehydrated, and they talked to local people 
who said this isn’t normal, we live with these animals; we haven’t seen this 
 
124 
before. And it really, you feel … really bad about this, but it’s still a long way 
away, you can’t be there and have the same feelings as them. But when you 
see this coming for you, then you realise how close it is.” (Rose) 
In this scenario, Rose draws a connection between her experiencing a particularly warm 
autumn in her hometown and her own impending future. Through the depiction of starving 
animals, and how closely she feels this reality, her own fears of death are heightened.  
Other women also tended to talk about their fears in a present and future tense. For example, 
Julie who mostly works outdoors, describes how she first started noticing the weather 
changing and how hot it was becoming in the summer. She says: 
“I was observing it because I worked outside, but most people worked inside, 
were out of touch with the seasons, and the … actual intense heat and 
everything, and so it really began to frighten me that we, that not everyone 
understood how much it was changing, and how rapidly.” (Julie)  
In one of our later interviews, we discuss her fear response in more detail. She presents a 
photograph of the extinct dodo bird (figure 19).  
  
Figure 19: Photograph, by Julie 
 
 
 
 
125 
When asking her what she felt was most frightening about climate change, she says:  
“What’s the most frightening? I think we’re heading for … I don’t wanna use 
the word apocalypse really, ‘cause that brings movies to mind, doesn’t it? 
But I think we’re, yeah, we’re definitely … on a path, we haven’t been on 
before, or we have but before humans were around I suppose, and it’s … 
we don’t know where it’s gonna end up.” (Julie) 
The association made between media (e.g. movies) is interesting as, several of the women 
also mentioned the influence of public narratives which appeared to trigger fear responses. 
For example, Eleanor describes what she referred to as one of her most visceral emotional 
experiences to climate change, through a cartoon flyer that she saw. In this image she 
describes how climate change was associated with other larger catastrophes and societal 
change. She says: 
“Eleanor: And it was when I saw that cartoon, so that’s 2020 we’re talking 
about, where they had this tsunami and the first one was coronavirus and 
the second one was Brexit and the third one was climate change, and I was 
absolutely horrified.  
Me: In what way? 
Eleanor: Well, it felt quite apocalyptic. It felt like … you think this is bad? You 
wait and see what the next one will be like … and the next one. And we know 
tsunamis wipe populations out so <long pause> yeah, I became aware that 
we, our generations could be the last of humanity.” 
The long pause in her account reveals how deeply she was affected by the imagery. She later 
describes that she felt so concerned about the state of the world at that point in her life, that 
she sought professional help. However, Eleanor’s experience is not unique, as fear centred 
on impending death as the result of a singular image or prophesy was shared by others as 
well.   
 
Rose, who had a religious upbringing had a very similar experience. In her account she 
equated climate change to the second coming of Christ, and the predictions made by French 
philosopher Nostradamus. She recalls the memory as follows:   
“It must have been seven or eight years ago, and <clears throat> <sighs> 
<pause> I was reading stuff, watching little videos and things, and there was 
this man, who was saying we have about ten years left as the human race, 
it’ll be gone in ten years, and he gave his reasoning and … I had this same 
 
126 
feeling that I had when, of dread when I’d heard about you know, the second 
coming and Nostradamus. I went in this like sick grief of awareness … this 
was the end.” (Rose)  
For both Rose and Eleanor, as well as several other women it appeared that fear responses 
were often influenced by wider societal media imagery and narratives. Such imagery and 
narratives appear to have been extremely powerful, as they were recollected so vividly, 
particularly in the case of Rose who associated her fears with a childhood memory as 
described earlier about Nostradamus. Overall, however, fear responses appear as defence 
mechanism to a potential threat to life caused by climate change.  
 
Overall, the perception that climate change is coming closer, bringing with it the imminence of 
death arouses fear responses in several of the women. Although the women were less likely 
to express this emotion, compared to sadness & sense of loss and anger, with only half doing 
so, the passages convey the sharpness of this emotion. Different to anger which erupts in 
bursts, and sadness & sense of loss which hollows out, the emotion of fear rattles the nerves. 
For many of the participants, fear around climate change is unsettling and conjures images of 
death, which often appear to be influenced by public media and social narratives. The 
influence of public narratives on emotional responses to climate clearly demonstrate the social 
nature of climate change.  
 
4.4. 
Chapter summary 
In summary, this chapter offered a deep dive into the experiences of eleven women who 
struggle with difficult emotions related to climate change. It presented empirical evidence that 
responded to my first research question: What are the climate grief experiences of women, as 
told through their life stories? In my work, climate grief experiences appear to be dominated 
by three overlapping emotions, that of sadness & sense of loss, anger and fear. Collectively, 
the findings contribute to the lack of knowledge around climate grief, from women based in 
the United Kingdom, which has been highlighted as a gap in the literature review, in chapter 
2.  
 
Sadness & sense of loss was shared by all the women but in several different ways. The 
women experienced multiple losses related to the environment, the future, personal 
relationships, and positive emotions such as hope and power. While sense of loss related to 
the environment and the future support existing findings (Randall, 2009; Cunsolo and Ellis, 
2018), losses related to personal relationships and positive emotions are two new 
contributions.  
 
127 
 
Next to sadness & sense of loss, anger was the second most dominant emotion identified. In 
climate grief experiences, it appeared that anger was centralized around the personal 
behaviours that could prevent climate change – and the inability or lack of willingness of others 
to perform those behaviours that could avert climate-related losses in the future. A key finding 
here, is that anger related to climate change appeared to be centred almost exclusively around 
social factors such as the beliefs and behaviours of others, illustrating that climate change 
should be seen as a social issue. The detailed findings around anger responses from a psycho 
analytical perspective are particularly important, as this has been highlighted as gap in 
knowledge within climate emotions research (Pihkala, 2022). The third most dominant emotion 
of fear was centred around the perception that climate change was imminent and that it would 
lead to death. Like anger, fear within the climate change literature is not as commonly 
discussed – although it is generally accepted that climate change threatens the existence of 
humanity.  
 
In interpreting climate grief experiences, Worden’s model (2001) was only useful to a certain 
extent. In many cases, the model did not go far enough to explain the complexity behind the 
climate grief experiences, particularly in the case of anger and fear. From a psychoanalytical 
perspective, these types of emotional responses could be perceived as defence mechanisms 
and thus they were useful in helping to unpack the layering of the emotional response. For 
example, psychoanalytical approaches helped to explain why anger was directed outwards 
towards others, as well as inwards, often turning into guilt. In both fear and anger responses, 
a desire to protect the self, also explained the hidden and subdued ways in which these 
emotions were sometimes expressed. Understood as a form of self-protection, psycho 
analytical approaches illustrate how, concepts of self, such as identity intersect with climate 
grief experiences. 
 
 
 
  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
128 
5. 
Findings part II: The strengths and limitations of using 
life story methods in studying emotional responses to 
climate change 
 
Whereas part I provided an analysis of the climate grief experiences of eleven women, part II 
provides an evaluation of the methodology I designed to obtain the data. Hence, this chapter 
will seek to answer my second research question: To what extent can a life story method be 
used to explore emotional responses to climate change? 
 
The origination of this second research question is a direct response to the need to innovate 
methods in social sciences to study emotional responses to environmental issues, as has 
been suggested by Lertzman (2015). To study climate grief experiences, I designed a novel 
adaptation of BNIM. Yet, if BNIM or any adaptation thereof can be used to study emotional 
responses to climate change and other social phenomena, a deeper examination of its 
strengths and limitations is needed.  
 
Moran et al (2022) argue that to date, little research exists which explores how participants 
experience or understand BNIM. Similarly, little is known about researchers’ experiences of 
applying BNIM, with only a handful of articles going into any depth at all. Thus, while much 
has been written about how to apply BNIM, critical reviews of the method, based on real life 
application, are lacking.  
 
To address these gaps, this chapter offers is an analysis of the advantages and limitations of 
using a life story methodology to explore the climate grief experiences of women. I will begin 
this chapter with a brief overview of the methodology which I specifically designed and adapted 
for this research. Here I will highlight where I have deviated from BNIM and why. This will be 
followed by a systematic literature review of BNIM using the methods described in chapter 3. 
Several themes and tensions will be discussed, in particular around the notion of 
empowerment and ethics.  
 
Several of the themes observed through the systematic literature review are woven into the 
final analysis, which will be presented last. Here I provide an evaluation of the method I 
designed to study climate grief experiences. The data used to carry out the analysis included 
data from all three interview sessions and fieldnotes. The analysis is centred largely around 
the SQUIN – the defining technique in BNIM, through the illustration of multiple cases. Where 
 
129 
suitable, I have taken the analysis further by considering the influence of subconscious 
processes, such as defence mechanisms (Hollway and Jefferson, 2000).  
 
5.1. 
A summary of BNIM data collection adaptation 
 
While my methods chapter clearly outlines my rationale and study design (chapter 3), this 
section will briefly summarize the method to set the stage for this findings chapter. I first came 
across BNIM through the work of Lertzman (2015) who applied an adaptation of the method 
in her study on environmental melancholia. Similar to my approach, she adopted a semi-
structured interview for her second interview. She highlights the importance of innovating 
research methods within the social sciences, more specifically around environmental issues. 
She suggests that new ways must be found to better examine unconscious processes and the 
complexity of emotions and experiences that underlie how people engage with and respond 
to climate change. Recognizing some of the limitations of traditional qualitative methods and 
the need to go beyond surface level responses, I was keen to apply an alternative method 
more closely embedded in psychoanalytical orientation.  
 
Considering that environmental issues are also deeply engrained within the social context, 
Lertzman’s (2015) research led to the work of Wengraf (2001). The Biographical Narrative 
Interpretative Method in its original form was first introduced by German-born Rosenthal who 
used the method to study the generational impact of the holocaust on its survivors and their 
family descendants (Rosenthal, 2010). Following this original work, the method was further 
described and systematized by Wengraf (2001) who wrote a detailed manual on this specific 
type of interviewing and interpretation style as part of a broader introduction to qualitative 
interviewing techniques. Although the methodological lines of BNIM have been somewhat 
blurred as it has been applied in multiple different ways (as we shall see in section 5.3) these 
initial guides served as inspiration for the methodology I designed to study climate emotions. 
 
The first interview started with a full SQUIN. A full SQUIN, instead of a partial SQUIN, was 
selected on the basis that I was interested in the whole life story. Although I did not assume 
that a ‘whole picture’ could be achieved using a full SQUIN (Foulds, 2017), I felt that narrowing 
the SQUIN to a specific life phase, period or emotional experience would undermine the non-
directive nature approach – a key BNIM principle I sought to maintain. Based on the 
information I gathered from the full SQUIN, I carried out a preliminary analysis by extracting 
the key life story data from each life story and plotting this information onto Visual Biographical 
Timeline. This is an adaption of BNIM. The original method works primarily with text-based 
 
130 
interpretation, through the development of a Biographical Data Chronology. This is used to 
differentiate between the told story and lived story, which can be further used in a case study 
analysis (Wengraf 2001). I instead chose to focus only on the told story and make a visual. By 
lifting out the data and plotting the sequence of events onto timelines, I was also able to 
analyse and categorize common themes. I’ve provided an example of a Visual Biographical 
Timeline below (figure 20). All the timelines (in larger formats) can be found in Appendix C.  
 
Figure 20: Visual Biographical Timeline, Jessica 
 
 
 
The Visual Biographical Timeline was presented to the participants during the second session 
with the aim of contextualizing their climate grief experiences into their life story. Through the 
examination of an individual’s life story presented on a visual timeline, we were not only able 
to draw out the wider themes and frameworks, but also how climate grief may also potentially 
evolve over time. It sometimes also showed how personal, or global life events could have 
played a role in influencing emotional responses to climate change. 
 
The open, non-directive nature of the full SQUIN likely contributed to some participants not 
explicitly speaking about their emotional responses to climate change during our first interview 
session. This potential issue was flagged during the pilot. I therefore limited my use of PINs 
and incorporated a semi-structured interview format during the second interview session. This 
is a second adaptation of BNIM, as BNIM requires that questions during the second interview 
are solely based on topics mentioned during the first interview. Interviewers must also apply 
the same phrasing used by the participant and the topics must be introduced in the same order 
as they were mentioned during the first interview (Wengraf 2001). While I used the participants’ 
 
131 
original phrasing in formulating my questions, I tended to focus on topics that appeared to 
bear relation to their emotional responses to climate change.  
 
A third interview is optional in BNIM, and it is often used to ask follow-up questions or introduce 
new topics (Wengraf, 2001). As I introduced new topics during the second interview, here I 
opted for a more creative method; a photo elicitation interview to further examine the emotions 
and experiences discussed during the second interview. Participants were invited to take 
photographs in their immediate environment, of objects, scenes or places which resemble 
their climate grief and any other dominant climate emotions. The purpose of the approach was 
to make more conscious the expression of climate grief in every-day experiences. An overview 
of the differences between my adaptation and traditional BNIM approach has been provided 
below in table 5. 
 
Table 5: Key differences between traditional BNIM and my adaptation 
 
Method 
Traditional BNIM 
My methods and adaptation 
First interview 
Full SQUIN or Partial SQUIN 
Full SQUIN (No adaptation)  
Life story analysis  
Biographical Data Chronology 
(Two track interpretation) 
Visual Biographical Timeline 
Second interview 
Interview only based on topics 
mentioned in first interview 
(PINs) 
Presentation of Visual Biographical 
Timeline 
Small selection of PINs 
Semi structured interview 
Third interview  
Optional, any format  
Photo elicitation interview 
(No adaptation) 
 
This adaptation of BNIM presented above responds to the need to innovate methods to better 
understand emotional responses to climate change. However, if BNIM or any adaptation 
thereof can be used to study emotional responses to climate change and other social 
phenomena, a deeper examination of its strengths and limitations is required. The remainder 
of this findings chapter will focus on answering this question, starting with a systematic 
literature review of BNIM.  
 
 
 
132 
5.2. 
A systematic literature review of BNIM 
While much has been written about how to apply BNIM, critical reviews of the method remain 
scarce. Moran et al (2022) argue that currently no research exists which explores how 
participants experience or understand BNIM. Similarly, little is known about researchers’ 
experiences of applying BNIM, with only a handful of articles going into any depth at all 
(Nicholson, 2009; Foulds, 2017; Peta, Wengraf and Mckenzie, 2018). As part of this thesis, I 
was thus interested in further understanding the application of BNIM in practice, as well as the 
experiences of participants and researchers in applying the method across other domains.  
 
To lay the groundwork for this findings chapter, I carried out a systematic literature review to 
better understand the state of the art regarding the application of BNIM in scientific research. 
To guide the literature review, I applied the PRISMA Framework (2020) which outlines the 
screening and exclusion process of the articles. Once all the articles had been identified, they 
were read and analysed using an analytical framework. A total of 52 articles and sources were 
used in the final analysis. The Prisma Flow Diagram and a detailed description of the review 
process is described in chapter 3.  
 
I will first present a brief overview of the domains and disciplines where BNIM has been used, 
followed by a more in-depth review of which BNIM methods have been applied. This section 
will close with a discussion of the benefits and limitations of the method identified in the 
literature. 
 
5.2.1. Application of BNIM data collection and analysis methods 
 
BNIM is largely applied in health and sociology domains, with small numbers of 
participants sharing common characteristics 
Based on the results of this systematic literature review, it appears that BNIM has been used 
to study a wide range of topics, across several overlapping disciplines. The largest body of 
work, however, falls within the area of health (40%), followed by Sociology (26%). The 
remaining articles are spread relatively evenly across Education (9%), Psychology (7%), 
Criminology (7%) and Political Science (6%), with just a handful of articles classified as ‘other’ 
(5%) such as Business. One quarter of the articles were published before 2015, with the 
earliest in 2006, while the remaining articles were published within the last decade (since 
2015). Despite the breadth of disciplines, however, the research articles share several 
similarities.  
 
 
133 
Figure 21: Proportion of studies applying BNIM  
 
Considering the qualitative nature and resource intensity of BNIM, most studies were carried 
out with smaller groups of participants. The number of participants in all studies ranged from 
3 to 25 (with one outlier of 44 participants). The median number of participants of those 
excluding the outlier was 12. Within each study, the participants often shared similar external 
characteristics, or specific role identities, which were often central to the topic under 
examination, i.e. male farmers (Cush and Macken-Walsh, 2018), African asylum seekers 
(Dalikeni, 2019), prisoners (Farrier, Baybutt and Dooris, 2019), infection control nurses 
(Mcnamara, Martin S., Fealy and Geraghty, 2013), or Iranian migrants (Sekechi, 2020). Hardly 
any studies (apart from Roseneil (2012)) focused on the experiences of individuals whose 
external identity markers, such as their gender, immigration status or profession did not form 
an integral part of the recruitment selection criteria for the study. 
 
While participants in BNIM studies often shared similar characteristics, the analysis also 
revealed that these commonalities were sometimes also simply determined by their shared 
experiences. These can roughly be divided into experiences which were taking place on a 
more personal level or societal level – depending on the field of study. On the personal level, 
an example includes patients undergoing treatment for health conditions, such as diabetes 
(Duke, 2021). In other studies, particularly in the discipline of sociology, the time-bounded 
nature of the research is reflective of a specific historical time period. Examples include single 
women becoming pregnant in Ireland between 1996 and 2010, a time when abortion policies 
were eased (Bradley 2020) or children living with a disability during times of austerity in Ireland 
(Flynn, 2019). In many cases, BNIM was applied to study and capture a process of change. 
For example, Weekes et al (2024) explored the experiences of black women undergoing a 
 
134 
career change, whiles Meares (2010) examined how skilled women from South Africa rebuilt 
their lives after moving to New Zealand. 
 
BNIM Data Collection and Data Interpretation – Use of conventional sequence was rare 
One appealing aspect or perhaps even a benefit of BNIM, is that although it encompasses a 
formulaic approach to both data collection and interpretation methods, there remains some 
flexibility in terms of how the individual method components can be applied. For example, 
BNIM data collection should consist of at least two interviews, but has a flexible approach to 
the third, meaning that the third interview is optional. A conventional BNIM analysis would then 
also include a case study analysis. BNIM’s case study analysis can only be conducted if BNIM 
data collection methods have been applied. Yet, the data can also be analysed by most other 
methods of qualitative data analysis depending on the study’s requirements. Using other 
methods of data interpretation, even if using the BNIM data collection methods, would in that 
sense not be considered a conventional sequence.  
  
By far, most studies analysed, apart from Flynn (2019) did not follow the conventional BNIM 
sequence. This observation has also been corroborated by Flynn & Wengraf (2021). 
Therefore, considering this analysis, it made sense to further explore the variances within 
BNIM data collection and data interpretation methods as two separate components. As a 
reference, an overview of how the studies applied the separate BNIM components has been 
provided in the figure 22 below.  
 
Figure 22: Overview of BNIM data collection and data interpretation methods applied 
 
 
135 
BNIM data collection: Preference for partial SQUINs and three interview sessions 
One of the most fascinating interviewing techniques used in BNIM is the SQUIN (Single 
Question Aimed at Inducing Narrative). The fact that all studies described this technique, 
suggests that the SQUIN is a defining marker of BNIM. As a reminder, a SQUIN can be applied 
in two different ways, either full or partial. A full SQUIN is completely open and asks for the 
whole life story. Partial SQUINs, however, can be centered around a specific phase of life, a 
biographical strand (e.g. career) or a historical phase (e.g. COVID) or a specific issue (e.g. 
migration), depending on the aims of the study (Wengraf, 2001). 
 
However, based on the information provided, it wasn’t always clear which type of SQUIN had 
been applied or how it had been formulated. Only two thirds of the studies were explicit about 
how the SQUIN had been structured. In the remaining third, no detail was provided and 
references to the SQUIN were more descriptive (Jones, 2006; Mullan, 2022). Of those studies 
which were explicit about the formulation of their SQUIN, the majority applied a partial format. 
Examples include: 
“Can you please tell me your story of your life since you were first advised 
to take medicines. I would like you to tell me about all the experiences, 
people, and events, which are important to you personally. Please begin 
wherever you like and take as long as you need.” (Sultan and Swinglehurst, 
2023)  
“Can you please tell me the story of your work life since you first heard about 
and became involved in transnational teaching? All those events and 
experiences that were important to you personally” (Smith, K., 2014) 
Only a very small minority applied what could be considered as a full SQUIN, as illustrated by 
the two examples below: 
“As we have discussed before, I am researching on the experiences of 
sexuality of women with disabilities in Zimbabwe. Could you please tell me 
the story of your life including your experiences as a woman with disability 
living in Zimbabwe, your intimate love relationships and sexuality 
involvements, including pregnancy and childbirth experiences that have 
been important for you up to now, begin wherever you like, I will not interrupt. 
I will just take some notes in case I have any further questions for after you 
have finished telling me about it all.” (Peta, 2017b) 
 
136 
“Can you tell me the story of your life and personal relationships—all the 
events and experiences that have been important to you personally, how it 
has been for you? Please begin wherever you like” (Roseneil, 2012) 
In this full SQUIN, the participants are encouraged to tell their whole story, and in the first 
example are provided with some topical guidance. This differs from the partial SQUINs 
previously illustrated, which clearly demarcate when and where the participant should start 
their story. The extent to which the SQUIN is focused, particularly when using the open 
formats, largely depends on the extent to which the participants have been made aware of the 
general direction of the study and ethical considerations (Wengraf, 2001). 
 
The first interview should be followed by a second. This either occurs shortly after the first 
interview during the same session on the same day, or as soon as possible. Over 40% of all 
studies carried out at least two interviews. However, in a few cases, this wasn’t entirely clear 
(Napier and Clinch, 2019; Neves, 2022). Although some studies were not as explicit regarding 
the number of interviews that were held, there does remain some ambiguity around the count 
of the first interview. As the second interview is an extension of the first interview, and will 
sometimes take place on the same day, this can sometimes be interpreted as one interview, 
with two parts – or two interviews.  
 
Nonetheless, based on the information available it appeared that in most instances, the 
studies accounted for two separate interviews, with the second interview largely following the 
format prescribed by BNIM. In a handful of studies, other interview approaches were used 
including FANI (Sekechi, 2020), the Tavistock Psychoanalytical Observational Approach 
(Nicholson, 2009), the presentation of a storyline based on the first interview (Lockey, 
Jennings and Shaw, 2010) and other more general semi-structured approaches (Bigabo and 
Jansen, 2020).  
 
A slightly larger proportion of studies, the remaining half, carried out three interviews. This 
interview remains optional and in its classic form is an opportunity for the researcher to 
introduce new topics. The format is up to the discretion of the researcher. Some studies used 
this interview to apply a semi-structured approach (Sultan and Swinglehurst, 2023). In another 
example, focus groups were applied alongside visual ethnography (Fenge and Jones, 2011). 
Lertzman’s (2015) study which inspired this thesis, was relatively unique as participants were 
asked to discuss an advert which was introduced during the final session. However, others 
such as Mooney et al. (2021) purposively did not apply a third interview, suggesting that the 
non-directive nature of the first two interviews would be jeopardized.  
 
137 
In summary, studies applying BNIM consistently applied the SQUIN, with the large majority 
opting for a partial SQUIN. Most continued the BNIM sequence conducting three interview 
sessions, and a slightly smaller proportion stopped at two interview sessions. A handful of 
studies deviated from the BNIM approach, by incorporating alternative approaches during the 
second interview.  
 
BNIM data analysis: Preference for alternative modes of analysis 
Compared to the data collection where the prescribed BNIM approach was mostly followed, 
most studies deviated from the prescribed method of data analysis. This means that compared 
to methods used to collect the data, there was much more variance regarding data analysis 
and interpretation. This is likely because while BNIM data collection can be analysed in several 
ways, BNIM data analysis can only be applied if the BNIM data collection requirements have 
also been met.  
 
Assuming that BNIM data collection has been applied, a BNIM analysis would broadly consist 
of the following three components: 1) the two-track interpretation of the lived and told story 
(the Biographical Data Chronology) 2) the use of a reflective panel during the analysis and 3) 
a case analysis (Wengraf 2001). Using the criteria mentioned above, a review of the studies 
indicated that just over a quarter of studies appeared to have applied the full BNIM sequence 
as prescribed, including the data analysis. Examples include: Flynn (2019), Roseneil (2012) 
and Smith (2014). The study by Egerod et al., (2020) was an outlier, as they applied BNIMs 
data interpretation method, but did not apply the recommended BNIM data collection.  
 
The remaining studies applied alternative data analysis methods. Most of these studies used 
thematic analysis to analyse the data (Pabian and Vandebosch, 2019; Duke, 2021). A small 
proportion of studies applied different forms of narrative analysis, such as narrative thematic 
(Sultan and Swinglehurst, 2023) and pluralistic narrative (Hawking, et al., 2020) approaches. 
The remainder of studies applied a framework method analysis (Flynn, 2021), or open coding 
(Mullan, 2022).  
 
Although a significant proportion of the studies did not apply BNIM analysis, it was notable 
that several studies did opt to include some sort of panel in their analytical procedure. For 
example, Neves (2022) carried out a thematic analysis alongside interpretation workshops, 
Jones (2006) had a reflective team, and Hawking et al., (2020) sense checked their analysis 
with a panel of critical friends. The use of a panel is a classic component of the BNIM analytical 
 
138 
approach, and it appears that this may have inspired other researchers to adopt a similar 
approach, to complement their alternative methods of analysis.  
 
A small proportion adapted the BNIM analytical sequence in other ways. For example, Mooney 
et al., (2021) applied open coding, rather than carrying out a case analysis, while Sekechi 
(2021) carried out a full BNIM analysis but incorporated a complementary interview approach 
(i.e. FANI) for the data collection. In another case, only the told story was analysed, but not 
the lived story, and was followed by a thematic analysis (Cush and Macken-Walsh, 2018). 
 
Overall, it was more common for studies to apply alternative, more conventional approaches 
to data analysis, such as thematic analysis. The lack of studies applying the full approach may 
be due to specific requirements of the study, but it has also been suggested that the approach 
is too resource intensive (Peta, Wengraf and Mckenzie, 2018). These limitations, but also 
strengths of BNIM will be discussed in the next section.  
 
5.2.2. Strengths and limitations of BNIM 
 
The following section delves into the strengths and limitations which the studies in this analysis 
identified. In this section I wanted to focus on the experiences of the application of the method, 
rather than providing an analysis of the rationale for why BNIM had been applied. However, it 
was somewhat more common for studies to provide a description of the method and their 
rationale for choosing it, rather than provide a critical reflection on the method post 
implementation. Nonetheless, a large proportion of studies did provide enough information to 
allow for the observation of some key trends.  
 
Data richness and volume 
One of the most frequently mentioned strengths of BNIM is the richness of the data which the 
studies were able to collect, with two in five studies mentioning this. This presented itself in 
multiple different ways. Some studies claimed that BNIM allowed them to collect rich data, 
allowing them to gain additional understanding of a topic or experience that they hadn’t 
previously considered (Mcnamara, Martin S., Fealy and Geraghty, 2013). According to Neves 
(2020), this is what makes BNIM particularly useful for studying concepts and experiences 
that are not predefined. In addition to gaining novel insights, Meares (2010), mentioned that 
BNIM enabled the researchers to go deeper into hidden dimensions of experience. Others, 
including Roseneil (2012), illustrated how BNIM allowed them to take into consideration 
sociohistorical contexts therefore providing a broader and more nuanced understanding of 
 
139 
subjectivity. Although rich data is beneficial, the sheer volume of data generated by BNIM has 
been mentioned as limitation due to its resource intensity. This was not only an issue for 
researchers handling the data, as was identified by Lertzman (2015), but Ross & Moore (2014) 
suggested that BNIMs lengthy approach may also be problematic for participants who might 
be time poor.  
 
SQUINs, PINs, countertransference and ethics 
The use of two to three interviews per participant and the SQUIN during which participants 
are allowed to tell their story uninterrupted, for often an unlimited amount of time, means that 
BNIM elicits large volumes of data. While this is often perceived as beneficial to the research, 
several studies suggest that the SQUIN as the key method to collect rich data comes with 
several interrelated risks. Altogether, over a quarter of studies highlighted several different 
issues.  
 
A handful of studies indicated that the open nature of the SQUIN caused some participants to
