go off topic (Mooney, 2021; Luczaj, 2023). While the nature of a full SQUIN can blur the lines 
between what is on or off topic, Mooney (2021) presents an interesting case. In his study on 
accessing child abuse services, he illustrated that while the SQUIN posed did not ask 
participants to speak about their experiences of abuse, but rather their experiences of 
accessing abuse services, one participant in the study did use most of the interview to speak 
about their abuse. Thus, while the participant shared a rich narrative, very little of it was 
relevant to the topic of research. While Mooney (2021) provided sufficient detail, others, such 
as Lucaj (2023) only generally commented on the fact that some participants focused on the 
topic and others didn’t.  
 
Yet other issues were also mentioned. For example, Hawking et al., (2020) noted that one 
participant struggled to tell their story and suggested that SQUINS might not be suitable for all 
participants. This was also observed by others. For example, Flynn (2021) who conducted a 
study with a small group of participants, some of whom had intellectual or developmental 
disabilities, found that younger people or those with an impairment were more comfortable 
with semi-structured interview styles questions, rather than the open format of a SQUIN. 
Based on her experiences, Flynn (2021) highlighted the importance of considering 
participants’ verbal and linguistic skills when using a SQUIN. In their work, Ross & Moore 
(2014) suggested that some participants may not have the confidence to tell their story.  
 
 
140 
To ensure an inclusive research practice, a smaller SQUIN, worded in more simple terms 
might be helpful (i.e. “Tell me about your life”). In addition, the interviewer may need to soften 
the non-directive approach advocated by BNIM. While it would remain important not to 
introduce any new questions or topics, in my own practice I found that providing small prompts 
based on the participants story and using their exact phrasing when a trail of thought appeared 
to be lost was helpful. This type of ‘gestalt rewind’ can help participants to retrace their steps 
and allow them to either pick up their story along the original path or inspire another direction 
which flows better for them. 
 
The open nature of the SQUIN can also at times be so powerful that a few studies highlighted 
that this may also have an emotional impact on the researcher (Nicholson, 2009; Peta, 
Wengraf and Mckenzie, 2018; Sekechi, 2020). Although this could be attributed to the nature 
of topics for which BNIM is often selected for, it is plausible that the method itself is a 
contributing factor. For example, Ross & Moore (2014) who compared BNIM to semi-
structured interview approaches, noted that although the semi-structured interviews tended to 
provide similar information, they often lacked the strong emotional dimension of BNIM 
interviews. This emotional load is then carried by the researcher, through a process of 
containment, which can at times be emotionally taxing.  
 
Several studies illustrated how stories of participants can be triggering for researchers. Peta 
et al.’s (2018) examination of BNIM illustrates how a researcher studying the sexuality of 
disabled women, relived previously told stories of trauma shared by her study participants and 
had to seek therapy as a result. Although this case was extreme, this experience has also 
been echoed by others (Nicholson, 2009; Ross and Moore, 2014; Sekechi, 2020). While 
Nicholson (2009) observed that there was a lack of space to explore her own strong emotions, 
Lertzman (2015) noted that she experienced fear, anxiety and dread through the process of 
countertransference. Transference within the context of a research interview, (or during 
therapy) occurs when the participant unconsciously transfers another emotionally significant 
relationship on the researcher. Countertransference is how the researcher responds to this 
transference. However, emotionally significant relationships may also be transferred onto the 
participant, by the researcher (Hollway and Jefferson, 2000). For example, a researcher may 
subconsciously perceive a participant as their daughter or father and act accordingly. This can 
also happen in other ways. For example, Sekechi (2020) who herself had a similar background 
to her study participants admits how she was emotionally affected by her participants’ stories. 
She found, however, that her emotional labour became a motivational driver and heightened 
her sense of responsibility to the participants.  
 
 
141 
While the emotional experiences of the researcher were rarely mentioned, the studies 
highlighted above raise an ethical dilemma regarding the application of BNIM. Only one study 
identified in the literature review goes into any depth regarding ethical issues of BNIM (Moran, 
Green and Warwick, 2022), although Ross & Moore (2014) also briefly raised this. The central 
critique is centered around the use of PINs, which are asked during the second interview. In 
their study, Moran et al. (2022) illustrate how the use of PINs to elicit deeper narratives was 
deeply distressing for several participants, raising concerns about (re)traumatization. These 
in situ research observations are critical as several studies using BNIM, argue that their main 
reason for applying it, is because of its empowering nature. Yet, it is not entirely clear if they 
are indeed empowering for the participant. This tension will be explored next. 
 
Empowerment dynamics 
According to Wengraf (2001), a key benefit of BNIM is that by means of the SQUIN, 
participants may only disclose what they wish. This, he argues, minimizes the power 
imbalance that can exist between the researcher and the participant. In around one quarter of 
the studies, participant empowerment was part of the rationale for using BNIM. These studies 
tended to include the voices of those who may be more marginalized or vulnerable. Examples 
include single pregnant women (Bradley and Millar, 2020), ethnic minorities with co-
morbidities (Sultan and Swinglehurst, 2023), children (Pabian and Vandebosch, 2019), or 
disabled women (Peta, 2017a). Mullan (2022) notes in his study with vulnerable youths that 
BNIM gives participants a great deal of autonomy over their narrative, while Nicholson (2009) 
noted that the approach would give ‘a voice’ to her participants. Another study highlighted that 
the method would empower participants, not consigning them to passive research subjects 
(Flynn, 2019). 
 
From this perspective, BNIM is not only applied because it is suitable to the characteristics of 
the study group, but it also serves an additional purpose, which is to create a sense of 
empowerment for the participants through the research experience itself (Fenge and Jones, 
2011). It is interesting to note however, that although some these studies (e.g.Dalikeni, 2019; 
e.g. Weekes, Ward and Mguni, 2024) were able to clearly provide the empowerment rationale 
of applying BNIM, only a few (Peta, 2017b; Bigabo and Jansen, 2020; Lynch, 2021) mentioned 
whether the experience of the interview was in fact empowering for the participant, albeit in 
limited detail. For example, Bigabo & Jansen (2020) in their study with genocide perpetrators 
noted how BNIM offered therapeutic value to participants, suggesting that life stories 
contribute to prisoner rehabilitation. Others, like Lynch (2021) mentioned that the research 
experience had been cathartic for some participants, as the process inspired greater self-
 
142 
reflection, while Peta’s (2017b) participants specifically said that the experience had been 
valuable to them.  
 
Despite these examples, there is still some debate as to whether empowerment can truly be 
achieved through BNIM. In their study, Ross & Moore (2014) appear somewhat critical about 
BNIM being empowering for participants. They suggest engagement, empathy and mutual 
understanding that leads to empowerment can only be achieved through a co-production of 
meaning. In their view, this involves a more interactive exchange of dialogue between the 
respondent and the interviewer. Although this may take place during the third interview, Ross 
& Moore (2014) argue that this should be done at an earlier stage.  
 
Although Ross & Moore (2014) regrettably do not go into any more depth on this topic, their 
critique in my view also highlights that not all participants will have the capacity to narrate and 
make sense of their story by themselves. For example, certain participants may require more 
verbal input and guidance of another human being to not only help tell their life story - but also 
to tell it in a way whereby they are able to imagine and craft an alternative meaning to a difficult 
experience. Although empowerment will look slightly different for everyone, the experience 
tends to include a process of ‘overcoming’ and transformation which is in part achieved by a 
new or fresh perspective. We cannot assume that everyone is able to access those deeper 
insights through their thoughts alone. Furthermore, although BNIM may support some 
participants in achieving a sense of empowerment, we must also consider that other factors 
may have contributed to the process.  
 
In summary, while a good proportion of studies, provided ‘empowering participants’ as a 
rationale for using BNIM, it was rare to find studies that critically reflected on whether this had 
been achieved. Hence it is still unclear whether BNIM is empowering for participants - and 
while it may be for some, there are additional risks to consider as it could have an adverse 
effect. 
 
5.2.3. Section summary 
In summary, this systematic literature review has demonstrated that BNIM studies are largely 
applied in the domains of sociology and health, with most studies aligned towards to the latter. 
Study samples tend to consist of small numbers of participants sharing similar characteristics. 
The labour intensiveness of the method and the large volume of data it generates, highlighted 
by several studies, is a likely correlation. Nonetheless, the amount of data BNIM generates 
was seen as strength as well, as most commented on the benefits of having rich data.  
 
143 
  
It was rare to find studies applying the conventional BNIM sequence of both data collection 
and analysis. In my review, the BNIM data analysis was rarely used, with most opting for other 
modes of qualitative analysis (e.g. thematic). Studies tended to favor BNIM data collection, in 
particular the use of partial SQUINs and three interview sessions. However, several studies 
raised ethical concerns about SQUINs and PINs. They suggest that these techniques can 
have negative consequences for the researcher through countertransference, and for the 
participant who may be re-traumatized through the retelling of difficult events. Others 
questioned whether the method was suitable for everyone, particularly those who may be 
more vulnerable, such as women, youths and those with impairments or lacking in linguistic 
skills.  
Interestingly, this appears to contrast with the rationale which others had given for selecting 
BNIM. Many studies indicated that BNIM was chosen specifically because the experience may 
be empowering for participants. However, the verdict on this point appears mixed or 
inconclusive as it was rare to find studies that were able to provide any real evidence of this. 
This systematic literature review thus brings to light a potential discrepancy between method 
rationale and real-life application – one which can pose ethical risks for the participant, as well 
as the researcher. In sum, the question remains whether the method is in fact suitable for 
those for whom it was intended.  
 
The final section of this findings chapter aims to explore in more detail my own experience 
and the experience of my participants in the application of BNIM.  
 
5.3. 
BNIM in practice: The value, limitations and ethics of SQUINS 
 
The literature review highlighted a significant gap in knowledge around the practical 
application of BNIM, more specifically how the method is experienced by the participants and 
the researcher. Considering the novelty of the BNIM adaption I designed, and the need to 
innovate methods in the social sciences particularly around environmental issues, this section 
provides a closer examination of my BNIM experience.  
 
The analysis is centred around the application of the full SQUIN, which is a unique contribution 
as most studies opt for a partial SQUIN instead. I will present two different cases illustrating 
how a full SQUIN can elicit very rich data, which may initially appear to bear little relation to 
the topic of research. I do this through an illustration of my line of questioning (an adaption of 
the PINs) and the responses that follow. In the process, this will point to the level of 
 
144 
interpretative work that is required when using a full SQUIN. I will then take the analysis further 
by showing how responses to a SQUIN may be also attributed to subconscious processes 
(Hollway and Jefferson, 2000) in both the participant and the researcher.  
 
In the final section I use another case study to highlight how using a full SQUIN can raise 
ethical concerns for both the researcher and the participant. This points to the main contention 
around BNIM, regarding the empowerment rationale. Using this case study, I will illustrate how 
shifting the power balance to the participant, could potentially intensify feelings of vulnerability 
and cause distress for some participants.  
 
Yet, overall, most participants appeared to have had a positive experience and were receptive 
to the SQUIN. This has also been highlighted by others (e.g. Foulds, 2017). Closing this 
section, I will provide evidence illustrating how BNIM, can also have an empowering and 
positive impact on the participants. Like the previous sections, I will centre the analysis largely 
around the receptivity towards the SQUIN and the feedback received at the end of the final 
interview sessions.  
 
5.3.1. Subconscious defences  
According to my systematic literature review, most studies using BNIM data collection use a 
partial SQUIN, choosing to narrow their question around a particular time, event or experience. 
My study on the other hand, was quite rare in that a full SQUIN had been used. It is not entirely 
clear why most studies use a partial SQUIN. A partial SQUIN may not, for example, reduce 
the likelihood for participants to go off topic – which is one critique of the method. For example, 
Luczaj (2023) who used a partial SQUIN by narrowing the story focus to academic career 
paths mentioned that not all participants were “cooperative” (according to Luczaj, 2023) and 
chose to speak about other topics than their professional lives. A similar situation emerged in 
Mooney’s (2020) study on child abuse, whereby one participant spoke about their experience 
of child abuse rather than their experience with telling their story to the authorities. None of 
the studies from the literature review who used an open SQUIN mentioned this issue. While 
much more evidence is needed, these examples could suggest that going off topic is not 
related to whether an open or partial SQUIN is used.  
 
With BNIM, however, the notion of what it means to ‘go off topic’ is interesting to consider. The 
non-directive facilitative nature of the SQUIN is an open invitation for participants to comprise 
their story according to their own train of thought and gestalt. In that sense, there is no such 
thing as going off topic because the objective of BNIM is not to follow the mental framework 
 
145 
of the researcher, but rather that of the participant. This idea of ‘going off topic’ is an important 
consideration, because in a few cases, I initially was also unsure to what extent the life stories 
that participants chose to tell during the first interview would be relevant to the research. It 
was relatively uncommon for the women to go into any detail about their emotions and 
experiences with climate change during the first interview session. While at first glance it may 
appear that no relevant data was obtained, this outcome is informative of the interpretive work 
required when using BNIM. However, it may also point to subconscious psychic defences 
which may be at play.  
 
To illustrate this, I will present two very different cases, that of Sophie and May. These cases 
have been selected to illustrate the different ways in which participants may respond to an 
open SQUIN and in one case cast doubt on the extent to which the story is relevant to the 
research topic. Rather than presenting the life story of each in full, I will instead show the follow 
up questions I developed post analysis of the first interview.  
 
Following a key BNIM guideline, all the questions were solely based on the life story which 
had been shared with me during the first interview. In addition, and as per BNIM guidelines, I 
used the same phrases and wording as in the life story at the beginning of each question and 
the questions were asked in the same order as they were mentioned, following the gestalt of 
the storyteller. I did not, however, use the phrase formats suggested by Wengraf, as the 
language and terminology they required did not feel accessible to me. I elaborated on this in 
chapter 3.  
 
The case of Sophie which will be presented first, was from a researcher’s perspective an ‘ideal’ 
response to an open SQUIN. It is ‘ideal’ in the sense that Sophie’s life story clearly includes 
events and experiences that appeared relevant to her emotional responses to climate change. 
The second case of May on the other hand, initially appeared more ‘problematic’ due to the 
lack of references to climate change. However, through closer examination and analysis, it 
became clear that several of May’s stories did provide pathways to her emotional experiences 
and other subconscious processes might have impacted the interview. The two cases will be 
presented next.  
 
The case of Sophie 
Sophie is my eleventh, and final interview. After I give a brief introduction to the research and 
presentation of the SQUIN, she indicates that the ask is clear and that she is happy to proceed. 
 
146 
Her life story starts in the year that she was born. She explains her family structure and paints 
a vivid picture of the type of environment in which she grew up.  
“OK. No problem. OK, so I was born in 1971. I have an older brother, so I 
was the baby, just the two of us. So, when I was born, we lived in what was, 
at the time, quite a small village in Leicestershire, so we were surrounded 
by fields and there was a canal nearby that we used to walk along, and we 
had a dog when we were very small as well. So I have a lot of memories of 
walking over the fields with the dog and my dad, in particular, and my older 
brother, and spending a significant amount of time outdoors.” (Sophie)  
Sophie tells me her life story in just under 40 minutes, which results in approximately 4,500 
words of interview text. Her life story focuses on key life events and experiences, many of 
which appeared to be connected to her emotional response to climate change. Based on her 
life story, I drafted the following list of questions which I asked her during the second interview, 
after reviewing with her, her Visual Biographical Timeline. This can be found in Appendix C.  
 
Figure 23: Excerpt interview questions Sophie 
 
1. You mentioned that when you were 15/16 you started becoming more aware of the 
hole in the ozone layer and polar bears facing extinction, do you remember what that 
was like for you?   
2. You commented that when people are between 16-18 that you find out or start to find 
out what you believe in as a person, rather than what your parents have passed down 
to you – were there any particular beliefs that you were developing that you noticed 
were different than others?   
3. You mentioned that this was also the time you started to realize “that the world isn’t 
the place you thought it was when you were a child” - can you tell me what that was 
like for you?  
4. Around the time, after you came back from xxxxx, you commented that for those 
teenage years, you perhaps become more disconnected from nature in the personal 
sense – as perhaps a reason for your depression – can you tell me more about that?    
6. You mention that in your work, you are connecting people with nature – so that that 
they will care about it, and then protect it – but you also mention – that it is to “to 
reconnect with what they might have had as children” - can you tell me more about 
what you mean here? 
 
147 
 
The questions above clearly demonstrate an evolution of her experiences with climate change 
and the emotions that may accompany those. This is an “ideal” example of how a full SQUIN 
and the line of questioning that follows, can be used to study emotional responses to climate 
change. While no life story is right or wrong, the clarity and sequencing of this specific life story 
was ideal in that it very easily lent itself to the exploration of the climate grief experience. The 
ease is related to the fact that there was not a significant amount of interpretative work required 
to analyse her life story and prepare a set of questions for the second interview. In her life 
story she made clear references to climate change, pointed out key transformational moments 
and also told her story in a chronological sequence.  
 
The case of May 
In other cases, however, how the life story related to climate grief experiences wasn’t that 
obvious. In the telling of her life story, May, for example, did not go into any further detail nor 
draw any obvious associations to climate change or how she felt about it. In this situation, the 
selection of follow up questions was challenging. In the moment the questions I designed felt 
experimental at best, because there appeared no obvious trail leading to her emotional 
experience. However, upon closer examination, there may have been other subconscious 
processes at play which may have clouded her narrative, as I explore now.   
 
May is my third participant, and after an introduction and exchange of formalities I present the 
SQUIN. Her response to my life story question is initially met with some hesitation. She first 
asks me how the question is related to my research.  After explaining the method, she appears 
somewhat satisfied and tells her life story. She says:  
“OK, so I’m XXXX born and bred, and I was born into a farming family. I 
didn’t pass my 11+, I don’t know if you know what that means so I went to a 
comprehensive school, and then I trained as a physiotherapist so that’s what 
I worked as. We married quite young, and we had a family quite young, in 
fact, this is our wedding anniversary today.” (May)  
After I congratulate her on her wedding anniversary (accidentally breaking BNIM protocol), 
she talks for another 2 minutes, bringing her life story to a close. Realizing that I would need 
more detail, I glance at my notes to assess which topic was mentioned first that I could ask 
more detail about (following BNIM protocol). I pick up on the fact that she was born into a 
farming family and formulate a question around the topic.  
 
148 
Me: Could you go back a little bit earlier because you said you grew up on a 
farm?  
May: Yeah.  
Me: Can you explain a little bit more about what that was like going back all 
the way to the very beginning?  
This appeared to help, because after a somewhat awkward start, May starts to feel a bit more 
at ease and tells me a slightly longer version. Nevertheless, the task remains challenging for 
her, and she closes with the statement: “I have nothing exciting to tell you about my life really.” 
I then continue to ask follow-up questions about specific experiences in the same order as 
they were mentioned in her initial life story. The interview lasts just over an hour and produces 
around 8,800 words of interview text.  
 
Although May’s interview produced almost twice as many words as Sophie’s, selecting entry 
points for questioning remained challenging. The objective was to collect more narratives 
related to climate change or climate emotions. If this wasn’t available or obvious, my approach 
was to explore other emotive subject matter or key life transitions or events. In May’s case, 
the online recruitment questionnaire which I had used to screen participants was useful. 
In response to my question to describe any experiences with climate grief or other difficult 
emotions related to climate change she had filled in: 
“My husband’s Church had the opportunity to have a ground source heating 
fitted with grants to cover the cost, the team voting on the issue could not 
see the benefits and went with replacing the condemned gas boiler. People 
may regret that decision this winter with the predicted fuel bills. The 
temperature for the new boiler has been set lower than previously and the 
idea of people needing to wear more layers of clothes to Church has not 
been greeted well. The Church of England website has a marvellous section 
on climate change matters but getting dioceses and congregations to 
engage with the matter is another matter.” (May) 
Although read out of context this may not be an obvious entry point, this was how May 
responded to a direct question about her emotions. Hence for May, her involvement with the 
Church appeared to play a role in how she related to climate change. As she had also 
mentioned her religious affiliations several times in her life story, I felt that this could be a likely 
pathway to discussing her emotional experiences. I therefore used this as a springboard for 
my line of questioning for the second interview. Below are the three questions I developed for 
May. 
 
149 
 
Figure 24: Excerpt interview questions May 
1. You mentioned that you currently split your time between the Quakers and the Church 
of England, can you tell me more about how that happened?  
2. You mentioned that two of your children were possibly green evangelists, can you tell 
me more about that? 
3. You said you always felt you were discouraged from going into farming and that your 
mother felt that girls need to go and make their own way in the world, do you remember 
any more detail about that time?  
 
Although at first glance, it seemed less obvious her answer to the first question was the first 
time where she again revealed her frustrations with the Church and their lack of response to 
climate change. Going into detail about a Church event that she attended, she said:  
“There were 22 of us and I think that was from 22 different churches, so it 
was really sad and I was sort of towards the younger end of it <laughs> but 
it was very good. It was a very good day but again we don’t seem to be 
inspiring the youth to think about climate change, all these important matters 
really.” (May)  
In this excerpt, May reveals that climate change is important to her, and that she may have 
some feelings of responsibility towards inspiring young people. She does, however, have two 
adult children of her own which are involved in climate change action. May’s own comments 
around her adult children indicated a direct relation to climate change, and interestingly her 
response to the second question, also ends with the following statement about her frustrations 
with the Church, particularly the lack of involvement of the younger generation. 
“But it’s very interesting at church really how they’re trying to get these green 
issues through and yet we’re not getting those 40-year-olds, 30- or 40-year-
olds engaging with it. Or our age being jostled by their children. I don’t know. 
It’s very strange. I think it’s just a very conservative, small c conservative 
environment the church seems to be in.” (May) 
May’s response to the third question in this series on a completely different topic, very 
interestingly, also builds on the same theme. After describing at length her lineage of 
hardworking Scottish farmers, she says:  
 
150 
“But my husband has this thing, he says it goes in generations, he says one 
generation works hard, one doesn’t, one works hard, one … you know...” 
(May)   
Although ever so slightly, the lack of interest in climate change of others, in particular the 
younger generation is mentioned again. In her response, she spent most of her time speaking 
pridefully about her generation’s attitude towards hard work, contrasting how she feels about 
the current generation. Her indirectness and the way in which she channels her sentiments 
through the persona of her husband or her children, suggests that she may not have been 
able or comfortable to express herself more explicitly.  
 
May’s case illustrates several points. Firstly, using a full SQUIN requires a significant amount 
of interpretative work due to its open nature. It is not clear whether this would have been lesser 
so had a partial SQUIN been applied. Second, climate emotions can be hidden and third, not 
all participants are able to or comfortable in talking about their emotional experiences in a 
direct way.  
 
That May could have felt uncertain about her participation can be derived from several 
observations. During the second half of the interview, she reveals that she wanted to 
participate in the research but was initially worried that her strong emotions would inhibit her 
from speaking about them. She says:  
“I think now, that’s probably partly why I’ve applied to do this, because I think 
maybe at one time it [how she felt about climate change] would have 
frightened me too much, and a) I would have felt I couldn’t converse about 
it, and b) I think I would have been too upset, that I couldn’t converse about 
it.” (May) 
The length of her life story and need for clarification to begin her life story are also two initial 
flags. Furthermore, she starts her life story with a distinct comparison between her and me. 
She points out that she didn’t pass her 11+, and had a different kind of schooling, which she 
assumes I wouldn’t have heard of. She then also closes her life story in a way that diminishes 
the value of what she’s shared, she says: “I have nothing exciting to tell you about my life 
really.”  
 
In this case, Hollway and Jefferson’s (2000) work can also be useful in helping to understand 
May’s response. The distinction she drew between us, her as less educated, and me not being 
familiar with what that meant, could be a symptom of the structural differences of power which 
 
151 
can exist between the researcher and the participant. Within the research context, it is 
important to be aware of these as much as possible. I found this not to be apparent just in 
May’s case.  
 
Several other participants frequently sense checked the latest climate change information with 
me or made assumptions about what I knew or the books I had read. Aware of this power 
dynamic, I sometimes found myself playing down my knowledge or keeping my responses 
vague to keep the interview focused on the participant’s experience. However, it was 
interesting for me to observe that I sometimes also remained ambiguous, because in some 
cases I was concerned about exposing my own lack of knowledge in certain areas. In that 
sense, I was also engaged in defending my being (Hollway and Jefferson, 2000). Perhaps I 
did not want to disrupt their view of me as a credible researcher.  
 
Better understanding these relational dynamics within the interview context, could in part help 
explain a lack of confidence of certain participants. In this case, May’s lack of confidence could 
have initially also been influenced by the unequal social power dynamics that were transferred 
into the interview setting. We clearly differed in our educational backgrounds. Yet to some 
extent this may also have been subconsciously inferred through my own anxieties, as well as 
hers.  
 
Thus, while some scholars (Ross and Moore, 2014; Hawking, et al., 2020) highlighted that 
BNIM could potentially be a challenging format for participants who lack in confidence, through 
my own research I found that this may be misinterpreted and should therefore not be a 
deterrent. Where lack of confidence appears, it is important to consider what other factors 
might be at play. In practice, this also means adopting a more flexible approach, whereby 
stepping away from the BNIM protocol is accepted. Without any soft intervention, the first 
interview session with May would likely not have been able to progress. Stopping an interview 
entirely would have surely shaken her confidence as well. Through a thoughtful and somewhat 
indirect line of questioning, May’s experiences and emotions did eventually emerge. In 
addition, for participants lacking in confidence a phased approach may be beneficial, as 
multiple points of interaction breeds familiarity.  
 
Although the use of full SQUINs remain underutilised, if willing to do the interpretative work, 
they can be very a very powerful technique. To avoid uncertainty regarding the relevance of 
the data collected, it may also be useful to consider several soft priming strategies. During the 
recruitment phase, potential candidates were made aware that the research project was about 
climate change and that I was interested in identity and difficult emotions. My research flyer 
 
152 
(Appendix G) mentioned these components. However, I had deliberately chosen to keep the 
subject matter topline to avoid influencing responses.  
 
The candidates were then asked to complete a recruitment questionnaire (see Appendix E), 
wherein I specifically introduced the emotion, climate grief by asking the following question:  
“Can you describe any experiences you’ve had with climate grief, or any other difficult 
emotions related to climate change?”. All potential participants provided responses to this 
question, wherein they reported difficult emotions related to climate change. Examples 
include:  
“Feeling alone or in the tiny minority, including in the company of friends and 
family” (Margaret) 
“Fear, dread of how my grandchildren's generation will manage / repair what 
has been done” (Charlotte) 
These types of questions primed the participants to think about how they felt about climate 
change in advance – and these initial responses were often threaded through the rest of their 
interviews as was demonstrated in May’s case. The materials which followed, such as the 
Consent Form and the Participant Information Sheet also included brief information on the 
topic of study. Although customary, these alongside the questions in the recruitment 
questionnaire would have primed the participants enough to understand the topic of research 
and helped to focus their thinking.  
 
In summary, full SQUINs are an invitation for participants to tell their life story whereby they 
decide on the direction of the interview, what to tell and how and when to tell it (Wengraf 2001). 
Nonetheless, full SQUINs remain underutilised in research. Although it is not entirely clear as 
to why, a plausible assumption is that too open a question might elicit data perceived as 
irrelevant to the research. By providing evidence of two very different cases, I have been able 
to demonstrate the level of interpretative work involved in working with full SQUINs. Life stories 
which may not appear to ideally fit the research topic, often require more encouragement and 
additional interpretative work.  
 
Nonetheless, SQUINs, irrespective of whether they are full or partial can remain challenging 
for some participants who may lack in confidence. While it is easy to blame this on the 
competency of the participant, lack of confidence can also be attributed to other factors, such 
as subconscious psychic processes, or structural power differences which have permeated 
the research setting. The very nature of qualitative research is that the outcome is not always 
 
153 
predictable. With non-directive techniques, such as the SQUIN the level of uncertainty vastly 
increases. While in some cases, this may be beneficial to the research, it also comes with 
additional ethical risks.  
 
5.3.2. Ethical dilemmas and containment 
While not all women easily spoke about their emotions, several women did disclose very 
personal and sometimes traumatic information about their lives. The method, particularly the 
SQUIN, sometimes elicited rich and very painful memories. Nearly every first interview elicited 
the sharing of at least one personal traumatic event, to the point where I was waiting, 
sometimes slightly hesitantly, in anticipation of it. The traumatic experiences were all different 
in their own sense, covering topics such as child abuse, sexual assault, spousal violence and 
deaths of partners and unborn children. While the use of the SQUIN was designed to uncover 
rooted emotions and to better understand climate grief in the context of their life stories, the 
SQUIN was so powerful that I felt that sometimes it had unpeeled too many layers, raising an 
ethical dilemma for both the participant and the researcher.  
 
Ethical dilemmas in psychosocial research have two points of entry, in that it examines 
whether the research in question has caused any harm to participants, or to the researcher. 
To my own surprise, I found myself at this junction several months into the research, after 
completing all three interview sessions with my seventh participant. While the ethics 
committee had approved the research and my research materials, and practices had aligned 
with all ethical requirements (e.g. information provision, informed consent, voluntary 
participation), one case brought into question the ethics around BNIM, in particular the SQUIN.   
 
As observed in the literature review, the ethics around the application of BNIM are rarely 
examined and where they are, they have been centred around the use of PINs (Particular 
Incident Narratives (Moran, Green and Warwick, 2022). PINs are questions used in BNIM to 
elicit narratives based on specific incidents mentioned in the first interview session, following 
the SQUIN. The main ethical dilemma surrounding PINs according to Moran et al., (2022) is 
centred around the unequal power dynamic between the researcher and the participant. 
Participants can be asked to share information that they are not comfortable with, especially 
if participants feel vulnerable or unconfident. BNIM, in particular the practice of “pushing for 
PINs” are critiqued for their potential to heighten emotional distress and present a risk of re-
traumatisation (Moran, Green and Warwick, 2022, p. 2). Although the literature primarily raised 
ethical dilemmas regarding the use of PINs, this section will illustrate how the use of a SQUIN 
 
154 
can also present an ethical dilemma, for both the participant and the researcher. To 
demonstrate this, I will present the case of Julie below.  
 
The case of Julie 
Julie is my seventh participant. With the larger half of my fieldwork completed, I’m fatigued but 
confident about the research process. At the first interview, I ask her to tell me her life story. 
She appears comfortable but not entirely knowing where to start, I suggest that this is entirely 
up to her, following BNIM protocol.  
Julie: Wow! That is quite a big question, isn’t it?   
Me: Yes!   
Julie: I’m not sure how much detail. I mean do you just wanna know what I 
did at different ages, or what struck me or what influenced me or –  
Me: I think just start wherever feels natural for you, and whatever comes to 
mind, so there’s no wrong or right way, so just whatever comes to mind.   
She starts her life story, beginning on the day that she was born and ending on the present 
day and her involvement in local climate action group. Her first interview lasts for 36 minutes, 
resulting in over 5,000 words of text. She closes her life story, with the comment:  
“Anyway, that’s my life story. Miserable, isn’t it? <Chuckles>” 
The chuckle at the end, is somewhat deceiving in terms of the content that she shared. Julie 
had encountered multiple difficult experiences in her life. Without going into any further detail 
about what was shared during this first interview, it’s worthwhile sharing my own notes post 
interview, illustrating how her life story had impacted me.  
“I just sat a bit still for a while – I think I feel a bit better now. It was really 
tough to hear all the pain she’s gone through. But I do think it ended okay. 
I’ve got a few follow up questions for her, but it’s going to be hard to draw 
this out in a timeline... I am also not sure about wanting to probe too much 
into irrelevant things that could bring up more difficult stories.” (Post first 
interview, Me)  
Whether it was her life story or my own inability to process the emotions that had come up as 
a result, the passage above clearly illustrates the effect that her life story had on me. Although 
I was able to develop several questions that bore relation to climate change, I was somewhat 
hesitant whilst preparing for the second interview.  
 
155 
“Getting settled in, to carry out my second interview with Julie. It's the end of 
the week, so I’m feeling ready to just leave it for a bit. Doing the interviews 
on such a consistent basis, is quite taxing. I am slightly apprehensive 
because the first interview caught me off guard a bit.” (Pre second interview, 
Me) 
Although the remaining two interview sessions proceeded as normal, my notes from the time 
observed that she appeared somewhat agitated at the start of the third interview. As we were 
preparing to go through the photographs she had taken and I asked her if there was a 
particular order in which she would like to talk about them, she said:  
“No, there’s no order and I have to say not a great deal of thought went into 
it ‘cause I just forced myself to remember every now and again, so I’m afraid 
it wasn’t top of my priority list, but anyway.” (Julie)  
It was after this final interview, after I turned off the recording that she mentioned that she had 
been distressed after the first interview. My notes at the time record how she found it not only 
hard to tell her life story, but also to see laid out on the Visual Biographical Timeline during the 
second interview. She said that it felt confrontational. I understood where she was coming 
from, because I also remember finding it difficult to process and translate her interview into a 
timeline.    
 
While this case was an outlier, it illustrates how a SQUIN can result in an emotionally 
unpredictable situation for both the participant and the researcher. I found myself looking for 
clues to see if I could have prepared better, but her responses on the recruitment questionnaire 
did not appear unusual. When asking her why she wanted to participate in the research she 
responded:   
“I am impatient with the response to the climate crisis and want to urge 
people, especially the young, to become more vocal with politicians about 
the urgency needed to cut emissions and keep to the 1.5-degree ceiling. My 
two teenagers are apathetic about the climate crisis although they came with 
me on one Friday School Strike before lockdown. I fear lockdown stopped 
the progress initiated by Greta and now a new movement or revolution is 
needed to spark interest again. Time is running out!” (Julie) 
Hence, there was no indication that Julie’s life story would warrant a precaution. Her reasoning 
for joining the study simply outlined her frustrated emotional state around climate change. 
While compared to May’s, Julie’s life story did more easily lend itself to the exploration of 
climate grief experiences, her case points to other issues which can arise while using an open 
 
156 
SQUIN. Although Julie did not appear overly distressed during her first interview, the method 
had an emotional effect on her, as well as me.  
 
Julie’s case demonstrates that the nondirective nature of the SQUIN, as well as the inability 
for the interviewer to re-direct or intervene can create a difficult situation for both the participant 
and the researcher. For the participant, a SQUIN can be confrontational in the sense that they 
may not have expected their life story to unfold in the way that it did. In addition, some 
participants may not have wanted to present themselves in a particular way. Exposing deeply 
intimate and emotional vulnerabilities may also, arouse emotions such as fear and anger, as 
way to defend themselves.  
 
From another perspective, a SQUIN may also potentially influence on the process of 
containment which is desirable during highly emotive interviews. Containment is based on the 
premise that emotions are unconsciously shared between people. As a protection mechanism, 
emotions that are too painful can be passed onto another person, who experiences the 
emotion through empathy. However, if the emotion is too difficult for the recipient, it may be 
cast off out or denied. (Hollway and Jefferson, 2000). Reflecting on my notes and how I 
responded to Julie’s interview, suggests that at the time, my capacity to contain her emotions 
was limited.  
 
While Julie’s interview was challenging, the nature of the SQUIN interview setting may also 
have been a contributing factor. This is because, during a SQUIN interview, there is little or no 
dialogue for longer periods of time – if at all. Although containment can be achieved through 
several non-verbal cues, they may not have the same effect. The interviews were also held 
online, which may have made them harder to detect.   
 
In summary, SQUINs can raise several ethical dilemmas for both the participant and the 
interviewer. Within the literature ethical risks related to BNIM have previously been raised by 
Moran et al., (2022), but only around the use of PINs. To soften the ethical dilemma, Moran et 
al., (2022) call for a reduction of methodological stringencies, while Nicholson (2009) suggests 
combining BNIM with other methods, such as Tavistock which could better support 
researchers while researching sensitive topics. In their work, Peta et al. (2018) also suggest 
that more psychological support is needed for interviewers. In this case, the interviewer sought 
therapy after experiencing secondary trauma after using BNIM.  
 
While Julie never responded to the emails I sent to her after her final interview, knowing that 
she was already in therapy was comforting. I did, however, report this incident as a “near miss” 
 
157 
with the Ethics Committee. Despite the challenging nature of Julie’s case, it is important to 
reiterate that her case was a bit unusual, as the remaining participants did not have a similar 
experience. Most found the BNIM research experience to be a positive one, suggesting that it 
could be empowering. I will close this section, highlighting how using BNIM or an adaptation 
thereof can also be a rewarding experience.  
 
5.3.3. Psychological safety, good listening and engaging with participants 
beyond the interview setting 
While the previous two cases, those of May and Julie, illustrated how using a SQUIN can pose 
certain risks and challenges, overall BNIM as an in-depth biographical method appeared to 
have a positive impact on the participants. Every participant who completed the first interview 
went on to complete all three, meaning that this study had a zero drop-out rate. While there is 
limited evidence to conclude that the experience was ‘empowering’ for the participants, it is 
highly plausible that it was a positive experience for most.  
 
In this sub-section I will illustrate through several examples how, for most of the women, their 
participation in the research appeared to be positive. I will primarily center my discussion on 
the practices which contributed to the psychological safety of the participants, focusing on the 
activities that took place during the interview session, but also beyond.  
 
Practices during the interview setting 
For most people, participating in a qualitative interview is a unique experience. Yet BNIM 
provides an even more unusual experience, in that the first interview is entirely dependent on 
one single question. The stakes during the first interview were high, as I had no other fully 
prepared questions to fall back on - and the participants were also likely to feel nervous. In an 
unfamiliar setting, participants would be asked questions about their life and share 
experiences which could be painful for them. Establishing psychological safety during the very 
first interview was thus critically important.  
 
Psychological safety is a belief that an individual is free to speak their mind honestly without 
any social retaliation. This type of safety is important to establish within the interview context, 
as firstly, participants will likely be in a situation which is unfamiliar to them which can create 
a sense of unease. Second, psychological safety enables participants to speak freely and 
candidly about their experiences, which allows for the collection of rich, personal data. As I 
was interested in learning about their emotional experiences in particular, I needed to create 
an environment where this felt safe to do so.  
 
158 
 
As I personally experienced what it was like to answer a SQUIN during my training with Tom 
Wengraf before I started the research, I was acutely aware of how daunting a SQUIN could 
feel for the participant upon hearing it for the very first time. I therefore took considerable care 
in presenting the question in a way that would feel encouraging. I delivered the SQUIN with a 
sense of energy and confidence, while also acknowledging the unusual task at hand. 
 
Overall, my approach was successful, and with a bit of encouragement for some, all the 
women told me a version of their life story. The interviews lasted anywhere between 36 
minutes and 1 hour and 55 minutes, with most hovering around the 1-hour mark.  My first 
participant, Jessica, laughed and jokingly asked “Crikey, how long do you want me to talk 
for?”. The eldest participant, Eleanor was pleasantly surprised and said:  
“Oh! My goodness! Wow. Okay is this information is anonymised in your 
work, yes? Oh! My goodness me what a wonderful opportunity, I do like 
talking about myself.” (Eleanor) 
The other participants - although positive - reflected on the scale of the task. For example, 
Sarah responded by saying ‘Wow… that’s a really big question”, while Margaret said: ‘Wow, 
yeah, gosh! And we’ve only got two hours.’ The youngest participant, Eva responded by 
saying: “Wow, my whole life story! It feels very indulgent!”.  
 
This reference of indulgence was particularly interesting because it was also mentioned by 
other participants. For example, Eleanor closed her life story interview by saying: “This is very 
self-indulgent”, followed by a small chuckle. Such comments are a reminder of the rarity of the 
interview setting, particularly one in which the topic of focus is solely on the participant and his 
or her experience.  
 
Throughout the first interview, I listened closely to each life story, while also video recording 
the interview. While I would essentially use the transcripts and the recording to analyse the 
data, I also took notes on a paper pad. While note taking is customary during BNIM, I also 
found this very useful in helping me to stay mentally focused during the interview. My 
concentration was not always flawless and active notetaking helped me to keep my focus and 
stay on track.  
 
According to Lavee and Itzchakov (2023), maintaining concentration is a key marker of good 
listening practices, which can help to enable psychological safety within the research setting. 
In addition to active notetaking there are also other practices which can be carried out prior to 
 
159 
the interview to enhance focus and attention. Here, the practice of DS-FA note taking required 
by BNIM proved particularly useful - not just in clearing my mind before the interview session 
but also as way to process what I had learned after the interview.  
 
According to Lavee & Itzchakov (2023), good listening practices during the interview also 
includes acknowledging what participants are saying by providing verbal or non-verbal cues. 
Although this may appear challenging during the first BNIM interview, where the interviewer is 
not supposed to ask any further questions but just listen, this can be achieved in other ways.  
Although it is difficult to showcase non-verbal cues of good listening, some transcripts 
recorded several moments of shared laughter and long silences. Mirroring emotions of the 
participant is a way to validate and accept a participant’s emotional behaviour. This practice 
was important throughout all the interview sessions.  
 
While creating psychological safety during the first interview was somewhat subtle, this was 
more obvious and easily achieved during the second and third interviews. These interviews 
provided an opportunity to reflect on the content shared by the participant, which is another 
marker of good listening practices (Lavee and Itzchakov, 2023). A clear example includes the 
application of PINs, which I used, albeit sparingly, during my second interview. These are 
created using the exact same phrasing and words of the participant.  
 
In addition, the detailed Visual Biographical Timelines which presented information from the 
first interview, and the discussion and iteration of its content with the participant during the 
second interview, was also a very clear way of demonstrating my intention to thoroughly 
understand what the participant had told me. Figure 25 depicts the online interaction between 
myself and Rose while I tell her, her life story from the beginning. During this process, she has 
time reflect on what she’s told me, and she confirms or updates her life story, which I amended 
for her in real time. These kinds of reflection practices were equally incorporated during the 
third and final interview, where I sometimes drew on information the participant had shared 
with me during first or second interview.  
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
 
160 
Figure 25: Joint revision and update of the Visual Biographical Timeline, Rose 
 
 
 
Towards the end of the final interview, I asked participants if there was anything else they 
would like to ask or say about the research. While some participants expressed gratitude for 
the opportunity, or where curious about what other participants had said, some were surprised 
with the amount of what they had to say: For example, Eva said “I think I’ve said more things 
than I ever thought I would!”. Others reflected on the value of the experience on a deeper 
level. For example, Margaret talked about the value of being able to speak openly about her 
emotions.  
“It’s been another really good discussion. I really like our conversations and 
thank you for giving me the space to say what I think. It’s very precious, I 
think, having space to say what you think. I’ll talk to myself but it’s not the 
same, it’s good just having somebody else there.” (Margaret) 
Similarly, Sophie also spoke about the value of the interview experience in helping her 
understand her emotional experiences better.  
“From my point of view it’s been really interesting, because it’s not something 
that I’ve … it’s been there for a long time but I haven’t really examined it in 
myself, so it’s been quite interesting to unpick it and like you say, name some 
of those emotions and where they might come from, and particularly today, 
looking at the photos, I can start to see the themes of how I’m thinking about 
things, and how things trigger me! But yeah, that’s quite interesting ‘cause I 
haven’t really seen that before. ‘cause when you’re in the moment, you don’t 
necessarily connect those dots, do you? (Sophie) 
 
161 
Overall, most of the participants appeared to have a positive experience which was 
demonstrated through their response to the SQUIN, their reflections after the final interview, 
as well as the fact that no participant dropped out of the research despite the time commitment. 
What likely contributed to the success of the research was the level of psychological safety 
which was established within the interview settings through the adoption of several soft 
practices - many of which were inspired by BNIM. However, I would also argue that there are 
other practices which extended beyond the interview session which contributed to a positive 
research experience.  
 
Engagements outside of the interview setting 
Considering the intimate nature of the research experience and the need to ensure 
psychological safety during the interviews, I decided early on to pay close attention to my 
interactions with the participants throughout the whole research process. Although the in-
between engagements play a critical part in the research design, these can often be 
overlooked, as research methods tend to primarily focus on the specific research techniques 
themselves. This is a shame, because these smaller moment-to-moment interactions outside 
the interview settings play a critical role in helping to build a safe and trusting interview setting. 
In this section, I will present a few examples of the communications and engagements which 
took place prior to each interview session to illustrate the level of attention paid and the tailored 
nature of the research design. While my methods in chapter 3 outline the techniques used 
during the interview sessions and my analysis of the data, here I focus on interactions which 
took place outside of the interview setting.  
 
While one could argue that communication between me and the potential participants and 
began through my research flyer, the more personalized contact began as soon as they 
submitted their responses to the recruitment survey. I made it a point to monitor online survey 
responses daily and to send all potential candidates a confirmation email when they submitted 
their survey responses. I felt that responding in a timely fashion was pertinent to maintain 
interest, momentum and would help to start to build a level of trust and reliability in the research 
process.  
 
The confirmation email itself was relatively standardized - but where possible, my email ended 
with a friendly personalized note which alternated depending on the context. In the case of 
Rose for example, an upcoming bank holiday was a good opportunity to start building a more 
personalized connection. An example of the first email I sent to Rose is provided below.  
 
 
162 
Figure 26: Example of email communication 1 
Subject line: Thank you for completing the recruitment survey!  
 
Hi Rose,  
 
I just wanted to quickly say thank you for completing the ‘Climate Change and Identity” recruitment survey. 
All your information has been received in good order.   
 
The recruitment process will continue for the next couple of weeks, with interviews beginning at 
the end of September. I will be in touch again to inform you further.  
 
Thank you for your interest in my research.   
 
Have a lovely bank holiday weekend.   
 
Kind regards,  
Maxine  
 
Rose responded to my email the next day, thanking me for my message and said that she 
looked forward to hearing from me. The promptness and friendliness of our first 
communication exchange was important in helping to lay a foundation for our relationship that 
was to follow.  
 
After this type of email communication, the participants were asked to complete another online 
digital form to schedule in the first two interviews, which were also agreed and finalized via 
another email communication. Throughout this process, participants were sent friendly 
reminders if I hadn’t heard from them within the specified time frames.  In addition to official 
Outlook interview invitations, I also emailed them short instructions for the photography 
exercise prior to the last interview to help them with their task.   
 
Overall, there were multiple engagements and touchpoints throughout the research process 
outside of the formal interview setting. In the case of Rose, for example, there were over 17 
email exchanges which had taken place between all three interviews. Her case was not 
particularly unusual. Other participants, such as Suzanne had 27 email engagements over the 
course of her research participation. For some participants, such as those with Margaret, 
several communications had taken place via WhatsApp to make the sharing of photographs 
easier.  
 
 
163 
Although I had a standard set of procedures to guide participants throughout the research 
process, every participant was different, and some required a more tailored approach. For 
example, one of the participants was going through a particularly difficult time in her life due 
to several health complications. While she was keen to participate in the research, it was 
sometimes challenging for her to stay focused. The second interview was particularly 
triggering for her, as she had just learned that she may have an illness. Although she was able 
to regain her composure and insisted on continuing the interview, after the second interview 
was completed I questioned whether it was appropriate for her to continue the research. As 
the third interview was optional, I did not want to put any pressure on her to continue if she did 
not feel up for it. Below is an example of the email instruction I sent to her as we prepared for 
our final interview session together.  
 
Figure 27: Example of email communication 2 
Subject line: Photography activity 
 
Hi Charlotte,  
 
Thank you very much for our meeting today. For the next few weeks - if you can (and please don't feel obligated at 
all) would you mind doing the following? 
 
Before our final interview, can you take up to 10 photographs of objects, peoples, situations, landscapes or anything 
at all, that resemble how you feel about climate change.  
 
You can email or Whatsapp them to me: XXXXXXxxxx 
 
Our next meeting is scheduled for XXXXXXXXXX. I will send an invitation for that with a Zoom link on the day.  
 
Please take good care of yourself.  
 
Kind regards,  
Maxine 
 
Despite the participant’s health problems, she was able and willing to participate in the final 
interview and when I asked her if she would like to share anything else at the end of the 
interview she said:  
“No, I don’t think so, thank you. I’ve really enjoyed the experience, I feel quite 
fond of you really. I think what you’re doing has the potential to really help 
people to understand something a bit differently. It’s always a privilege, I 
think, to be asked some really good questions and to reflect a bit more on 
where you’re at with things. It’s an opportunity to learn more about yourself 
but hopefully also to have my views put in the mix there….”  
While communications which occur outside of the interview setting may not appear significant 
on their own, the engagements which we have with research participants can impact how the 
research participants essentially show up during the interview session. Building trust and 
 
164 
creating psychological safety consists of small thoughtful and intentional practices which 
places the interests of the participant first. Overall, there were several practices which I 
adopted, some of which were advised by BNIM which help to create psychological safety 
within the interview setting – all of which contributed to a positive research experience for the 
participants.  
 
5.4. 
Chapter summary 
This findings chapter sought to answer my second research question: To what extent can a 
life story method be used to explore emotional responses to climate change? It provided an 
examination of the strengths and limitation of using BNIM to study emotional responses to 
climate change. This is a direct call to the need for more innovative methods in the social 
sciences regarding the study of environmental issues (Lertzman 2015). In addition, it 
contributes to the lack of practical knowledge available on the real-life application of BNIM 
(Moran et al. 2022), particularly around the use of SQUINs. 
 
The section began with a systematic literature review, highlighting key contentions around the 
empowerment rationale. Through a case study review, supported by psycho analytical case 
study analysis mainly through the work of Hollway and Jefferson (2000) several key findings 
were identified. The results indicate that BNIM can elicit rich data and is suitable for examining 
hidden emotions, although a great deal of interpretative work is required to uncover these in 
certain cases. However, using SQUINs may also pose risks for the participants and 
researcher, due to the intensity and emotional charge of the interviews. Overall, however, it 
appears that BNIM can be a positive experience for most participants, consisting of several 
practices which can enhance psychological safety within the interview setting.  
 
 
 
 
 
165 
6. 
Conclusions and discussion 
 
In bringing this thesis to a close, this final chapter will summarize key conclusions based on 
the findings presented in the previous two chapters. Considering that the research was carried 
out in-depth with a small sample of eleven participants, a caveat of these findings is that they 
cannot be generalized to a wider population. However, the strength of the qualitative research 
is the ability to provide rich and detailed accounts of experiences, some of which may be 
entirely unique the individual. Overall, qualitative research findings can provide us with a much 
deeper understanding of specific types of social phenomena.  
 
Although the findings are not meant to be generalized it is important to remember that the 
climate grief experiences presented in this thesis include the experiences of women only. In 
addition, is also worth highlighting that while I did contact several BAME oriented 
organisations, this research was not successful in recruiting any participants from the BAME 
community. Hence, the voices represented in this research are from women who identified as 
‘White - English, Welsh, Scottish, Northern Irish or British or White Other’.  This caveat should 
be taken into consideration when understanding the research findings.  
 
As a reminder, this research sought to answer the following two research questions:  
 
1. What are the climate grief experiences of UK women, as told through their life stories? 
  
2. To what extent can a life story method be used to explore emotional responses to 
climate change?   
 
The sections in this chapter mirror the two research questions, whereby the first section will 
present and discuss the key findings concerning climate grief experiences. The findings 
contribute to a broader understanding of loss within the context of climate change, and while 
the climate grief experience was dominated by three emotions, the emotion of anger is of 
significance in terms of its contributions. Several avenues to explore more deeply the 
relationship between identity and climate grief will be presented in the discussion.  
 
The second section will focus on the evaluation of the life story method. Here I will illustrate 
that although life story methods, such as BNIM can be powerful tools to study emotional 
experiences and complex social phenomena, there are certain risks to consider for both the 
participant and the researcher. Several suggestions for further research will be presented. The 
 
166 
final section discusses the additional ways in which life story methods provide further value. 
This section will close with a summary of the key findings and how they directly relate to the 
research contributions presented in chapter 1.  
 
6.1. 
The climate grief experience 
 
The aim of the first research question was to obtain a richer understanding of how women 
within the United Kingdom experienced climate grief. The data was collected through the 
application of an adaptation of BNIM, an in-depth biographical psychosocial life story method. 
Using an emotion-based analysis, the interpretation of the findings was supported by 
Worden’s model of regular grief experiences (2001) and where possible, a psychoanalytical 
lens was also applied based on Hollway and Jefferson’s work (2000). All together, these 
analytical approaches allowed for several key findings to emerge.  
 
Overall, the findings contribute to a gap in knowledge in understanding grief responses within 
the context of climate change. The need for more research into this specific emotional 
experience was suggested by Cunsolo & Ellis (2018) who in addition conceptualized the term 
‘ecological grief’ which inspired this thesis. As far as this thesis can ascertain, this is the first 
piece of research which explores the experiences of climate grief with women in the United 
Kingdom.  
 
Overall, the findings illustrate that although the climate grief experience can consist of several 
overlapping emotions, the most dominant emotions experienced were those of sadness & 
sense of loss, anger and fear. Out of all three emotions, the most novel and relevant 
contributions can be drawn from the first two emotions, around which I will centre my 
conclusions and the discussion in this section.  
 
6.1.1. Going beyond environmental losses, through the lens of social values  
Based on this research, the findings illustrate that the emotions of sadness & a sense of loss 
played a key role in the climate grief experience. Several of the losses identified appeared 
interconnected, what Cunsolo et al. (2020) refer to as ‘cascading losses’, in that the impact of 
a singular loss can lead to other losses, which may not have been foreseen. This is evident 
also in the overlap of tangible and nontangible losses, whereby for example, the loss of nature 
and wildlife also led to a loss of beauty and knowledge. 
 
 
167 
While several of the women shared similar kinds of tangible losses related to climate change, 
such as losses such as wildlife, biodiversity and the natural environment, there appeared less 
commonality regarding the nontangible losses, which suggests the highly personal nature of 
these types of losses. Nontangible losses that stood out included a loss of a ‘good’ future, one 
that is predictable and stable, as well as beauty, knowledge attributed to the natural 
environment.  
 
However, nontangible losses also included a loss of positive emotions such as hope and 
power as well as the loss of social relationships - both which are novel contributions to the 
new science of loss (Barnett, et al., 2016). With regards to loss of social relationships, several 
of the women reported a sense of alienation, owing to a disconnect which - through their grief 
experience - emerged between themselves, family members and their social environment. For 
some of the women their emotions coloured the world around them, whereby every mundane 
practice such as doing the shopping or experiencing the weather aroused difficult emotions.  
 
Altogether, the nontangible losses identified, such as those related to the loss of positive 
emotions and social relationships clearly point to climate grief as a social process. The losses 
reveal what the women valued on a personal level; yet they can also be seen as a reflection 
of the socially significant values in British society. From that perspective, values-based 
approaches remain useful in understanding losses within the context of climate grief, as 
suggest by O’Brien & Wolf (2010). Taking this observation further, while the collective 
responsibility of climate change can exacerbate feelings of powerlessness and hopelessness, 
as my findings have suggested, it is also plausible that these losses are being compounded 
by the heightened sense of individuation, which currently dominates much of Western culture. 
The path forward thus requires strategies which aim to meaningfully connect individuals to a 
sense of community.  
 
The perception of personal losses and how they intersect with wider sociocultural values point 
to the localised nature of climate change impacts. Better understanding these dynamics are 
critical as they can help societies mitigate climate change and better prepare individuals and 
societies for a changing world. Hence, these findings are also relevant on a global policy level, 
which should advocate for localised approaches to climate mitigation, as well as compensation 
through, for example, the Loss and Damage Fund. This is particularly important for finding 
ways to address non-economic losses, where there remains considerable ambiguity in how 
such losses should be measured and valued. Perhaps it is not so much a matter of measuring 
the value of losses, which are incomparable, but rather acknowledging that such losses are 
 
168 
taking place and providing communities with support to develop localised strategies to work 
through the losses, in a way that is meaningful to the local population.  
 
While the Loss and Damage Fund is currently focused on communities in the global South, 
the findings of this study clearly illustrate that the psychological impacts of climate change are 
also impacting on communities in the Global North. While feelings of sadness & a sense of 
loss are often the focal point of the grief experience, this research identified two other 
emotions, those of anger and fear, which have not received as significant attention within the 
literature. According to Pihkala (2022), empirical, in-depth studies which specifically focus on 
climate anger are rare and have been highlighted as a gap in research. The next section will 
thus briefly summarize and contextualize the importance of the findings on anger.  
 
6.1.2. The complexity of anger and the value of grief models  
Overall, the findings illustrate that the emotion of anger was shared by most of the women in 
the study, and while on the surface it appears easily explained, the emotional experience of 
anger was inherently complex. A key finding was that anger related to climate change was 
almost exclusively focused on social factors. More specifically, anger was centralized around 
the inability or lack of willingness of others to perform the behaviours that could avert the 
impacts of climate change. This finding aligns with findings from a recent study from Norway 
(Gregersen et al., 2023) who also found that anger responses were related to human inaction 
and were directed outwards towards other responsible agents, such as politicians. The 
complexity around anger was further amplified here, as in terms of climate engagement, anger 
was a predictor of self-reported activism and policy support - but not individual mitigation 
actions. The rationale provided was that in their anger, participants were potentially deferring 
blame to others. While Gregersen et al. (2023) do not provide any further detail on why this 
could be the case, from a psychoanalytical perspective, blaming others could be interpreted 
through a process of splitting. Hence, these types of studies point to the complexity of climate 
emotions, and particularly in the case of anger call for more in-depth methods of interpretation.  
 
In my own work, I found that anger directed outwards was more easily expressed, however 
anger expressed inwards was much more subtle and hard to detect. This could be explained 
through the fact that anger directed inwards, points to self-implication and culpability, which 
may be too difficult to bear. As a form of self-protection, anger is more easily expressed 
outwards towards others. These internal dynamics could also potentially explain the 
discrepancy between anger and individual mitigation actions as previously identified by 
Gregerson et al. (2023).  
 
169 
However, as anger was related to policy support, but not individual mitigation actions, it is not 
clear whether anger is a motivator or barrier to climate action. Other studies have shown for 
example, that anger does lead to climate action. A recent study by von Gal et al. (2024) 
amongst Italian university students found that anger, rather than fear appeared the strongest 
predictor of engaging in environmental action. In another study, this time using Australian data, 
Stanley et al., (2021) also found that anger about climate change was associated with both 
personal and collective climate action. Furthermore, they also found that feeling anger lowered 
feelings of depression, anxiety and stress, contributing to overall wellbeing. Hence, Stanley et 
al., (2021) highlight that expressing anger could function as a healthy adaptive coping strategy, 
which then allows individuals to better engage in climate action. These studies further illustrate 
the importance of framing anger around climate change in a positive way.  In addition, as the 
studies presented analysed data from three different countries (i.e. Norway, Italy, Australia), 
the results may also point to different sociocultural norms which may judge certain emotional 
responses to be more acceptable than others. 
 
From models designed for regular grief experiences, such as those developed by Worden 
(2001), we know that anger must be processed to ensure that it does not regress inwards, 
where it can potentially become self-destructive. Ensuring that societies provide socially 
accepted public spaces to express their emotions, such as anger, is thus an important part of 
adapting to climate change. While protests tend to be highly emotional events where the 
expression of anger is accepted, the findings from this research suggest that attending 
protests to regain a sense of hope and power was sometimes short-lived. Hence, there 
remains a need to consider other more meaningful ways of working through climate emotions.  
 
From this perspective, understanding anger alongside the other emotional responses to 
climate change as part of a wider grief experience has significant value. This is because all 
societies have rituals and practices in place which can be borrowed and modified to help 
individuals and communities to collectively work through their grief responses. Several 
collective-based practices have been developed, such as performative dance based on the 
concept of grief circles (Kaya, 2020) or Remembrance Day for Lost Species (RDLS), which is 
a coalition of artists and activists who come together each year to bear witness to species and 
habitat extinction (Brewster, 2020).  
 
As several of the women participating in the study illustrated through their contributions, that 
a loss of social relationships and a sense of alienation was a key part of the climate grief 
experience, moving towards more collective and socially accepted grieving practices would 
be highly beneficial in helping them move through their grief. In her work, Varutti (2024) 
 
170 
suggests that when ecological grief remains unacknowledged and remains in the private 
sphere, this is not only detrimental to the individual but also impacts on the rest of the society. 
She stipulates that only through the process of collective mourning, will ecological grief and 
other difficult climate emotions be legitimized which is imperative to moving through and 
beyond collective social denial. In that sense, regular grief models are not only useful in 
helping to understand climate grief responses, but they also provide inspiration for the tools
