which can be applied to help cope with and work through difficult emotional experiences. 
 
However, coming together to grieve is also important within the context of identity formation 
which is needed for positive self-esteem purposes. In their exploration of RDLS practices, 
Mihai & Thaler (2024), suggest that who, what and how communities come to mourn and 
memorialize is essential for their identity. In addition, they argue that on a community level, 
the development of new practices, such as public mourning, and ways of understanding and 
processing our emotions, are needed for the creation of new social identities and institutions, 
which will be in a better position to respond to ecological and adapt to ecological losses (Mihai 
and Thaler, 2024). Although it was beyond the scope of this thesis to examine identity 
dynamics more concretely, it is my hope that this thesis will contribute to future studies which 
will be able to empirically concretize this relationship. Multiple directions of travel are possible 
which will briefly be summarized in the next section.   
 
6.1.3. Discussion: Moving to alternative interpretations of identity in climate 
grief experiences 
This thesis grew out of a desire to better understand identity dynamics within the context of 
climate emotions. Ecological grief, the concept on which climate grief is based, provided the 
clearest avenue through which identity could be explored in relation to climate change. Current 
studies on ecological grief (Borish, et al., 2021) frame identities though social roles or 
livelihoods which are closely intertwined with the natural environment. Changes within the 
natural environment due to climate change have a direct impact on the ability of individuals to 
practice their social identities within their community, which may lead to a loss of self-esteem 
and difficult emotional responses.  
 
Within the context of the United Kingdom where this research took place, understanding how 
identities played a role in the climate grief experience remained somewhat challenging. This 
was in part because the women’s identities were not directly tied to their livelihoods, and hence 
they were not directly under threat due to climate change. Hence, it was difficult to narrow in 
on another understanding of identity beyond gender. Yet through the research several insights 
emerged which could contribute to future areas of research, which I will briefly discuss below.  
 
171 
 
First, the study supports the notion that certain types of identities might make certain 
individuals more vulnerable to climate grief experiences. Several of the women participating 
in the study were actively involved in climate action and professional roles involved in climate 
change, which meant that they were frequently exposed to negative information about climate 
change. While women have been found to be more vulnerable to climate change, as discussed 
in my literature review, there may also be specific role identities within the UK can play a role 
in climate grief experiences. The combination of several identity factors (e.g. being a woman 
and working in climate change) and how the interrelate and can be used as focal point of 
future studies on climate grief. This view is supported by Benham & Hoerst (2024) who recently 
published a global scoping review on how socio-ecological factors, such as identity relate to 
ecological grief.  
 
Yet there may also be an opportunity here to apply other identity lenses which may be useful 
in helping to interpret the relationship between identity and climate grief. By adopting a very 
broad understanding of identity, one could argue that several of the losses expressed by the 
women could be understood as subjective parts of their identity. While this is clear in the case 
of the loss of social relationships which would impact on a woman’s ability to practice a specific 
role or identity, this could also occur in different ways as well.  
 
For example, some of the women were attached to certain mental formations or desires, such 
as the ability to have a good future or a sense of certainty and stability, which could be a 
projection of their desired identity. In their work, Vignoles et al. (2012) call for a more integrative 
operational definition of identity, which for example, would include amongst several other 
aspects, a union of an individual’s personal characteristics and beliefs about themselves, 
alongside identification with material possessions. Perhaps part of that identity formation may 
also extend beyond the non-material possessions to include attachments to mental 
formations. Future work could help to better conceptualize these mental formations as they 
relate to our sense of identity.   
 
Lastly, while Vignoles et al. (2012) advocate for a more integrative understanding of identity, 
others such as Edelglass (2009) call for a more expansive understanding of identity – through 
the concept of the ecological self. Instead of understanding identity as part of the climate grief 
experience, the ecological self could be understood as an identity which may be developed 
through the experience of climate grief. Based on philosophical Buddhist concepts such as 
interdependence, Edelglass (2009) describes the ecological self as a “larger sense of identity” 
(2009, pg. 431), which is attributed to an increased awareness of climate change. He suggests 
 
172 
that through this process, the current culturally defined notions and sense of self may cede to 
exist.  
 
Overall, a more robust exploration of new identity concepts may help us to understand climate 
grief from a different angle. Such calls overlap with the work of others, including Borish et al. 
(2021) who in their study on ecological grief nodded to the relevance of the indigenous Inuit 
identity as being eco-centric, and having a more interrelated concept of self. Future avenues 
of research could explore both identity concepts in closer detail in how they relate to climate 
grief experiences. As ecological grief has become a transdisciplinary concept, there is 
considerable scope to blend different approaches and methods. Here, life story methods may 
also be of further value, which is the topic of the final section of this chapter.  
 
 
6.2. 
The value of using life story methods to study climate 
emotions and other social phenomena 
 
To answer my first research question: What are the climate grief experiences of UK women, 
as told through their life stories? I adapted BNIM, biographical research method, which led me 
to my second research question, which asked: To what extent can a life story method be used 
to explore emotional responses to climate change?  
 
By adapting an existing research method and carrying out an evaluation of its application, this 
thesis contributed to Lertzman’s (2019) call for more innovative social science research 
methods within the environmental sciences. As our societies and social worlds are becoming 
increasingly more complex, our research methods must also evolve and adapt to this reality. 
Data collection methods which are rooted in the psychosocial that lend themselves to psycho 
analytical interpretations, such as BNIM, appear promising in meeting this need. In the final 
section of this thesis, I will summarize the findings from chapter 5 and draw several key 
conclusions. Overall, while the advantages of BNIM adaptations outweigh its disadvantages, 
these must be carefully considered and navigated.  
 
6.2.1. Balancing resource intensiveness and emotional labour 
Using BNIM to study complex emotions and social phenomena has several advantages. Its 
highly adaptable format lends itself to the integration of several different methods to suit the 
needs of the research. In conventional BNIM approaches, the format of third interview is 
entirely flexible. Overall, the ability to combine different methods which are then applied across 
at least two sessions allows for a deep exploration into complex experiences.  
 
173 
 
While I had several reservations regarding the formulaic approach of the second interview 
which I modified as a result, the findings illustrated that a SQUIN proved to be a deeply 
powerful tool. While the systematic literature review showed that most studies opt for a partial 
SQUIN, I demonstrated that using a full SQUIN to study a specific emotional experience can 
also be used. However, this approach does not come without any risks which need to be 
carefully managed.  
 
First, the volume of unstructured data which a full SQUIN elicits can be resource intensive. In 
some cases, additional interpretative work was needed to find and understand how certain 
stories shared by the participant was related to their experience of climate grief. Some 
participants were less explicit and left clues which could have easily been overlooked. It 
therefor takes additional effort and finetuning on behalf of the researcher to pick up on these 
for further questioning. BNIM thus remains a resource intensive method, which requires 
additional analysis in between interview sessions.  
 
While the additional work can be taxing on the researcher, my findings also demonstrated that 
in some cases, using the SQUIN may present an ethical dilemma for both the researcher and 
the participant. While the ethical critiques around BNIM are mainly centred on the use of PINs 
(Moran, Green and Warwick, 2022), my findings suggest that the full SQUIN itself can create 
an ethical dilemma. The results showed that a full SQUIN could cause a participant to share 
traumatic experiences which they may not have expected, causing discomfort and distress.  
 
Yet such occurrences may also create an ethical dilemma for the researcher, who is expected 
to contain and hold space for a participant’s difficult emotional experiences. The resource 
intensiveness of the interpretative work, alongside the need for containment made the data 
collection emotionally taxing as I approached the end of my fieldwork. Thus, while BNIM and 
more specifically the SQUIN remains a powerful tool to study complex emotions, a balance 
alongside support mechanisms must be found to ensure that potential harms are minimized 
for both the participant and the researcher.   
 
6.2.2. Contributing to participant empowerment by establishing psychological 
safety 
While a few cases illustrated that BNIM can require more interpretative work and can cause 
an ethical dilemma for the researcher and the participant, overall using BNIM had several 
advantages. This is in part illustrated through the richness of data which the participants 
 
174 
shared, but also through their experiences of participating in the research - which were largely 
positive.  
 
Overall, BNIM was a useful method to study complex and sensitive topics such as climate 
grief experiences. The SQUIN and the rest of the interview sessions provided participants with 
the space to discuss how they felt, giving them room to reflect upon how they were feeling 
about climate change and how impactful it had been on their lives. However, it is important to 
remain critical of the promise which some scholars claim that BNIM brings.  
 
My findings illustrate that while several scholars selected BNIM in part because it would reduce 
the power imbalance between the researcher and the participant and hence could be 
empowering experience for the participant ((see for example Dalikeni, 2019; Weekes, Ward 
and Mguni, 2024)), it wasn’t always clear whether this had been achieved. While my own 
evaluation of the BNIM adaptation suggested that participating in the research was a valuable 
experience for the participants, in the end it wasn’t entirely clear exactly which parts of the 
method, or combination thereof was most impactful.  
 
While I discuss the importance of establishing psychological safety through good listening 
practices which appeared to have contributed to a positive, and potentially empowering 
experience for participants, there are potentially additional factors which were not captured in 
this research. Therefore, a more thorough evaluation of these types of methods, as well as a 
more systematic post study evaluation of BNIM would be a useful avenue for future research. 
Taking this a step further, these types of studies could be centralized on the topic of 
empowerment as a direct outcome of research participation. Evaluating our approaches are 
critical in accelerating the evolution of research methods within the environmental sciences.  
 
6.2.3. Discussion: Connecting past to future, and the use of the Visual 
Biographical Timelines 
Building on my findings, the final section of this chapter will discuss several other observations 
on the method. Altogether, these further illustrate how life story methods can be a valuable 
tool in exploring emotional experiences and social phenomena. The research illustrated that 
while not all the women made explicit their emotions around climate change during their life 
story interview, several of the women drew on their life stories to help contextualize and better 
understand their climate grief experience in the second and even the third interview. In several 
cases, meanings attached to specific memories appeared interrelated with how they perceived 
potential future losses. Sarah, for example, shared a memory of snorkelling in the Great 
Barrier Reef, and she related this to a sense of loss she had on behalf of her own children 
 
175 
who she felt would be unlikely to have that experience. As several losses related to climate 
change are future oriented or could be considered as anticipated losses (Cunsolo and Ellis, 
2018), using life story approaches can be useful. This is because our past experiences and 
our memories of them often play a role in how we make sense of and imagine our futures.  
 
Yet, I found that life stories were also beneficial in other ways. For example, they helped to 
contextualise the climate grief experience within the broader social realm. Several of the 
participants spoke about climate change, in connection with other broader societal issues. 
This included references to social justice movements, like Black Lives Matter and other 
globally significant events such as the threat of nuclear war in the seventies. While these 
events may not always appear to be directly related to climate change, they illustrate the ways 
in which people attach shared meanings to global issues and how climate change is 
understood within society.  
 
The life story approach also showed how, for some of the participants, some of these broader 
themes, such as social inequality, were also woven through their own personal experiences. 
For example, one participant’s life story included several experiences of racism towards her 
husband, which meant that they relocated quite frequently. Such examples illustrate how wider 
themes associated with climate change, such as social injustice, can become deeply 
enmeshed into people’s lives and in part, may influence how they emotionally respond to 
climate change.  
 
Life story methods were useful in their ability to reveal such cross-cutting observations, and 
while I chose to focus on the SQUIN for my method evaluation, the Visual Biographical 
Timelines do deserve some more attention in that regard. Considering the visual nature of the 
timelines, it became increasingly apparent how climate change intersected with the 
participants’ life stories. Whether through key life events, such as having children, or a global 
event which may or may not even be related to climate change, such as 9/11. In addition, they 
may also be further developed to examine the evolution of an ‘ecological self’ (Edelglass, 
2009), which is an identity concept I discussed in the previous section.  
 
Overall, the findings of this research show that the benefits of using life story methods, 
outweigh the risks, which through careful consideration can be mitigated. Considering the 
added value of life story methods which have been presented in the discussion, BNIM and 
future innovations serve as a powerful tool in studying complex climate emotions and social 
phenomena.  
  
 
176 
6.3. 
Linking key findings to research contributions 
 
I will close this thesis with a summary of the key research findings as they relate the research 
contributions outlined in chapter 1.  
 
1. With regards to the Loss and Damage domain, the study found that tangible climate 
related losses tended to be shared, however there appeared less commonality 
regarding nontangible losses, demonstrating the highly subjective nature of these 
types of losses. Nontangible losses included a loss of positive emotions such as hope 
and power as well as the loss of social relationships - both which are novel 
contributions to the science of loss (Barnet et al. 2016).  
 
2. The climate grief experience consists of several overlapping emotions, however, the 
most dominant emotions experienced are those of sadness & sense of loss, anger and 
fear. Out of all the emotions expressed, anger formed a critical emotional component 
of the climate grief experience, and while on the surface it appears easily explained, 
this emotion was inherently complex. Worden’s model (2001) in addition to 
psychoanalytical approaches (Hollway and Jefferson, 2000) showed how anger 
responses related to climate change were socially embedded.  
 
3. As this study was carried out with women only, all the research findings represent the 
voices of women. The importance of maintaining family and other social relationships, 
alongside more traditional child rearing roles are just two examples of how being a 
woman may influence the climate grief experience. However, without any comparable 
research with other genders, this study cannot ascertain whether these factors apply 
exclusively to women. 
 
4. Life story methods, such as BNIM are a powerful tool to study climate emotions. 
However, BNIM is resource intensive and can pose ethical risks for the participant the 
researcher. Hence, additional support mechanisms alongside psychological safety 
practices must be implemented to ensure a positive, perhaps even empowering 
experience for the participants.

Appendix D: Overview interview format 
 
Interview 1: SQUIN (Single Question Aimed at Inducing Narrative)  
  
Preamble  
  
“I would like you to tell me your life story and all the events and experiences which have played a role 
in who you are today. Start wherever you like. Please take the time you need. I’ll listen first, I won’t 
interrupt, I’ll just take some notes afterwards.”  
  
  
Interview 2 Semi-Structured Interview  
  
A. Summary revision of the timeline  
  
Example 1: Sophie: Part 1: Timeline review and additional questions  
  
1. You mentioned that when you were 15/16 you started becoming more aware of the 
hole in the ozone layer and polar bears facing extinction, do you remember what that was 
like for you?    
   
2. You commented that when people are between 16-18 that you find out or start to find 
out what you believe in as a person, rather than what your parents have passed down to 
you – were there any particular beliefs that you were developing that you noticed were 
different than others?    
   
3. You mentioned that this was also the time you started to realize “that the world isn’t 
the place you thought it was when you were a child” - can you tell me what that was like 
for you? How do you think that affected you?   
   
4. Around the time, after you came back from XXXXXXX, you commented that for those 
teenage years, you perhaps become more disconnected from nature in the personal 
sense – as perhaps a reason for your depression – can you tell me more about your 
relationship with / nature / what it’s like?     
   
5. You mention that when XXXXX was born, it was really important for you, to have 
plenty of time outside, why is that?    
   
6. You mention that in your work, you are connecting people with nature – so that that 
they will care about it, and then protect it – but you also mention – that it is to “to 
reconnect with what they might have had as children” - can you tell me more about what 
you mean here?  
  
  
Example 2: Eva: Part 1: Timeline review and additional questions  
  
1. You mentioned that you father is XXXXXXXXXXXXXX and that occasionally he’s 
‘brought it up’ and ‘that’s shaped a lot of where you’ve got to now’ - can explain what you 
mean here?    
   
2. You mention that you’ve been very lucky to have “a strong connection with nature 
throughout all of your upbringing” - what does that mean to you? / What does that look 
like?   
    
3. You mentioned that you were involved with XXXXXXXXXXXXXXXX for a bit, but then 
moved away from it –can you tell me more about that?   
  
  
 
212 
Interview Topic Guide  
   
A. Section 1: Progression of climate change awareness  
   
1. When did you first learn or hear about climate change?    
o 
How did you feel?   
o 
What was happening at the time?   
   
2. When did climate change first become relevant to you and your life?   
o 
How did you feel?   
o 
What was happening at the time?   
   
B. Section 2: The climate grief experience  
   
3. Thinking about your life, can you tell me about a moment or time in your life when you 
felt particularly strong emotions about climate change?    
   
o 
What was happening at the time? / Why were you experiencing this?   
o 
What types of emotions were you feeling?    
o 
How did you experience those emotions?   
o 
What happened as a result of those emotions?    
o 
Are there any other moments in your life when this occurred?     
   
   
4. (If not mentioned) – Do you remember a moment in your life where you felt a sense of 
loss or grief in relation to climate change?    
  
o 
What was happening at the time?  
o 
What caused this emotion?  
o 
How did you experience this emotion? What types of emotions did you 
experience  
o 
What happened as a result?   
  
5. Is there anything you have personally lost / or are currently losing because climate 
change?    
  
  
C. Section 3: Impacts of climate grief experience  
    
6. What influence do you think your feelings about climate change [use the emotive 
words they use] have had on your present life? (life decisions and events)   
   
7. What type of influence do you think your feelings/emotions about climate change 
have had on your relationships?    
   
8. What type of influence do you think your feelings/emotions about climate change 
have had on you personally?   
  
  
D. Section 4: Coping   
   
9. How do you cope with your emotions around climate change?    
  
  
E. Section 5: Photo Elicitation Activity [discussed and follow up by email]  
  
 
 
 
 
 
213 
Example 1: Sophie  
  
“Can you take between 5-10 pictures of objects, people, situations, landscapes or anything at all that 
resemble how you feel about climate change. Please focus on the emotions you mentioned during our 
session today, including sadness, loss and grief…”   
  
  
Example 2: Eva  
  
“Can you take between 5-10 pictures of objects, peoples, situations, landscapes or anything at all that 
resemble how you feel about climate change. In your case we spoke a bit more about fear and 
uncertainty, so please include photographs resembling those emotions…”   
  
[Closing]  
  
  
  
Interview 3: Photo Elicitation Topic Guide  
  
[Preamble]  
  
1. How did you find the exercise overall?  
  
2. With which photo would you like to begin? [based on participant’s reponse]  
  
o 
What is happening in this photo?   
o 
Why did you select / take it?   
o 
Which emotion (s) does this photograph convey?   
  
3. Is there anything else that you want to share – that’s been left unsaid?   
  
4. Is there anything you want to ask me?   
  
 [Closing]

Appendix L: Deep dive into identity theories 
 
To better understand how climate emotions intersect with identity dynamics, it is useful to 
consider the many ways in which identity can be defined and constructed. Multiple scholars 
across the disciplines have different, but overlapping perspectives on what identity is and how 
it is constructed. As Elliot (2019) so eloquently pointed out, there is not one fully embracing 
explanation of how identity is navigated. It is therefore worthwhile exploring several different 
identity theories to obtain a more holistic perspective.  
 
While it would be beyond the scope of this overview to capture all identity theories, with respect 
to the disciplines covered, I have selected several from the overlapping domains of psychology 
(Erikson 1959) sociology (Tajfel and Turner, 1986) post-modernist thinking (Giddens, 1991), 
as well as the narrative tradition (Somers, 1994; McAdams, 2001). Through the examination, 
it becomes apparent that the conceptual boundaries are often porous with each discipline 
providing an alternative or complementary perspective, focusing on specific areas and 
negating others. Despite the theoretical nuances, it appears that most identity theories 
converge on two key principles which inspired the structuring of this subsection. Firstly, that 
identities are fluid and change over time and second, that identities are relational and therefor 
influence by the environment.  
 
Identity Principle 1: Identities are fluid and change over time 
Although many identity theories exist, most can agree that identities are not fixed, but they are 
fluid and can evolve and change over time. This is accepted across several disciplines 
including developmental psychology (Erikson, 1959), symbolic interactionism and identity 
theory(Stets and Burke, 2000), social identity theory (Turner, et al., 1987) post modernism 
(Giddens, 1991; Chaffee, 2019) and narrative traditions (McAdams, 2001). Yet the theories all 
have a slightly different interpretation on the nature of identity and the temporality of change. 
Several examples will be described in the subsequent paragraphs. 
 
On one side of the spectrum, within the field of developmental psychology, identities are seen 
as more internal. They are expected to change and evolve over time, but this change has a 
boundary and tends to be most active around the period of adolescence. According to his 
theory, Erikson (1959) suggests that the process of identification begins at a young age, and 
then reaches a crisis point in adolescence. This period is often coupled with heightened stress 
and anxiety. The identity then settles and culminates in adulthood. While there is much fluidity 
 
236 
throughout the process of identity formation, when adulthood is reached, Erikson (1959) 
suggests that at that point identities become more or less stable.  
 
Erikson’s (1959) theory have also been applied in narrative traditions. For example, the 
concept of ‘narrative identity’, pioneered by McAdams (2001), perceives individuals to have a 
singular coherent identity. Narrative identities are described as an individual’s internalized 
story which is created by bringing together a reconstructed past and imagined future and 
provides a sense of unity and purpose (McAdams and McLean, 2013). In their paper, Smith 
and Sparkes (2008) interpret McAdams’ (2001) approach as psychosocial, or as ‘thick 
individual and thin social relational’ – where the internal has primacy over the social. Although 
the concept recognises the various roles and representations which individuals incorporate 
into their narrative identity, the intrinsic nature dominates. In this theory, achieving coherence 
and order in one’s narrative identity is related to an individual’s psychological wellbeing (Smith, 
B. and Sparkes, 2008), and thus the inability to create coherency in narrative identity could be 
associated with emotional distress. Similarly, according to Erikson (1959) the healthy 
functioning of identity is important to our sense of wellbeing.  
 
Despite the integration of Erikson’s (1959) theory, one key criticism concerning the narrative 
tradition is the assumption that once a story is told in the first person, the narrative can be 
used to draw conclusions about a person’s identity. Identities are confined within the individual 
and an assumption is made that ‘the stories we tell about ourselves is how we conduct our 
lives – is who we are’ (Bamberg, 2012). Nonetheless, the key point to draw out is that, the 
culmination of an internalized narrative identity in early adulthood illustrates how within this 
theory, a slightly more static nature of identity is assumed.  
 
While identity change from the perspective of developmental psychology can be seen as a 
long-term process, with most activity occurring during adolescence, identity change according 
to sociological approaches are described as more situational and short-lived (Vignoles, 
Schwartz and Luyckx, 2012). According to social identity theory, how individuals develop self-
concepts are based on social group memberships, such as nationalities, social class and 
occupations. Having a social identity generally implies group membership, which from an 
emotional perspective can provide individuals with a sense of belonging  (Turner, et al., 1987). 
Although their work was primarily focused on self-esteem, in groups and out groups and 
intergroup conflict, the theory suggests that some of the social identities which individuals can 
have, are not fixed. Individuals may have several social identities, which can be made salient 
depending on context (Turner, et al., 1987). Such practices can be used to increase an 
 
237 
individual’s influence within a particular membership group (Oakes, 1987). This theory points 
to the functionality of identity, that is more removed from the self.   
 
Unlike the social identity theory provided by Turner et al. (1987), Stryker and Burke (2000) 
provide a more static, but complex conceptualization of identity. However, this interpretation 
requires a further examination of how they have defined an identity. In this case, identities are 
described as parts of the self which are composed of the meanings which individuals attach 
to the various roles, they play in society (Stryker and Burke, 2000). From this perspective, 
identities are seen as ‘internalized role expectations’, whereby the roles are attached to the 
positions which individuals occupy within their own network of relationships in society (Stryker 
and Burke, 2000, 286). In that sense, identities are seen as self-cognitions, or schemata, 
which are not situation specific. Instead, this scheme or hierarchy of identities can be activated 
and carried into different situations (Serpe and Stryker, 2011). Again, such an interpretation 
points to an identity as having many parts and being more functional – indicating that they are 
not static as different identity ‘parts’ can be made salient.  
 
While identity theory (Turner et al., 1987), interprets identities as roles negotiated on the level 
of social networks, and to some degree the wider social structures around them, Giddens 
(1991) has a more globalized perspective on identity processes. His theory is particularly 
interesting with respect to climate emotions, considering the globalized nature of climate 
change. With respect to identity change, Giddens (1991) also assumes that identities are not 
fixed. In his interpretation, self-identities are created and sustained in reaction to incoming 
information, through a process of reflexivity. Self-identities are not perceived as distinctive 
traits, or roles adopted by the individual, such as Stryker and Burkes’ (2000) or Turner et al.’s 
(1987) interpretation – rather self-identity, is described as ‘the self as reflexively understood 
by the person in terms of his or biography (Giddens, 1991, p. 53). Others such as Elliot (2019), 
suggest that the study of identity is the study of how individuals ‘emotionally invest in their own 
self-making, self-construction and self-renewal’ (Elliot, 2019, p. 3). This illustrates how 
meaning is created and continuously evolving, rather than being static and assigned.  
 
As illustrated above, identity is often interpreted as being fluid in nature. However, there 
appear differences regarding the timing of that change. For example, for Erikson (1959) 
identity change is heightened during adolescence and early adulthood, but according to 
identity theory, an individual’s schemata of selves are on rotation depending on the social 
context in which individuals find themselves in (Stryker and Burke, 2000, p. 286). Yet it is the 
temporal nature of that fluidity that also appears to differ between theories. While identity 
 
238 
change may appear situational for some (Turner et al., 1987) for others, identities are 
considered a continuous life project or in other words, a never completed (Giddens, 1991) 
 
That identities are subject to change appears to be shared by a wider selection of identity 
theories. This overlaps with the second principle, which suggests that identities are relational 
and influenced by the environment. This will be discussed next.  
 
Identity Principle 2: Identities are relational, and influenced by the environment  
While several identities theories illustrated in the previous section can agree that identities can 
change, they also tend to assume that identities are not formed isolation but are influenced by 
the social and the global systemic structures that surround the individual.  
 
Within developmental psychology (Erikson, 1959), individuals are seen as having a singular 
coherent identity as they mature into adulthood, however these identifications or self-
representations develop throughout childhood as a result of interactions with other people, 
such as parents or peers. These self-representations, not described as multiple identities, are 
picked up in the early stages of life are seen as the building blocks of a coherent future identity 
(Schachter, 2013). Despite the interpretation of an individual having a single coherent identity, 
this process does not occur in isolation. In fact, Erikson (1959) believed that the individual and 
society were intricately woven.   
 
Within the sociology literature, identities are created, developed and negotiated in relation to 
wider social structures. Social identity theory (Turner, et al., 1987) assumes that social 
identities exist to play out specific functions within groups, while identity theory perceives 
identities as roles which are determined by the positions individuals hold across various 
groups (Stryker and Burke, 2000). Identities are related to the role, or the social positions 
which exist within a specific social structure. Yet at the same time, identities are internal, as 
they consist of an internalized expectations associated with a role. All these roles are 
embedded within a number of groups, each of which provide their own context for the 
meanings associated with the role (Stryker and Burke, 2000). As individuals take on multiple 
role relationships across various groups, this can sometimes lead to identity competition 
(Stryker and Burke, 2000). Although unclear from this theory, it may well be that these types 
of identity competitions can lead to emotional stress, when individuals need to navigate 
alternative roles across different social groups and group settings.  
 
Providing a more nuanced perspective, Vignoles et al. (2012) suggest that identities are both 
personal and social – but not just in their content but how they are constructed, maintained 
 
239 
and changed over time. The two ways of viewing identity, through the personal or social, are 
actually ‘two sides of the same coin’ (Vignoles, Schwartz and Luyckx, 2012, p. 5). This implies 
that the meaning which an individual attaches to a particular identity will to some degree be 
shaped by the meanings which have been socially constructed about that particular identity – 
at that moment in history. In that sense, individuals are simply passing through various zones 
of space and time (Burkitt, 2004). While individuals can create a personalized version of that 
identity, from a sociocultural perspective, they cannot easily escape the meanings applied to 
it by society. Similarly, Elliot (2019) suggest that identities reflect the social, cultural and 
existing power relations within society. Although he suggests that individuals are agents in 
their own identity formation, they are bound and influenced by the systems in which they are 
embedded. This is referred to what is also known as the ‘doubled’ aspect of identity (Elliot, 
2019).  
 
While social structures and roles have a more intimate relationship with identity in Stryker & 
Burke’s (2000) and Turner et al.’s (1987) theories, according to Giddens (1991), it is the wider 
global context which has a profound influence on identity formation in the modern age. 
Through the process of globalization and unlimited access to information, identities are 
constantly open to renegotiation. According to Giddens (1991), individuals are no longer 
bound by immediate social structures. Chaffee (2019) suggests that when it comes to large 
global challenges, such as climate change, individuals are forced to rethink their identities. 
Leading with this theme of identity disruption in the context of global change, Giddens (1991) 
proposes that anxiety arises when individuals feel a lack of biographical continuity, or in other 
words, when no continuous narrative can be achieved. From that perspective, it appears that 
societies in flux due to global change inevitably have an impact on the individual identities of 
its members.  
 
Overall, the last two sections have explored several different identities theories, illustrating 
how they all converge on two key principles. First, that identities are fluid and subject to 
change, and second that identities are relational and influenced by external environments. 
Most identity theories suggest that identities are not developed in isolation but are constructed 
and influenced by the wider social structures and institutions that surround them. While it 
would be beyond the scope of this literature review to include all identity theories, these 
provide a foundation for better understanding how identity dynamics could potentially play a 
role in climate grief. Where possible I have tried to illustrate where identity tensions could 
cause an emotional response. However, despite the contributions provided, it appears that the 
existing theories may be somewhat unsatisfactory with regards to climate grief. I therefor 
 
240 
examined a more fringe interpretation of identity which may be useful within the context of 
climate change. This will be presented next.  
 
The Ecological Self – An expanded sense of self  
What connects most identity theories, is that identities are fluid, dynamic and are shaped by 
our environment and the world around us. If this is true, then it is plausible that the context of 
climate change and a growing awareness of ecological destruction, could influence the 
development of a more novel conceptualization of identity, through a change in sense of self. 
Although ‘self’ and ‘identity’ are sometimes used interchangeably, it is useful to briefly 
differentiate. While identity can broadly be described how an individual presents themselves 
to the world, or their ‘social face’, for the purposes of this review, self is broadly defined as the 
sense of who and what one is. Based on the foundational work of Joanna Macy, the fringe 
concept of an “ecological self”, requires deeper exploration. This section will explore this 
concept in more detail how it could lend itself to the understanding of climate grief.  
 
The term ‘ecological self’ was first pioneered by philosopher Arne Naess and was a 
popularized within ‘deep ecology’ - a social movement turned into field- in environmental 
philosophy. Although Bragg (1996) notes that this field has been pushed to the fringe of 
academia, she highlights that the specific concept of ‘ecological self’ is worthy of deeper 
examination. Overall, thirty years later it appears that Bragg’s assumptions were correct, and 
that ‘ecological self’ currently does not seem to have a concrete academic definition. 
Consequentially, this also means that research is significantly lacking in this area. There are, 
however, a few papers contribute to laying the academic groundwork of this concept, which I 
will briefly summarize next.  
 
In her paper, Bragg (1996), presents three aspects of an ecological self, based on the deep 
ecology literature. Firstly, the ecological self is described as an expansive sense of self which 
includes all life forms, ecosystems and the Earth. Second, an ecological self, experiences 
emotional resonance will other forms of life, feels related to other forms and behaves towards 
the ecosphere as if they would to themselves. Third, it involves an expansion of a sense of 
self to the ecological (Bragg, 1996). Hence, an ecological sense of self is developed through 
realization. In the remainder of the paper, she draws out key similarities with other existing 
identity theories, particularly from social constructionist approaches. Others, such as Hoskins 
(2023) have also explored the concept in more detail pointing out overlap with notions of place 
identity. In his work on ecological displacement, Hoskins suggests that the ecological self 
contends a reciprocity between culture, nature and self, and includes knowledge connection 
to place, whereby place understood as self (Hoskins, 2023). 
 
241 
The work of Edelglass (2009) also provides several useful markers which overlap. His work is 
based on Joanna Macy’s Essay: “The Ecological Self: Postmodern Ground for Right Action,”  
(Macy, 1995) which was not accessible to me at the time of this writing. Macy’s work is largely 
influenced by Deep Ecology, as well as Buddhist philosophy - which for several scholars 
provides a suitable foundation for rethinking how to cope with ecological grief and other climate 
emotions. Broadly speaking, the idea of the ecological self is largely based on Buddhist 
concepts such as interdependence and moral consideration for nonhuman natural beings 
(Edelglass, 2009). According to Macy, the ecological self combines the mystic with the 
pragmatic and transcends separateness, leading to a profound interconnectedness with all 
life forms (Macy, 1995). In other words, Edelglass (2009) describes the ecological self as a 
“larger sense of identity” (2009, pg. 431). This expanded sense of self can largely be attributed 
to increased awareness of climate change, which could lead to stretching or collapse of the 
current, culturally defined notions and boundaries of the self. In other words, awareness and 
acknowledgement of climate change expands our current sense of self and identity.  
 
What makes the concept of the ecological self relevant to the study of ecological grief and 
other climate emotions is that these difficult emotions are perceived as a clear demonstration 
or evidence that humans are connected to the nonhuman world. Furthermore, Edelglass 
(2009) suggests the experience of emotions such as grief and fear signal a shift towards the 
development of an ecological self – and are therefore seen as a natural response. In fact, 
such responses are required to break through a ‘sense of futility and isolation’ (Edelglass, 
2009, p. 431) that may be experienced within the context of climate change. Despite the lack 
in research regarding the concept of the ecological self, the concept remains promising in that 
it is aligned with several existing identity theories described in the previous two sections. 
 
Furthermore, these interpretations may also overlap with indigenous world views and 
interrelated concepts of self, which were touched upon in Borish et al. (2021), in their latest 
study on ecological grief. Here they highlighted, how the Inuit identity is ‘ecocentric’, whereby 
the self is seen as an integral part, rather than separate from the natural world. These types 
of expansive identity constructs, have also been highlighted by other studies on ecological 
grief and identity loss within indigenous populations, such as the Shawi in the Amazon. This 
study noted how spiritualism was a key cornerstone of their worldview, whereby ecological 
change was sometimes perceived as punishment for disrupting the balance of their 
relationship with nature (Arotoma-Rojas, et al., 2022). Although the study primarily focused on 
transitioning livelihoods, from forest-based to agricultural, such perceptions of self and nature, 
can be critical to understanding the potential depth of the experience of ecological grief.   
 
242 
Overall, it appears that alternative identity concepts could contribute to the development of 
new grief models, potentially around grief experiences. Although conventional models of grief, 
such as those developed by Kubler-Ross (1979) and Worden (2001) could be useful in helping 
to explain and understand regular grief experiences, grief in the context of climate change 
may require at the same time, a much broader, but also deeper understanding of identity and 
self-concept.
